Betting or check-raising here would be spewy with almost no fold equity and would commit me with a very weak holding. Checking preserves the option to call a small turn bet to realize equity and to fold to heavy pressure. If Flash checks behind I’ll go to the river and re-evaluate; if he fires a small sizing (~≤1/3 pot) I will call to see the river, but I’ll fold to pot-sized or larger turn aggression. For now: check.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades is a card that potentially favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, but Minni's check-call on the flop (9s-6d-2c) suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a 9 or a 6. While a second barrel could potentially force a fold from her weaker holdings, checking back with Ace-high allows me to realize my showdown value or potentially hit an Ace on the river for free. Given I am the massive chip leader and Elon is extremely short-stacked, I want to avoid unnecessary high-variance spots against the only other deep stack at the table.

I have pocket threes (3♦3♥) on 9♠-6♦-2♣-Q♠-4♣ and never improved to trips — just a small pair. Button opened preflop and c-bet the flop, I called as a set-mining line; both of us checked the turn. Given the action, Flash's range contains many missed overcards and some made hands (9/6/2 pairs, Qx, overpairs). A river bet is very thin value: few worse hands will call (maybe pocket 2s or stubborn floats) while many better hands will call or raise. I also don't block key bluff-catching cards, so bluffing here is poor. Lowest-variance, +EV play is to check and go to showdown; if Flash now fires a small river bet I'll evaluate a call for pot odds, but I will fold to heavy river aggression.

I have Ace-high on a board of 9-6-2-Q-4. I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, which Minni called. We both checked the turn. Minni has now checked the river. While my Ace-high has some showdown value against missed draws like 8-7 or 5-4, it is unlikely to beat any pair she might have (like a 9 or a 6). However, betting here is unlikely to represent enough strength to fold out a pair, and checking back allows me to see the showdown for free. Given my chip lead and the presence of a very short stack (Elon), I will take the low-variance route and check.

3-handed on the button with KQs suited, a premium hand with excellent playability, position, and equity. Standard to open-raise for value and to take control of the pot. Opponents have massive stacks and defend reasonably wide based on history, but my hand is too strong to limp or fold. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a standard size that builds the pot while denying equity to weaker hands. My stack is 11.5BB, so I'm committing a reasonable portion without going all-in unnecessarily. This maximizes EV over the long term in the tournament.

I have A♣2♣ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 with the big blind behind. The button is a very short stack (850), so raising to put him all‑in or fold him is the highest EV play: A2s has decent heads‑up all‑in equity vs a short open/shove range and plays well if Flash (big blind) calls. Calling 250 keeps me out of position and invites multiway pots with a marginal hand. I will raise to 850 total to force the short stack to commit or fold and to isolate the hand; if Elon shoves I’m willing to call, if Flash makes a big 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate on range/commitment.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Elon opened from the button for 300, and Minni 3-bet from the Small Blind to 850. While I have a large stack and the price to call is 750, T9o is a weak hand to play against a 3-bet range, especially when out of position against Minni for the remainder of the hand. Elon is also very short-stacked and likely to be committed. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

Short-stacked at 8.5BB on the button with KQs suited, a premium hand with excellent equity against any reasonable 3-bet range from SB, especially after my open-raise. Minni's 3-bet to 850 forces me all-in for my remaining 550 (total 850), into a pot of 1250, offering over 2:1 odds on the call. Her range is likely wide for value and isolation against a short stack, including Ax, broadway, pockets below QQ. KQs crushes most of that range (~55-65% equity typically). History shows Minni aggressive preflop but folds to heavy action postflop; no reason to fold a hand this strong. Committing the stack maximizes EV in tournament.

Heads‑up after my 3‑bet preflop; Elon called with a fairly wide, short‑stack calling range. Flop Td‑6h‑7c is pretty dry and misses most calling hands (broadways, small pairs, suited connectors are present but many have no pair). I have A♣2♣: no made hand but I have a backdoor club possibility and good blocker value with the Ace. As the preflop aggressor I should c‑bet to take the initiative and fold out unpaired hands or weak pairs. I’ll size small — ~1/3 pot — to get the job done without committing too much of my deep stack. If he shoves short, calling is easy given pot odds and my equity to hit an Ace/2 or backdoor flush; if he calls I can assess turn and often take it down on later streets. Betting 600 maximizes fold equity while keeping future options open.

All-in for 300 into a 2400 pot offers excellent pot odds of 8:1 (needing only ~11% equity to break even). KQs has two live overcards, blocks strong hands like KK/AK/KQ, and realizes solid equity (estimated 25-40%) against Minni's SB 3-bet and c-bet range, which includes value like Ax, broadway, mid-pockets, and some bluffs. Preflop call already committed short stack effectively; folding now on a rainbow connected board where I have backdoor potential would be overly nitty in a tournament spot with massive implied odds already realized. History shows Minni aggressive but capable of weak cbets; realize equity and maximize EV.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♥4♦. In heads-up an Ace is a strong hand and, with position postflop, it's correct to take the initiative rather than limp. Flash in the BB has been variable, so I want a raise size that gets fold equity against wide hands but doesn't bloat the pot unnecessarily given my weak kicker. A 3bb open (300 total) is a standard, balanced size here: it applies pressure, builds a pot when I'm likely ahead, and keeps future decisions manageable with 18,950 behind. Plan: c-bet many flops to leverage position; if I face a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate and likely fold to heavy aggression with this kicker, but continue vs a single call.

In this heads-up situation against Minni, I am in the Big Blind holding 6d 5h. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. The pot currently stands at 400, and it costs me an additional 200 to call, giving me 2:1 pot odds. While 65-offsuit is a marginal hand, defending the Big Blind against a standard 3x open in a heads-up or short-handed context is generally appropriate given the stack depths (nearly 190 BBs effective). I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as my hand has some connectivity and the price is reasonable.

I have bottom pair (5h) on a board that contains a King and a Nine, both of which are well within Minni's opening range from the Button. Leading out here (donking) is generally not optimal as I'm likely only called by better hands or draws with high equity. I will check to see how Minni proceeds and control the pot size with my marginal made hand.

Heads-up on the button with A♥4♦. I opened preflop and Flash just called, so his calling range is wide (lots of Kx, broadways, suited connectors and club holdings). Flop K♣–9♦–5♣ is relatively dry but has two clubs; I have no pair but an ace blocker and position. Standard play is a continuation bet to apply pressure and fold out unpaired overcards, floats and some weak pairs — I want fold equity but don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (300 into a 600 pot). If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate; if he raises substantially I’ll fold most of my range here to preserve my stack.

In this heads-up pot, I am holding bottom pair (Fives) on a King-high board. Minni has placed a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring roughly 25% equity to make a profitable call. Given that Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, will likely C-bet a high percentage of her range on this texture (including many hands I currently beat or have decent equity against), folding bottom pair immediately would be too exploitable. I will call the 300 and re-evaluate on the turn, looking to see if she continues her aggression or if I improve my hand.

On the turn, the board has become more coordinated with the 8d, though it doesn't significantly change the strength of my bottom pair of Fives. In this heads-up pot, I should continue with a check-call or check-fold strategy depending on the bet size from Minni. Leading out here (donk betting) doesn't make sense as I am not representing a strong range and want to keep the pot small with my marginal holding. I will check to see how Minni acts.

Pot is 1,200. I opened pre and c-bet 300; Flash called, so his flop calling range is wide (Kx, medium pairs 9/5, club draws, some broadways and underpairs). The turn 8♦ is a relative blank for my A♥4♦ — I have only ace-high and a single diamond, so my ability to improve is limited and I’m behind most of the hands that flat-call a c-bet (especially Kx and pocket pairs). Betting the turn would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity (many Kx and draws will simply call) and would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding. I prefer pot control: check back, see the river, and use position to decide whether to fold to aggression or call a small river bet / realize equity vs bluffs. If Flash checks back we’ll showdown cheaply; if he fires a sizable bet on the river I’ll fold unless the price is very attractive or the river is a great card for my line.

On the river, the board is 5c-9d-Kc-8d-As. I have bottom pair (Fives) with a Six kicker. Minni checked the turn, which often suggests she doesn't have a King or a strong draw. The Ace on the river is a major scare card. I don't have enough value to bet for a call from worse hands, and my hand has some showdown value against complete bluffs or lower missed draws. I will check to see if Minni checks back or to evaluate if she bets. Leading out here doesn't achieve much as I won't get better hands to fold often enough to justify the risk, and I won't get called by worse.

Check. Board A♠–K♣–9♦–8♦–5♣, pot 1,200. I opened and c‑bet the flop; villain just called and both of us checked the turn. That line is consistent with Kx, medium pairs (9s/5s), underpairs, missed club draws and the occasional Ace. The river Ace gives me top pair but a very weak kicker — it also completes some hands (Ax) that now beat me. Flash checked the river, showing weakness or pot control rather than a value shove. Betting here would be thin value: it will get called by worse Kx/9x hands but also gets called or raised by better Aces, and would unnecessarily bloat the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. I prefer pot control and a cheap showdown — check and see the showdown. If Flash leads into me for a small probe I can call some sizes, but fold to heavy river aggression from lines that represent a better Ace or two pair.

In a heads-up situation, position is highly advantageous. While 32s is one of the weakest starting hands, it has some potential for straights and flushes. At this stack depth (approx. 200bb), I will complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply rather than folding or raising with such a low-equity hand.

Flash (SB) limped to 50, pot is 200 and I (BB) can act last preflop. Heads-up with deep stacks I want to seize initiative and isolate a wide limp range. J♣3♣ is marginal but has good playability (a Jack blocker, suited, backdoor straight/flush potential) and performs well multi-street. I’ll make a standard isolation raise to put pressure on his limp-call range while risking a small portion of my stack. If called I’ll continuation-bet on many flops to leverage fold equity; if I miss and face significant resistance I’ll give up unless I pick up equity (pair/flush/straight draws).

In this heads-up scenario, I am on the button and will have position for the remainder of the hand. While 3-2 suited is one of the weakest hands in a vacuum, the deep stack sizes (nearly 200bb effective) and the 2:1 pot odds on the call make it playable. Having position allows me to potentially outplay Minni on later streets if the board texture is favorable or if I flop significant equity. I will call the raise and evaluate the flop.

I raised preflop to isolate Flash’s limp-call range, so I should continue to pressure here. The flop A♣ K♦ 9♥ is high and often hits his calling range (many Ax/Kx and broadways), so I’m not expecting to get value when behind — but a small c-bet will fold out lots of air and marginal hands while keeping the pot manageable if called. J♣3♣ has a useful Jack and club blocker plus a backdoor club chance, so I’ll bet ~1/3 pot to leverage fold equity. Plan: bet 200 to target weaker holdings; fold to a large raise, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn (only continue aggressively with improvement or very favorable turn cards).

I have 3-high on an Ace-King-Nine board with no draws other than a very weak backdoor flush draw. This board texture heavily favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, and even though I have position, calling a bet with no equity or viable way to improve is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Q♠6♦ — this is a hand I can open-raise in position. Pot is 150 and the to-call is 50 (complete to 100), but raising gains initiative and fold equity and lets me play postflop with position. I’ll make a standard min/open raise to 200 (total) — small, respectable sizing that keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (Q6o is easily dominated), call/cash in position if Flash just calls and c-bet most flops (value bet if I hit a Q, apply pressure on dry boards, control size on heavily coordinated/overcard boards).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has opened with a minimum raise to 200. Given Minni's frequent pre-flop aggression in previous hands, I should represent strength and take control of the pot. A 3-bet here allows me to define Minni's range and potentially win the pot immediately, or build value against weaker Ace-high hands and Broadway cards. I will raise to 600.

Flash has 3-bet me to 600 after my 200 open. With Q♠6♦ in position this is marginal but playable heads-up — I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize position rather than 4-bet a hand that is often dominated. The pot is 800 and I must call 400, giving me ~33% pot odds; against a wide 3-bet range I have sufficient equity and strong implied odds with ~19.7k stacks. I’ll call to see a flop (total 600 committed). Postflop plan: if I hit a queen or get a favorable dry board I’ll apply pressure/value bet; on coordinated or overcard-heavy boards I’ll be prepared to check-fold to heavy aggression. I won’t 4-bet as a bluff because Q6o performs poorly as a squeeze candidate versus a 3-bet.

I have top pair with a ten kicker on an Ace-Jack-Seven board. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage here, and my hand is likely the best at this moment. I will lead with a continuation bet to get value from weaker hands like a Jack, smaller Aces, or spade draws, and to maintain the initiative in the hand. A bet of 600 (half pot) is standard and effective for this situation.

Call 600. The bet is small relative to the pot (pot 1800, to call 600 — ~25% break-even), and I have about 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 sixes) → roughly 24% to hit by the river, plus I retain positional advantage and good implied odds with deep stacks. Q6o is easily dominated by an Ace/J-heavy 3‑bet range, so a raise would be a thin, high-variance bluff that’s likely to be crushed when called or reraised. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and lets me evaluate the turn action (if I pair Q/6 I can extract value; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I can fold).

I have improved to top two pair (Aces and Tens) on the turn. While the Ten of Spades is a bit of a 'scare card' because it completes a potential flush and some straight draws (like KQ), my hand is still exceptionally strong in a heads-up pot. I need to continue betting for value to get paid by weaker Aces (AK, AQ, A9), Jacks with draws (QJ, KJ), or even high spade draws that haven't hit yet. A bet of 1200 (half the pot) keeps the pressure on and builds the pot while allowing me to evaluate if Minni shows significant resistance.

Fold. Heads-up with Q♠6♦ on A♠ J♦ 7♠ T♠ facing a 1,200 bet into a 3,600 pot: I need ~25% equity to call. Raw outs look tempting (roughly 9 spades + 3 non‑spade queens = 12/46 ≈ 26%), but many of those outs aren’t clean — queens still lose to Ax and some spade rivers will give villain a higher flush. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop raise, turn barrel) strongly favors value hands (A‑x, sets, made spades) that crush or heavily dominate my holdings. Raising as a bluff is high variance here — I don’t block enough of the strongest spade/value combos and a raise will often be called or shoved over. Given the pot odds, the polarized strength of Villain’s line, and deep stacks, folding preserves equity for better spots; I’ll let this one go.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 6s, a very weak hand. Given the stack depths and Minni's active play in previous hands, there is no reason to play this hand out of position post-flop. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♣9♦ (J9o). This is a hand I want to be aggressive with — position and fold equity matter a lot heads-up. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but limping/calling surrenders initiative. I’ll raise to take control of the hand, apply pressure to the BB, and play postflop in position. I choose a 3xBB sizing (raise to 300 total) to avoid being routinely exploited by min-raises while keeping the pot manageable given deep stacks. If Flash calls I’ll c-bet favourable flops and control the pot on dry runouts; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold J9o out of position or evaluate a call only against a small sizing with good implied odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d-5h, a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. While I only need to call 200 more into a 400 pot, 3-5 offsuit has extremely poor playability and equity. Since we are effectively heads-up in this hand and I am out of position, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the Button/Small Blind with 2d 4s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the cost to call is only 50 chips to see a flop, this hand has very little equity and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position if Minni raises or even just checks the Big Blind. Given the current tournament stage and the strength of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack for higher-equity opportunities.

Call to 100 total. 9♣4♠ is a weak, uncoordinated hand, but I’ve already posted the 50 SB and the pot is 150, so calling 50 more gives >3:1 pot odds to see a flop. I also keep positional advantage postflop (button acts last after the flop), which is valuable for playing marginal hands. Raising here would be standard in some heads-up strategies, but with this particular holding and Flash’s history of aggression/3-bets, I don’t want to inflate the pot or face a big 3-bet with junk. Plan: see the flop cheaply, continue if I pair or pick up a good draw, otherwise use position to check-fold or make a small C-bet only when the board and opponent’s action justify it.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) in the Big Blind. Minni limped from the Small Blind, which usually indicates a wide or weak range in heads-up play. ATo is a premium hand in this context and is well ahead of a typical limping range. I will raise to 400 to build the pot, extract value, and take control of the hand going into the flop.

I’m on the button (SB) with 9♣4♠. Flash raised to 400, so I must call 300 more (pot is 500). Calling would require ~37.5% equity (300/(500+300)) to break even. 9‑4o is a very weak, often‑dominated hand versus a raising range, and Flash has shown aggression that can make postflop life difficult. I had planned to call only a small completion to 100, but this 4x raise inflates the pot too much for this junk. Don’t want to bloat the pot or face bigger aggression with garbage — preserve stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

In a heads-up situation, King-Jack offsuit is a very strong hand that warrants a raise from the button. I am in position and have a deep stack, so I will put in a standard 3x open-raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up against the Button who opened to 300 from the SB. I have 7♣3♣ — weak but suited and playable in heads-up. The raise size is standard and, with deep stacks, I should defend a wide range from the big blind. Calling the extra 200 (total 300 committed) gives decent pot odds and preserves postflop options and implied odds if I hit a flush/straight or pair. I don't want to 3-bet light with this hand; plan is to see the flop and fold to large c-bets when completely missed, but continue with any pair, two pair, or strong draw.

I defended the BB preflop with 7♣3♣ to see a flop; the T♥ J♠ 8♦ flop misses me. I’ve got only a one-card straight out to any 9 (4 outs) and a very remote backdoor club flush — raw equity is low. With deep stacks and only 600 in the pot, I want pot control and more information rather than firing a bluff here. Plan: check, fold to a sizeable c-bet, and only continue if I get a very cheap price or the turn improves me (9 or a club/pair). Checking keeps the pot small and preserves stack size vs a wide but often-betting opener.

On this flop, I have top pair with a King kicker. The board is quite wet, with many straight draws (any 9 or Q is a draw). Minni checked to me. I need to bet here for both value and protection. A bet around half the pot is standard to get calls from worse Jacks, Tens, or straight draws while charging them to see the turn. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot.

I defended the BB with 7♣3♣ but the flop T♥ J♠ 8♦ completely misses me. Flash opened wide preflop and now c‑bets 300 into the ~600 pot (pot = 900). Calling 300 to win 1200 requires ≈25% equity. My realistic outs are tiny: 3 sevens + 3 threes + 4 nines (raw chance to improve by river is misleadingly high-ish when combined, but most of those improvements are low pairs that will often still be behind the button’s c‑betting range). There’s no flush draw (flop has no clubs) and I have no meaningful blockers. Raising is a thin bluff with poor fold equity and would commit a lot with essentially no showdown value. Given the poor realized equity versus a standard heads‑up c‑bet and the desire to preserve stack depth for better spots, I fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with J♦5♣, pot 150 and it costs 50 to complete. With only one opponent and deep stacks, the button should be opening a very wide range — J5o is marginal but fine to raise for initiative. I’ll make a standard min-raise to 200 total to apply pressure, keep the pot controllable, and take the lead postflop. If Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable boards and play cautiously on strong resistance; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 3s, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While the price to call is only 100 more, 63o has poor playability out of position. I should not defend with such a low-equity hand and prefer to wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the button with 96o. While 96o is a weak hand, this is a heads-up situation where I have the positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Minni has been playing relatively aggressively in recent hands. Completing the small blind for an extra 50 chips is a low-cost way to see a flop, but folding is also a standard play with this hand. Given the stack depths and the minor investment, I will fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my position, as 96o plays poorly post-flop against a standard big blind range.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with K♥5♦. In heads-up I should open a wide range and take the initiative — being in position postflop is a big advantage. K5o is marginal but perfectly fine to raise here to build the pot and gain fold equity against the big blind. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) — big enough to discourage too many speculative calls but small relative to our deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing); if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to steer the hand.

I am in the big blind with Q2o. Minni has raised to 3x from the small blind. While it's heads-up and I have a significant stack, Q2o is a very weak hand to defend out of position. Minni has been quite active, but I'd rather wait for a better hand to engage or a better spot to bluff. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids a potentially difficult post-flop situation with a dominated queen or a low-equity hand.

I am on the button with 2d3d, which is a speculative suited connector (gapped). In a heads-up situation against Minni, I have a massive stack advantage (21,200 vs 18,650) and position throughout the hand. While 23s is weak, it plays decently well post-flop, and I only need to call 50 more to see a flop. However, I want to maintain some pressure and capitalize on my position. Folding is unnecessary given the odds, but calling is fine to keep the pot small with a marginal hand. Given the stack depths and the fact that it's a 150-hand tournament, I'll take a low-frequency call here to see if I can outplay my opponent post-flop.

Heads-up in the big blind with Js9s and Flash just limped. This is a playable, suited, connected hand against a likely wide/weak limp range. I can check and see a cheap flop, but a modest raise isolates the limper, gains fold equity, and builds a pot when I have good postflop playability. I will raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) — big enough to discourage dead money but small relative to our stacks so I’m not overcommitting out of position. Plan: take the initiative preflop; if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I C-bet favorable flops and play carefully OOP; fold to a large 3-bet, consider calling a small one based on odds.

I am on the button with 2d3d, a very weak suited hand. Minni has raised to 300 pre-flop after I completed the small blind. While I have position, 32s is one of the weakest hands to continue with against a raise. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call for a 600 total pot), but the playability of 32s is poor. However, in heads-up play, I have a massive stack advantage and position. I previously considered calling to see a flop, but against a 3x raise from the big blind, it's often more prudent to fold these bottom-tier speculative hands to wait for better spots, especially since I've been active. But given the specific stack depth and current context of the tournament, I will call to see a flop and rely on post-flop position.

Heads-up, I was the preflop raiser and I act first on this flop (out of position). Board 6c-8d-5s is relatively dry and misses many of a limp/call range, so a c-bet here is the right play to take the initiative. My Js9s has two overcards, a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs) and backdoor spade possibilities — roughly ~10 meaningful outs to improve by river (~38% equity). I’ll make a medium/large c-bet to fold out worse unpaired hands and small pairs while getting value from worse draws and charging his floats. Bet ~2/3 pot (400). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and barrel only when I improve or the turn is favorable; fold to a large check-raise or clear strong action.

I am on the button with 2d3d on a flop of 6c-8d-5s. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4), the pot odds (400 to win a total pot of 1400, or 28.5%) do not justify a call when I only have an 8.5% chance to hit my gutshot on the turn. Furthermore, 2-3 high has zero showdown value, and even if I hit the 4, any 7 in my opponent's range would make a higher straight. Given my current chip lead and the early stage of the tournament, there is no reason to pursue such a marginal and vulnerable draw against a 2/3 pot continuation bet.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB). 6♥8♣ is a playable, positionally advantaged hand with straight potential — good to take the initiative. Limping (calling 50) surrenders initiative and gives the BB cheap odds to see a flop; folding is too tight here. I want to open-raise and try to steal the blind or play the flop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to get reasonable fold equity and discourage easy flats. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or continue versus a single call and c-bet favorable flops to realize equity in position.

I am in the Big Blind with K d 8 s. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard heads-up opening range, and King-high with a decent kicker has sufficient equity to defend in position-neutral situations, especially since I only need to call 200 to see a pot of 400. While K8o isn't a premium hand, folding here would be too tight against a frequent raiser like Minni. I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of Qh-7d-9d, I have King-high and a backdoor diamond flush draw with the Kd. This board texture hits a lot of Minni's opening range, as she will have many Queens, straight draws (86, JT, T8), and diamond draws. However, she has checked to me or I am first to act on this street depending on the platform's order, but as Big Blind checking to the raiser is standard. Since there is no bet yet, I will check and see how she proceeds. Folding is not an option when I can check for free, and betting here as a bluff into a board that connects well with her range is risky. I will check to see her reaction.

I raised to 300 preflop and Flash just called, so the pot is 600. Flop Qh 7d 9d gives my 6h8c an open-ended straight draw (needs a 5 or a 10) — 8 outs, roughly 31% to hit by the river. Flash checked, showing weakness or pot control. In heads-up position this is a classic semi-bluff spot: I want to take the initiative, get fold equity, charge worse draws, and build the pot when I have decent equity. I’ll size about half-pot (300). That sizing keeps calls reasonably priced (calling 300 into this pot requires ~25% equity), is +EV given my draw, and lets me fold to heavy aggression. Plan: if called, evaluate the turn — bet for value if I hit, continue selectively as a bluff if the turn texture and opponent weakness justify it; fold to a strong check-raise.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has made a standard 1/2 pot continuation bet of 300 into 600 on a board of Qh-7d-9d. I am holding Kd-8s, giving me King-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw (using my Kd) and several backdoor straight possibilities (any 6 or 10 gives me an open-ended straight draw). The pot odds are 3:1, meaning I need roughly 25% equity to justify a call. Given the width of a button opening range and the frequency of C-bets, King-high has sufficient equity and potential to improve to see a turn card. I will call the 300 and re-evaluate on the next street.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high (Kd 8s) on a turn board of Qh-7d-9d-4s. After calling a 1/2 pot bet on the flop, the turn 4s is a relatively blank card that does not improve my hand. I have no made hand and very limited draw potential (only a King or an 8 would likely give me the best hand, and even then, I'd be vulnerable to any Queen or better). I'm out of position against Minni, who has been aggressive. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet, as my hand lacks the equity to continue further.

I have 6♥8♣ on Q♥7♦9♦4♠ — I’m on an open‑ended straight draw (5 or 10 = 8 outs, ~17.4% to hit on the river). Heads‑up the BB checked the turn after calling my flop c‑bet, which shows a wide range with many hands that will fold to turn aggression (air, weak pairs, some underpairs and missed overcards). The pot is 1200. A half‑pot double‑barrel (600) gives me meaningful fold equity while keeping the price reasonable if I get called. EV check: I give up immediate fold equity; EV bet: with e≈0.174 and b=600 I only need the opponent to fold ≈28% of the time for this to be +EV — a realistic number heads‑up against a passive check. Plan: bet 600 as a semi‑bluff. If called I still have 17% to make my straight and will proceed cautiously on the river (value bet if I hit, check/avoid big bluff‑catching bets if I miss). If I face a significant check‑raise on the turn I’ll fold — I’m on a one‑card draw and can’t comfortably continue.

On this turn board of Qh-7d-9d-4s, I am holding only King-high (Kd 8s). Minni has fired a second barrel of 600 into a 1200 pot, showing consistent aggression. While I called the flop with King-high and a backdoor diamond draw, the turn 4s is a complete blank that does not improve my hand. I am out of position and have very few outs that would give me a confident winning hand (only a King, and even then I could be out-kicked or beaten by a set/two-pair). Given the coordinated nature of the board, it is highly likely Minni has at least a pair of Queens, a nine, or a strong draw that she is semi-bluffing. I do not have the equity required to call another bet. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Holding Ace-three offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation is a standard raising hand. It has good raw equity and high-card value against a wide range of hands the Big Blind will defend with. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that is likely ahead of my opponent's random Big Blind distribution.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♣ J♦ facing a 300 open from the button. AJo is a strong, value-heavy hand in this spot and Flash has shown a tendency to open-raise frequently. Although I’ll be out of position postflop, I want to take the initiative preflop, deny equity to weaker broadways and suited connectors, and extract value from worse A-x and Kx hands. A 3x sizing (~900 total) is a standard, constructive 3-bet here — big enough to narrow his calling range but not so large that a small 4-bet commits me. Plan: if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on coordinated boards and use pot control OOP; if he 4-bets very large I’ll fold and preserve chips. Commit total = 900.

In this heads-up situation, I opened for 300 with Ace-three offsuit from the Button/Small Blind. Minni has 3-bet to 900. While A3o is a relatively weak hand, I have position for the remainder of the hand and the pot odds are 2:1 (600 to call into a total pot that will be 1800). Based on previous hands, Minni has shown significant aggression and a wide 3-betting range. Calling allows me to evaluate the flop with a potential top-pair hand or use my positional advantage to take the pot away if she shows weakness on later streets.

Heads-up 3-bet pot, pot = 1,800. I have A♣J♦ on K♥ Q♥ 3♦ — ace-high with decent blockers to Ax combos but currently behind any K or Q. As the preflop 3-bettor I can credibly represent K/Q and should take the initiative to fold out weaker unpaired hands and extract value from worse A‑x and some Qx. I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP, so I’ll continuation-bet about half the pot (900). Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll exercise pot control on the turn and re-evaluate (check back blanks, raise or value-bet if I improve); if he check‑raises large I’ll concede this street and fold.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board of King-Queen-Trey. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has now led out for half the pot. This board texture (K-Q-x) is much better for a 3-betting range than it is for my calling range. Even though I have a pair, I am likely behind to any King, Queen, or pocket pair higher than threes. While Minni is aggressive, calling here would likely force me to face another large bet on the turn with a very weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up with me on the button (SB) and deep stacks (205 BB). 4♣5♥ is weak but has decent post‑flop playability heads‑up and I have positional advantage after the flop, so I should be aggressive rather than limp/call. I want to take the initiative, steal the blind, and play the hand in position if called. Raise to a standard ~3x BB (300 total) — big enough to apply pressure and price out some marginal hands, but small relative to my stack. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold most of the time; I’ll defend or call only versus small 3-bets or when I can realize implied odds. Postflop plan: c‑bet favorable dry boards, play cautiously on wet/face cards and control pot size.

I am in the Big Blind holding J9o. Minni has raised to 3x from the Button. J9o is a standard defending hand in heads-up play, as it has reasonable equity against a wide button opening range. Given the pot odds (2:1), calling to see a flop is the most efficient play here. I want to avoid 3-betting and bloating the pot out of position with a marginal holding like J9o, but it is certainly too strong to fold.

I have Jack-high on a very dry board (2-4-8 rainbow). As the Big Blind, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here wouldn't accomplish much with such a weak hand, so I will check and see how Minni reacts. If she bets, I will likely have to fold given I have no made hand and no real draws.

Heads‑up on a dry low flop (2♣ 4♠ 8♥) and I’ve hit a pair of fours with 4♣5♥ in position. I was the preflop aggressor, so I should continue taking the initiative — this board is good for a c‑bet: it folds out many overcards and extracts value from worse hands (ace/king highs, small pocket pairs, etc.). I’ll make a half‑pot bet to build the pot while keeping it controllable with deep stacks. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll give strong weight to folding; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

I have Jack-high on a dry board of 2-4-8 rainbow. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a half-pot continuation bet. While I have two overcards, I have no equity in terms of draws (straight or flush). Continuing out of position with a marginal Jack-high is not profitable long-term against a standard C-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation with a speculative hand like 8h4h, I want to see a flop cheaply given the deep stacks and Minni's recent aggression. Folding is too tight for a suited hand on the button, and raising might invite a 3-bet that I'd have to fold to. Limping allows me to realize my equity and play the hand post-flop.

Flash (button/SB) just limped (called 50), showing a very wide, weak range. Heads-up K♥7♦ is playable and performs well as part of a raising range vs a limp. I want to seize the initiative and either take the pot now or isolate with a hand that can hold up heads-up. I’ll make a standard, modest isolation raise to 300 (3x the BB) — big enough to get value/fold equity and price out pure spews, but small enough to keep weaker hands calling so I can realize equity. If Flash 3-bets I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP on the flop.

I have 8h 4h in position. I limped and Minni raised to 300. Calling 200 more into a pot that will be 600 gives me 3:1 odds. Given the suitedness and the fact that I'm in position, this is a standard call to see a flop. I need to be careful if I don't hit, as Minni has been aggressive, but I have enough playability to continue here.

I raised preflop and called heads‑up; on this flop (K♠ J♠ 6♠) I have top pair (K♥7♦) but no spade — the board is very mono and the caller’s limp/call line is wide so his range contains many spade combos (including some made flushes) but also lots of one‑pair and weaker broadway hands I beat. I want to keep the initiative, charge hands I beat (other Kx, Jx, Qx, Ax, small pairs) and deny free turns that could help single‑spade hands (even though two spades are required to complete a flush). A ~2/3‑pot c‑bet accomplishes that balance: extracts value from worse, folds pure air, and keeps the pot controllable if I face strong action. I’ll bet 400; if he flats I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn, and if he check‑raises big I’ll give credit to a made flush/set and likely fold.

I have 8h 4h on a monotone board of Ks Js 6s. I have no spades, no pairs, and no significant draws. Minni has been aggressive throughout the session and her 400 bet into a 600 pot on this board is a strong continuation. There is no reason to float or try to bluff on a board that is so likely to have connected with her pre-flop raising range or given her a flush draw. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 8♣9♥ — a playable, connected hand that belongs in a wide open-raise range. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to just call. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) to leverage position and fold equity: this sizing is consistent with my table image (I’ve opened to 300 frequently) and either wins the pot preflop or lets me see a flop in position with a decent speculative hand. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll concede with this marginal holding; if he calls I’ll use position to steer postflop and c-bet or check-call depending on the board texture and his tendencies.

I have King-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has been raising frequently from the button. Instead of just calling and playing a guessing game on the flop as I have in previous hands, I will put in a three-bet to take control of the pot and build value. This tests the strength of Minni's opening range and allows me to take the initiative.

Heads-up, deep stacks and I have 8♣9♥ in position — a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability. Flash’s 3-bet to 900 is a standard polar/value+bluff sizing but not huge; folding is too nitty and 4‑betting with 98o is too thin and will be dominated by his value range. Calling 600 more (900 total) gives ~33% break-even equity and preserves position and implied odds to realize equity on flops. Plan: call to see the flop; c-bet or lead when the flop hits my range/draws, check and realize equity or fold to heavy action on dry A/K-high boards. If Flash 4-bets large I’ll concede this marginal holding.

I 3-bet pre-flop with King-Queen offsuit to take control of the hand. The flop comes 5-4-7, which is a low and somewhat connected board that doesn't directly hit my range, but I still hold two overcards and the initiative. I have been playing somewhat passively in recent hands, folding frequently to Minni's post-flop aggression. To maintain the story of a strong overpair (like AA, KK, or QQ) and to take advantage of the fold equity created by my pre-flop action, I will place a continuation bet of roughly 40% of the pot. This allows me to win the pot immediately if Minni missed or has a weak holding, while keeping the pot manageable if she continues.

I call. Board 5♠-4♠-7♦ with 8♣9♥ in position: Flash’s bet of 700 into a 2500 pot is very small (~22% pot), so the break-even equity to call is low. I have four clean outs (any 6) to make a straight (≈8.5% on the turn, ≈15.8% by the river) plus additional equity from pairing an 8 or 9 and good implied odds with deep stacks. Given position and the cheap price to see the turn, calling preserves fold equity on later streets and lets me realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position. Plan: call 700 more (total committed = 900 pre + 700 = 1600), see the turn — raise/value-bet if I hit the straight or get a favorable read, fold to heavy aggression if I miss and Flash applies significant pressure.

The turn 8d is a very dangerous card for my range. It completes several possible straights (any 6 now has a straight) and heavily connects with the types of hands Minni would call a 3-bet and a flop continuation bet with, such as middle pairs or connectors like 67, 78, or 56. Having only King-high with no draw, continuing to bet here is risky as I have very little equity if called. I will check to see how Minni reacts. If she checks back, I can see a free river; if she bets, I will likely have to fold given the coordination of the board.

I have 8♣9♥ and the turn (8♦) pairs my 8 — I’ve got a decent, live one‑pair hand in position. Flash was the preflop 3‑bettor and c‑bet the flop, but he checked the turn which signals a lot of missed overcards and weak hands in his range. With position and the initiative to act, I want to extract thin value from worse broadways/one‑pair hands and charge any straight (6) or other backdoor draws, while keeping the pot manageable. A half‑pot sizing (~1,600 into 3,200) accomplishes that: it folds out pure air, gets called by worse, and protects my hand. If I face a check‑raise on the turn I’ll assume a very strong holding (6/9/two‑pair/set) and fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river based on the card and his action.

The turn card 8d is extremely unfavorable for my range. It completes straights for hands containing a 6 (67, 68, 69, 56) and improves many other hands that Minni would likely call a 3-bet and flop continuation bet with, such as 78 or middle pairs. After I checked to show weakness, Minni's bet of 1600 into 3200 puts me in a difficult spot with only King-high and no real draws. I lack the equity to call, and bluff-raising into such a coordinated board is too risky given how well this texture hits her likely calling range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 42 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am on the button and it only costs 50 more to call, playing this hand out of position post-flop against an aggressive opponent like Minni is likely to lead to a loss in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 7♣9♣ — a playable, suited connector with good post-flop playability and blockers. Limping hands in this spot gives the BB good odds and initiative; I want to take the lead and apply pressure. A 3xBB open to 300 (total) is a standard, balanced sizing here: big enough to fold out worse hands and avoid giving too-good odds to float, but small enough to keep the pot manageable with a marginal hand. If called the pot will be ~600, and I’ll play post-flop with positional advantage; I’ll respect large 3-bets and fold to heavy aggression. Raise to 300 to isolate the BB and seize initiative.

I am holding King-Six of spades in the Big Blind. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average defending range. Minni has opened for a standard 3x raise to 300. While I could 3-bet to take control of the hand, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and utilize my hand's post-flop potential without bloating the pot out of position against a stack that currently covers me.

On a flop of Jack-King-Ten, I have flopped top pair (Kings) with a 6 kicker. This board is extremely coordinated and hits many parts of a button raising range, including straight draws (AQ, Q9, 98) and two-pair combinations (KJ, KT, JT). However, I have top pair in a heads-up pot. I am first to act. Checking to the aggressor is standard here to see how they react to this heavy board. If Minni bets, I will likely call to evaluate the turn, as my hand is too strong to fold but vulnerable to many cards.

Flop: Jc Kc Ts. My hand 9c7c has the nut club draw plus two gutshot straight outs (8 and Q). That’s 15 unique outs (~54% to hit a straight or flush by river; ~32% to hit the turn). Flash checked — I have both strong equity and fold equity heads-up as the preflop raiser. I want to semi-bluff: build the pot when I’m often ahead of his check/call range and also fold out weaker pairs or unpaired overcards. Bet ~2/3 pot (400) balances pressure and pot control — if called I’ll continue with turns that improve me and be cautious to heavy aggression (fold to big check-raises without improving).

I have flopped top pair with Kings on a very wet and coordinated board (Jc-Kc-Ts). While my kicker is weak (6s), top pair is significant in heads-up play. The board offers many straight possibilities (AQ, Q9, 98) and two-pair combinations (KJ, KT, JT) that are well within Minni's button raising range. Minni has continued for 400 into a 600 pot. Folding top pair immediately here would be too weak, but raising would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, see a turn card, and re-evaluate based on the action and board texture changes.

On a highly coordinated board of Jc-Kc-Ts-9s, my top pair of Kings with a 6 kicker is extremely vulnerable. The Nine of Spades completes numerous straight possibilities for any Queen or Eight in Minni's range, both of which are common in a button-opening range. While I have picked up a flush draw to go with my top pair, the board texture is too dangerous to lead into. I will check to Minni to see how she proceeds. If she bets, I will have to evaluate based on her sizing, considering I have top pair and a flush draw as insurance, but for now, checking is the best way to control the pot size out of position.

I hold 9♣7♣ on J♣ K♣ T♠ 9♠ — I’ve turned a pair of 9s plus a huge club flush draw and still have the 8/Q straight outs. That’s both real showdown value and a lot of equity vs. Flash’s check-call range (Kx, Jx, Tx, club draws, some overcards). Flash’s passive check on the turn after calling the flop suggests many medium-strength hands and draws; betting here extracts value from worse pairs and charges his draws while keeping pressure. I’ll size to get value and deny correct odds to pure overcards: bet ~2/3–3/4 pot. Bet 1,000 chips. If called I’ll re-evaluate the river — I’ll press with completed draws or good improvement, but be prepared to fold to large river raises if I miss.

On a board of Jc-Kc-Ts-9s, I have top pair with a King and have picked up a King-high flush draw. This board is extremely coordinated, and any Queen or Eight now makes a straight, which are very much in Minni's button-opening range. However, with top pair and a strong flush draw (9 outs), I have significant equity to continue. Minni's bet of 1000 into a 1400 pot gives me roughly 2.4:1 odds, and considering the potential to improve to a flush or have the best hand against a semi-bluff, calling is the most profitable play to see the river.

I have top pair with Kings, but the board is extremely coordinated with multiple straight possibilities (any Queen or Eight makes a straight). The 4d on the river is a complete brick. Given the texture, my hand functions as a bluff catcher at best. I will check to see if Minni checks back or to evaluate a potential bet, though calling a large bet on this four-straight board would be very difficult with just one pair.

Board J♣ K♣ T♠ 9♠ 4♦ ; my hand 9♣7♣. I turned second pair (9s on the turn) plus had a club draw on the flop that missed on the river. Flash checked the river after calling my flop and turn bets, which suggests a lot of medium-strength pairs and some showdown‑type hands (Kx, Jx, Tx, A‑high, missed draws). I have positive showdown equity vs a wide calling range (I beat A‑high and some air/missed draws) but I’m behind K/J/T and any Q (straight). With a small pot (3,400) and deep stacks, a half‑pot river bet is the right, low‑risk way to extract thin value from worse hands (A‑high, some Kx/Jx that will check‑call, missed draws) while keeping the price reasonable if I face resistance. I will bet half the pot to get value and to fold out pure air; if Flash raises large I’ll assume a stronger made hand and fold this vulnerable second pair.

The board is extremely coordinated (Jc-Kc-Ts-9s-4d), and any Queen or Eight completes a straight. Minni has been the aggressor on all three streets (flop, turn, and river). While I have top pair with Kings, my kicker (6) is very weak, and this board texture hits a Button opening range perfectly. Almost all of Minni's likely semi-bluffs on the flop and turn (like AQ, QJ, Q10, or even 89) have improved to a straight or two pair. Given the 25% pot odds required to call, it is unlikely that Minni is bluffing frequently enough on this specific four-straight board to justify a call with just a single pair. Preserving my stack for better spots is the more prudent play.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button (SB) holding 7-4 offsuit. While heads-up play typically demands a wide opening range, 7-4 offsuit is a very weak hand. Minni has been playing aggressively and effectively over the last few hands, and my stack is significantly smaller. Folding here is a disciplined way to avoid getting involved in a marginal post-flop situation with a sub-optimal hand while out of position against an active opponent.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♣ J♦ is a strong, positionally advantaged hand. I want to take the initiative and avoid giving the BB a cheap flop with a wide defending range, so I will raise rather than just call. Min raise is to 200, but a standard 3x BB to 300 gives good fold equity while leaving room to maneuver postflop with deep stacks (I have 24500). If called I plan to use position to c-bet on dry Ace-high or disconnected boards and control pot size on dangerous textures; if Flash 3-bets large I will reassess (can fold to extreme aggression), but overall raising to 300 maximizes EV here.

In heads-up play, King-Five offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend the big blind against a standard 3x raise. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call and see a flop. Given Minni's high frequency of raising, folding here would be too tight.

I defended my big blind with King-high and the flop comes Nine-Eight-Two rainbow. This board doesn't hit my hand directly, but it also doesn't hit a wide opening range from the button particularly hard. In heads-up play, King-high still has some showdown value. I will check to see how Minni reacts. Checking allows me to see if Minni continues to be aggressive or if she checks back, giving me a free card to potentially hit a King or a backdoor straight/pair.

Heads-up as the preflop aggressor with A♣J♦ on 9♣-8♠-2♣: I have ace-high with a backdoor club (needs both turn+river clubs) and the board is fairly dry. As the raiser I should take the initiative — a continuation bet here will fold out lots of worse hands (Kx, Qx, smaller aces with weak kickers, random floats) and price out equity hands. I don’t want to bloat the pot with deep stacks and no pair, so a disciplined half‑pot c-bet applies pressure while leaving room to fold to strong action. If Flash raises big I can fold (no made hand); if called I’ll use position to evaluate the turn and continue only on favorable cards or as a controlled bluff. Bet 300 into the 600 pot to maximize immediate fold equity and keep postflop options flexible.

On a flop of 9c-8s-2c, I have King-high with no direct draw. Minni has bet 300 into a 600 pot. In heads-up play, I still have two overcards and some backdoor equity, but this board texture is quite coordinated and favors many of the hands Minni would raise from the button (9x, 8x, or even pocket pairs). While I have defended frequently, I don't have enough to justify a call here given my lack of pair or significant draw. Fold is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 3h, a very weak starting hand. Given the current stack sizes and the poor playability of this hand, it is best to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than committing more chips to the pot with a sub-optimal hand.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Ten♠–Seven♦. In heads-up play you should open a very wide range from the button to take the initiative and use positional advantage postflop. Calling the 50 (seeing a flop passively) surrenders that initiative and gives the BB cheap equity; raising puts pressure on Flash and gives us fold equity. A standard 2×BB open to 200 total is a compact, effective size here — it builds the pot when we have the betting lead but keeps things controlled with our marginal hand. Postflop plan: continuation-bet many flops, fold to large, committed aggression (big 3-bets) unless we hit or have strong draw equity; call small–medium 3-bets to realize equity given deep stacks. Therefore I open-raise to 200 to seize initiative and exploit position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 9d. Minni (Button) has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, 98o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a wide button opening range. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is too good to fold, and the hand has decent playability on many flop textures.

I am out of position on a monotone flop of Kd 4d 3d. I hold the 9d, giving me a mediocre flush draw. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react to the board texture. I have no made hand and very little showdown value, so I'm looking to play defensively.

Heads-up on the button with T♠7♦ on a K♦4♦3♦ monotone flop. I have one diamond (7♦) giving me a four-flush with ~9 outs (~35% to hit by river) and I’m in position. Flash checked, so I have both equity and fold equity — a continuation bet here is a standard semi-bluff to fold out Kx without a diamond, medium pairs, and other non-diamond hands while building the pot when I hit. I’ll size to about half the pot to apply pressure without overcommitting (pot = 400, bet = 200). If I face a sizable check‑raise I’ll give up (they likely have the nut flush or a very strong hand); if called I’ll continue evaluating the turn and can barrel good turn cards or control the pot on dangerous cards.

I am out of position on a monotone diamond board with the 9d flush draw. Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot. With my flush draw and the fact that I only need to call 200 to win 800 (4:1 pot odds), calling is the mathematically correct play. I have 9 outs to a flush, though some of those could be second-best if Minni has a higher diamond. However, the price is good enough to see a turn card.

I am out of position on a board of Kd 4d 3d 7s. I currently hold the 9d, which gives me a flush draw. The 7s on the turn is a brick that doesn't change the board's texture. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see how she proceeds. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the pot odds provided, but checking allows me to potentially see a free river or keep the pot size manageable while I am still drawing.

I have T♠7♦ on K♦4♦3♦7♠. I just improved to a pair of sevens and still have a 4-flush (three diamonds on board + my 7♦) — there are 9 diamond outs to make a flush on the river and 2 remaining sevens to make trips, so ~11 outs (~24% to improve). Flash check‑called my flop bet and then checked the turn, so his calling range is wide: Kx, some medium pairs, single-card diamond combos, missed draws and occasionally a slow-played pair/set. Betting here is standard: I want fold equity to fold out Kx/no-diamond hands, charge any pure spade/club hearts draws, and extract value from worse pairs while keeping the pot manageable if I’m behind. I’ll make a half‑pot semi‑bluff/value bet (400 into 800). If he calls I can reassess on the river and continue with diamonds or check/river‑control on scary cards; if he raises I’ll assume strong made hands (sets/two diamonds) and fold or re-evaluate only to very large raises given pot odds and stack depth.

On a monotone board of Kd 4d 3d 7s, I hold the 9d, giving me a flush draw. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 800. I need to call 400 to potentially win a total pot of 1600, which requires 25% equity. While my direct odds of hitting a diamond on the river are approximately 19.5% (9 outs), the implied odds make this a reasonable call in heads-up play. If I hit my flush, I can potentially extract more value on the river, although I must remain cautious of higher diamonds (Ad, Qd, Jd, Td) in Minni's range. Given the pot size and my stack, calling is the most appropriate action to realize my equity.

I have 9-high and missed my flush draw on the river. The board is Kd 4d 3d 7s Js, and I have been calling Minni's bets with the 9d blocker. At this point, I have zero showdown value. While I could attempt to bluff by leading out or check-raising, Minni has shown significant aggression throughout the match and holds a chip lead. Given the board texture and my missed draw, the most prudent play is to check. If Minni bets, I will likely have to fold as I cannot realistically beat any part of her range and a bluff into a big stack is high risk.

I have T♠7♦ — a pair of sevens with a missed diamond flush (board K♦4♦3♦7♠J♠). Flash checked the river after calling a flop and turn bet, so his calling range is wide (Kx, some Jx/Qx, missed diamond draws, occasional sets). My hand has thin showdown value and a diamond blocker that reduces some of his flush combos, so a small value bet is appropriate to fold out pure air and get called by worse pairs and missed draws. I’ll make a half-pot thin value/targeted bluff (800 into 1600): it extracts value from hands like Qx/Jx and charges missed draws, while keeping the commitment small. If he raises big I’ll strongly assume a made hand (flush/set/Kx) and fold.

I have 9-high on a board of Kd 4d 3d 7s Js. I was chasing a flush with the 9d which did not materialize. Minni has bet 800 into a pot of 1600 on the river after showing aggression on every street. Since I have no showdown value and cannot beat any part of her range (even her bluffs likely consist of better high cards like Ace-high or Ten-high), and a river check-raise bluff is too risky given the current stack sizes and her demonstrated aggression, I must fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2o. In heads-up play, the Small Blind has position for the duration of the hand. However, Minni has shown significant aggression recently, frequently raising pre-flop and betting multiple streets post-flop. While I'm getting 3:1 odds to call the remaining 50 chips, Q2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly against such an aggressive opponent. I have been folding quite a bit, but I prefer to wait for a hand with better playability or showdown value to challenge her dominance rather than bleeding chips with marginal hands like this.

Heads-up on the button (also small blind) I should be opening a very wide range. Qd5h is a marginal but playable hand in position — it has decent postflop playability and blockers to some broadway combos. I’ll make a standard, small 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) to apply pressure, steal the blind, and take the initiative while keeping the pot controlled. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll use position to navigate post‑flop (c-bet favorable boards, check/call when appropriate).

In a heads-up situation, King-Eight offsuit (K8o) is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a min-raise from the Small Blind/Button. The pot odds are 4:1 (calling 100 to win a total pot of 400), and K8o has significant equity against a wide opening range. While Minni has been aggressive, folding here would be too tight, and raising (3-betting) is unnecessary given the strength of the hand and the fact that I will be out of position post-flop.

I have King-high on a board with an Ace and a Queen. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. This board heavily favors the range of the Button/Small Blind, and I have no significant draws or showdown value. Checking is the standard and most prudent play here to see how the opponent reacts.

Board is Qc As 7d; I hold Qd5h — top pair but a very weak kicker and the Ace on board sits heavily in Flash’s cold‑calling range from the BB. There are no meaningful draws to protect against, so a c‑bet would mainly get called by many A‑x and Qx hands that either beat me or have showdown value, and it can put me in a difficult spot vs a check‑raise. With position and deep stacks, pot control is preferable: check to keep the pot small, realize equity versus non‑A hands, and preserve the option to extract value on later safe runouts. If Flash bets a small/medium size I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn; if he raises strong I’ll fold to avoid committing with a marginal kicker.

On a board of Ace-high and Queen-high, my King-high has very little showdown value, but it is not a hand worth turning into a bluff yet. Minni checked back the flop, which suggests she may not have a strong Ace or Queen, but she could still have a marginal pair or be planning to call a bet. I will check the turn to see if she checks back again, which would give me a chance to see a free river or potentially bluff on the next street.

Check. I have Qd5h — top pair but a very weak kicker on a Qc As 7d 6s board. Flash’s check-check line on the flop and turn signals either weakness or a medium-strength Ace that’s not going to fold to a turn stab; there are virtually no clean draws to deny. Betting here will mostly get called by Ax (which beats me) or by Qx and other showdown hands (which I may be ahead of but can keep the pot small). With deep stacks I want pot control and to preserve options for the river: check to see how Flash proceeds, call small bets if he shows mild aggression, and only invest more if the runout improves my hand or he gives a clear read. If the river is a safe, non‑Ace card and he checks, I can probe for thin value; if he shows aggression on turn or river I’ll fold to large pressure given my poor kicker.

I have improved to a pair of Kings on the river. The action so far (Minni checking back on both the flop and the turn) suggests that she likely does not have an Ace. Since the King fell on the river, it is highly probable that my hand is now the best. I will place a value bet of half the pot, hoping to get called by a Queen or a marginal pocket pair that Minni was checking for showdown value.

I have Qd5h on Qc As 7d 6s Ks — top pair with a very weak kicker. Flash’s river 200 into a 600 pot is a small (~1/3‑pot) bet after he checked the flop and turn; that sizing is consistent with a thin value bet or a blocking probe/bluff. Calling is cheap and correct: I must call 200 to potentially win roughly 1,000 (after my call), giving me ~5:1 pot odds (≈16.7% equity required). My hand beats a lot of missed draws and worse Qx hands and will often be good against thin riverings — while folding would be too passive and raising to 400 just bloats the pot with a marginal kicker and will be called by Ax/Kx that already beat me. I’ll call to get to showdown and avoid committing more with a vulnerable kicker.
I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build a pot and take the initiative. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 total.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I have J♠2♣ — a weak, marginal hand but defendable from the big blind in heads‑up. Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed including my BB) gives me good pot odds (200 to win ~600 → need ~25% equity) versus a wide button opening range, so a standard defend is correct. I don’t want to 3‑bet and build a bigger pot out of position with such a hand, and folding would be too nitty given the price. Plan: see the flop, call with any pair or strong draw, but keep pot control and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards unless I pick up real equity.

I have J♠2♣ on K♥ Q♥ 8♠ — no pair and only a backdoor spade. The button opened wide preflop and likely holds many K/Q/top‑pair and heart‑draw combos that dominate me. Pot is small (600) and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially zero equity. Best play is pot control: check and see a free turn. If Flash fires a meaningful bet on this dry, high board I’ll fold to pressure; I’ll only consider calling very tiny probes if I have a clear plan to take the pot away on later streets.

I have middle pair (Queens) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a King-high board. While the King is an overcard, my hand is very strong in a heads-up range. Minni checked to me, and I want to bet for value against weaker Queens, draws (like J-10 or hearts), or pocket pairs. A bet also helps protect my equity against overcards and draws. I will bet 300, which is half the pot.

Heads‑up vs a wide button open — Flash c‑bet 300 into a 900 pot on K♥ Q♥ 8♠. I hold J♠2♣: no pair and only a backdoor spade, so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. The bet is small (300 to win ~1200 → ~25% pot‑odds), so a single call is the lowest‑variance, +EV line: call to see a turn and fold to meaningful turn/river pressure unless I pair my J or pick up real equity (running spades/Jacks). Raising would be speculative and folding here is too nitpicky given the price, so I call and keep pot control.

Heads‑up, I’m on the button’s left (BB) with J♠2♣ on 8♠ Q♥ K♥ K♦. Pot is 1,200 and there is currently no bet on the turn. I have no pair and essentially no realistic draw (a single spade and only 6 loose outs to pair), so my hand has very little showdown value against a wide button opening/c‑betting range that contains plenty of Kx, Qx and heart combos. The turn pairing the board actually increases the chance Flash has a king or a decent Q/heart‐value hand. My prior plan was pot control: call cheap on the flop and fold to meaningful pressure unless I improved. That still applies. Best line is to check and keep the pot small — I’ll fold to any sizeable bet on the turn, but will consider calling very small probes (roughly ≤300 into 1,200 for pot‑odds) to see a river. If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and only continue with real improvement or a well‑timed bluff. So: check to control the pot and avoid bloating with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the board pairs the King (8s-Qh-Kh-Kd). I currently hold Kings and Queens with an Ace kicker. This is a strong hand, but the second King is a bit of a double-edged sword. If Minni was calling the flop with a King, she now has trips. If she has a Queen, I am currently outkicking her. By checking back here, I am exercising pot control. I have significant showdown value, and checking allows me to see the river for free without risking a check-raise from a potential trip Kings. It also allows Minni to potentially bluff on the river with missed draws (like hearts or J-10), which I can then evaluate.

Board: 8♠ Q♥ K♥ K♦ 6♥. I have J♠2♣ — no pair, no heart, no real showdown equity. Flash opened wide and c-bet the flop, then both checked the turn, so his river range still contains plenty of Kx/Qx and some two‑heart combos that now make a flush. I have very poor blockers to either value or flush hands and calling or raising as a bluff is thin and high variance against a short buy‑in opponent who can easily call with many hands that beat me. Best low‑variance +EV line is pot control: check and fold to any river bet. If he checks back we see showdown (or muck if I lose) without risking chips. Action: check.

I hold Kings and Queens with an Ace kicker on a board where a flush and trip Kings are possible. After Minni checked the river, I have high showdown value but limited value-betting potential against hands that would actually call. Checking back ensures I win the pot against weaker Queens or missed straight draws while avoiding a potential check-raise from a flush or a King that played the hand passively. This is the most consistent play for pot control in a tournament setting.

Heads-up on the button with A♣5♥ (A5s) — a strong, playable hand with positional advantage. With deep stacks and only one opponent, I should be aggressive preflop to build pots and take the initiative. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) applies pressure to the big blind, gets value from worse aces and broadways, and still keeps many dominated hands and speculative calls in. I’ve already posted the 50 SB, so this is a 250-chip additional commitment; I can comfortably continue postflop or fold/call a big 3-bet depending on sizing. Overall raising to 300 maximizes EV in this heads-up spot.

I am holding 5c 6c in the big blind. This is a solid speculative hand with good playability and straight/flush potential. Minni opened to 300, and I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 pot odds. In a heads-up situation, defending suited connectors is standard, and calling allows me to see a flop without over-committing chips out of position.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) on a board with a King. Being out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts and decide whether to continue based on the bet size.

Heads-up on the button I opened preflop and have position and initiative. I don't have a made hand (A♥5♥ is just Ace-high), but I hold the Ace blocker and a backdoor heart possibility; Flash checked the flop (2♠ K♥ 4♥), which shows weakness or a marginal holding. A half-pot continuation bet here leverages position, folds out many missed broadways and small pairs, and keeps the pot manageable versus stronger hands. With deep stacks I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll bet ~50% pot to apply pressure but retain flexibility. If called I will proceed cautiously on the turn (check behind non-improving cards, value-bet if I pair my Ace or hit a heart, fold to heavy aggression if I miss). Action: bet 300.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) and some backdoor club potential. Minni's bet of 300 into a 600 pot represents a 1/3 pot continuation bet, which she likely makes with a wide range of hands including air, draws, and Kings. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring approximately 25% equity to call. While my direct equity with the gutshot is only about 8.5% for the next card, the presence of backdoor straight and flush possibilities, combined with potential implied odds if I hit my straight, makes a call reasonable at this price. Folding seems too tight against a small C-bet, and raising is risky out of position against an aggressive opponent.

I am currently out of position with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) and no pair. The turn card (9s) doesn't improve my hand or significantly change the board texture in a way that favors my range over Minni's. Since I am first to act on this street and have been playing a check-call line, checking is the most appropriate action. This allows me to see how Minni reacts and potentially take a free card if she checks back, or evaluate the pot odds if she decides to bet.

Heads-up on the button with A♥5♥. Pot is 1,200; I c-bet the K♥4♥2♠ flop for 300 and was called. Turn 9♠ is a blank for most of my opponent’s value range but I still have the nut heart draw (A♥ + two hearts on board = 4-to-a-flush) and the Ace as a blocker. Flash checking the turn shows some weakness or pot-control — a good spot to apply pressure with a semi-bluff. Betting puts pressure on single-pair hands (Kx, 9x, small pairs) and floats, builds the pot when I hit my flush (9 outs on the river ≈ 19.6%), and keeps the initiative in position. I’ll size to ~50% pot (600): big enough to generate folds and extract from worse, but small enough to preserve stack and fold to a large turn check‑raise. Plan: if called, proceed to the river and value-bet big when I complete a heart or an Ace; if I miss the river I’ll control pot size or fold to heavy aggression. If faced with a sizable turn raise I’ll assume I’m behind and fold.

I am currently holding 5c 6c on a board of 2s-Kh-4h-9s. I have a gutshot straight draw needing a 3 to complete a 6-high straight. Minni has bet 600 into a 1200 pot, giving me pot odds of 3:1. To justify a call based on pot odds alone, I would need approximately 25% equity. My 4 outs (the 3s) only give me about an 8.7% chance of hitting on the river. Given that I am out of position and Minni has shown strength by betting both the flop and the turn, I do not have sufficient equity or implied odds to continue with a weak gutshot. The most profitable play in the long run is to fold.

I have 3d 4h, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is only 50 chips to call from the small blind, the hand has very poor playability post-flop. Given Minni's recent aggressive play style, I'm likely to face significant pressure on the flop. I'll fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button I should open a very wide range — Q5o is perfectly fine to raise. I want to take the initiative and use position postflop, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB). That size gives fold equity vs weaker hands and keeps the pot manageable for postflop play against Flash’s 14,500 stack. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll usually fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet or realize my equity on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine suited (Qc 9c). Minni, the Button, has raised to 300. Q9s is a very strong hand for defending the Big Blind in heads-up play due to its potential for straights, flushes, and top-pair strength. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (200 to win a pot of 400). I will call to see the flop and play post-flop.

On this Ace-high board (As Ts 4c), the advantage lies significantly with the pre-flop raiser (Minni). My hand, Queen-Nine of clubs, has completely missed the flop, offering only very thin backdoor flush and straight possibilities. Standard play in the Big Blind as the caller is to check to the aggressor. I will check and likely fold to a continuation bet unless the price is extremely favorable or the turn improves my equity.

Heads-up I opened a very wide range preflop, but the flop A♠ T♠ 4♣ is hostile to my Q♠5♥. I have no pair and only a remote runner-runner spade flush backdoor — my hand has very little showdown value and poor equity versus the BB's calling range (many Ax/Tx and broadways). Flash checked, which suggests either weakness or a hand that wants to see a free card; my fold equity with a continuation bet is limited and a c-bet will often be called by better holdings. I’ll check to control the pot, preserve stack, and use position to reassess on the turn. Plan: check-call a small probe if he bets a price that gives correct pot odds to see a cheap turn, fold to large or polarizing bets, and look to bluff or realize equity only on favorable turn cards.

On the turn, the 6 of clubs gives me a flush draw. Since Minni checked back the flop on an Ace-high board, it is highly probable she does not have an Ace. By betting now, I can represent the Ace myself or a Ten, putting pressure on her likely weak holdings or draws. Even if called, I have significant equity with my queen-high flush draw.

Heads‑up on the button I opened a very wide range, so I checked the flop to control the pot. Now Flash bets 400 into a 600 pot (pot shows 1,000; calling 400 would make it 1,400), so I need ~400/1,400 ≈ 28.6% equity to justify a call.
