My Q♠5♥ has no pair and almost no meaningful draw on the turn — I have at best the three queens + three fives = 6 raw outs (~13% to hit on the river), and there is no way to make a flush with only one spade in hand and two spades on board with one card to come. Against Flash’s likely turn‑betting range (many A‑x, T‑x, some value combinations and thin river‑bluffs) my realistic equity is well below the ~29% required. A bluff‑raise is too risky here (he calls/3‑bets enough and I don’t have good blockers). I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

In heads-up play, Jack-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard play here to seize initiative and build the pot or take down the blinds immediately.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a button open to 300. Q7o is marginal but has decent showdown value and blocks some Qx combos; the opener’s range is wide from the button. Pot odds are attractive — I risk 200 to win a 400 pot (plus future action) — so defending is cheap. I don’t want to 3-bet with a weak offsuit queen out of position (a small 3-bet will get called too often and bloat a pot I’m likely to play OOP). I’ll call and see a flop, planning to control the pot and evaluate based on texture (check/call or fold to heavy pressure if I miss).

Heads-up on a K♥9♥4♣ flop with Q♥7♠. I’ve missed but have a Queen blocker and some showdown value against a wide button opening range. Out of position with a marginal hand I want to control the pot and force Flash to pay to get value, so I’ll check. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (~<=1/2 pot) to see a turn and fold to large overbets or a check-raise; if checked through I’ll show down or reassess on the turn.

I have hit middle pair (Nines) on a King-high board. While this hand has some value, it is not strong enough to bet for three streets of value, and checking back is a standard way to control the pot size and reach showdown. By checking, I also protect my checking range and can evaluate the turn based on the next card and Minni's action.

I have Q♥7♠ on a 9♥ K♥ 4♣ K♣ board. I missed — no pair and no meaningful draws. The turn pairing the board means any King in Flash’s range now beats me (trips), and many mid-strength hands (9x, 4x, big overcards that connected) will still call a bet. Flash checked both flop and turn, so he could be weak or slow-playing a K, but with my weak hand out of position I want pot control and to avoid getting raised off a marginal holding. A small stab will fold out only the very weakest hands and get called by better — negative EV. I’ll check and go to showdown or fold to significant river aggression.

I have a pair of nines with a Jack kicker on a board of 9h-Kh-4c-Kc. Minni has checked for the second time. While my hand has improved to two pair (Kings and Nines), it remains a medium-strength hand. Checking back on the turn allows me to control the pot size and ensures I reach the river to see a showdown. Betting now might force out hands I beat while only getting called by better ones or draws that have significant equity. Checking is the most prudent way to realize my hand's value.

Board K♥ K♣ 9♥ 4♣ 8♣, my hand Q♥7♠. Both players checked flop and turn — Flash’s line is passive. River completed a third club, which gives opponents two‑club combos a flush; those hands would often bet for value, not check. My hand has only queen‑high showdown value and very little fold equity as a bluff. A thin value bet is unlikely to get called by many worse hands and will get called or raised by better (Kx, 9x, full houses, club flushes). Given I’m out of position, the pot is small (600) and there’s no meaningful upside to bluffing here, the lowest‑risk, highest‑EV line is to check and take my showdown. If Flash bets, I can fold to significant river aggression.

On the river board of 9h-Kh-4c-Kc-8c, I have two pair, Kings and Nines, with a Jack kicker. Minni has checked on every street after calling my pre-flop raise. While my hand is relatively strong, it is a classic 'showdown value' hand. If I bet, it is unlikely that Minni will call with a hand weaker than mine (like a pair of fours or a low pocket pair), but she might call or raise with a better Nine (A9, Q9) or a slow-played King. By checking, I ensure that I reach showdown and win against her missed draws and weaker pairs without risking further chips.

Heads-up on the button with ThTs — a very strong, clear open-raise hand. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to complete, but I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and extract value from worse hands (Axs, Kx, mid pairs, suited connectors) while keeping some fold equity. Against a single big-blind with deep stacks I'll make a standard heads-up raise to 300 (3x the BB). That size is big enough to discourage some speculative calls and define ranges, yet small enough to keep worse hands and weaker pairs in and preserve postflop playability. If Flash 3-bets, I’ll evaluate based on sizing and tendencies (call smaller 3-bets, consider 4-bet/fold vs very large/ polar 3-bets). Total commit will be 300 chips (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so I add 250).

I am holding 9-2 offsuit in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and plays very poorly out of position. Minni has been aggressive with pre-flop raises, and while I only need to call 200 more into a 400 pot, 9-2 offsuit rarely realizes its equity. It's better to fold now and wait for a more playable hand to conserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation, 9-8 offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button (Small Blind). Raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a continuation bet on the flop. Given the stack sizes, a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) who opened to 300. I have A♠Q♣ — a top heads-up hand that is ahead of a wide SB opening range and has good blockers. With healthy stacks I want to take the initiative and extract value / fold equity rather than play a capped OOP range. I 3-bet to 1000 (≈3.3x the open): big enough to pressure weaker broadways and many suited connectors but small enough to keep worse hands calling. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’m OOP so proceed with a controlled postflop approach — c-bet medium on favorable/wet boards, check/control on dry A/Q-high textures and re-evaluate vs large resistance. If he 4-bets large I will typically fold unless the sizing and pot odds justify a call.

I raised from the button with 9-8 offsuit to take control of the hand, but Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While 9-8 offsuit has some connectivity, it is significantly behind a standard 3-betting range and performs poorly when called or re-raised. Calling the extra 700 out of position (or even with the button) puts me in a difficult spot post-flop where I am likely to be dominated. Given our deep stacks and the fact that we are only on hand 56 of 150, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with T♦4♦ (suited). In heads-up play the button should be opening very wide — this hand is fine to raise for initiative and to leverage position postflop. I’ll make a standard small open to 2BB (raise to 200) to pressure the big blind, keep the pot manageable vs a deep, potentially aggressive opponent, and retain good postflop playability with a suited ten. Plan: fold to an obviously large 3-bet (or re-evaluate vs sizing); if called, use position to c-bet dry flops, pot-control on connected/paired boards, and look to realize equity or take the pot away when appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 3s. Minni, who has a larger stack, has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, 7-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and plays very poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive opponent like Minni. I will fold and wait for a better spot to protect my stack.

In heads-up play, Queen-high hands like Q6o are generally strong enough to open from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot. If the opponent 3-bets, I can evaluate, but folding pre-flop would be too conservative for this position.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (total 300). Q♦4♦ is a suited queen with decent preflop equity and good playability heads-up. Pot odds: I must call 200 to win ~400 (≈33% break-even). Against a wide button opening range my equity with Q4s should exceed that and deep effective stacks give solid implied odds on sets/flushes or when I hit a Q. 3-betting light is an option, but I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a marginal hand — calling keeps worse hands and lets me realize equity postflop. I will call the 200 (total commit 300).

Check. I'm out of position on a dry-ish 8♣7♣2♦ board with Q♦4♦ — I have only an overcard and a backdoor diamond, no club draw. The BTN's opening range hits this flop frequently and will c-bet a lot, so leading OOP with a marginal holding just bloats the pot and invites raises. With deep stacks I want to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and see how Flash reacts. Plan: check-call a standard continuation bet to keep worse hands and bluffs in, fold to heavy aggression, and if Flash checks back re-evaluate the turn (bet if I pick up real equity or take a free card).

On a flop of 8c-7c-2d, I have Queen-high with one overcard and no direct draw. Minni checked, which is standard for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. While I have little connection to this board, it favors the caller's range (78, 89, connectors). However, I should still represent strength with a continuation bet to try and take down the pot immediately, as I still have the range advantage from raising pre-flop. A standard half-pot bet should suffice to test her interest in the hand.

Heads-up, I’m OOP with Q♦4♦ on 8♣7♣2♦. Pot = 900 and I must call 300, so I need ~25% equity to break even. Versus a wide button open/c-bet range my hand comfortably has that equity — a decent chance to hit a Q (top pair), plus runner-runner diamond potential and implied odds with deep stacks. Raising OOP with a marginal holding would bloat the pot and play poorly if I miss. Plan: call the 300 to control pot size and realize equity; re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair my Q or pick up real equity I can start betting, if Flash barrels big or raises I’ll give up.

I called the flop to control the pot and realize equity, but the turn (J♣) kills my backdoor diamond and leaves me with only one real overcard (Q♦) and about 6 marginal outs (3 queens + 3 fours) — roughly ~13% to improve on the river. Flash checked the turn, which looks like pot control or weakness. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position with poor fold equity and put me in tough spots if raised. With deep stacks and such low river equity, the correct line is to keep the pot small and see a free river: check and re-evaluate. If I pair up on the river I'll bet for value; if Flash leads big when I miss I'll fold.

The Jack of clubs is a highly coordinated card that potentially completes flushes or provides a pair of Jacks. While I could attempt to represent these hands with a second barrel, Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size with my Queen-high and see the river for free, preserving my stack in case she is trapping or has improved her hand.

River (8c 7c 2d Jc Ac) — I hold Q♦4♦ and was controlling pot oop. The river Ace kills most of my showdown chances: any A‑x, Jx, pocket pair, or (critically) any club in Flash’s hand (there are 4 clubs on board) already makes a flush. Flash’s line — open, flop c‑bet, then checking turn — is consistent with both missed draws/spewy c‑bets and slowplayed strongs; that ambiguity means I have very little fold equity bluffing. Betting here is high variance and unlikely to extract value; checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to real strength. Plan: check and fold to aggression; if Flash checks back I’ll see showdown for free and hope he missed. This minimizes risk and is the lowest‑variance, +EV line given the board texture and his line.

On the river, the board shows four clubs (8c, 7c, Jc, Ac), creating a highly coordinated texture. My hand, Qs 6d, does not contain a club, meaning any single club in Minni's hand gives her a flush. While I have the Ace of clubs on the board to provide a high card, my overall showdown value is extremely low (Ace-high with a Queen kicker using the board). Minni checked the river after calling my flop bet and seeing the turn check through. This check could be a trap with a flush or a sign of a weak pair/missed hand. However, given that she called the flop, her range is weighted towards pairs and draws, many of which would have included a club. Bluffing into a four-flush board is risky, especially since I have no blockers to the flush. Checking back is the safest way to preserve my stack, as I am unlikely to fold out a better hand or a club, and I may occasionally win against a total air hand like 5-4 or 6-5 without a club.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with pocket 5s — this is a clear open-raise for value and initiative. Flash in the BB will have a very wide range, so I want to raise to deny good odds for a cheap flop and build a pot when I have the lead. Standard heads-up sizing ~3x the BB is appropriate here: raise to 300 total. If he 3-bets a small/moderate amount I can call and set-mine; versus a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an isolation shove or very large 3-bet). Pot math/position favors aggression — raise to 300.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding 8h 4d in the big blind. Minni, who has been relatively aggressive throughout the session and currently holds a significant chip lead, has raised to 3BB (300). 84o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I am looking to preserve my stack and wait for better spots to close the gap, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

I have Ace-Six offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand to open or call with. Since I'm on the button, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand.

I’m BB with Q♦6♦ facing a button raise to 300. I’ve already posted 100, so calling costs 200 more and my total committed on this street will be 300. In heads‑up play the opener’s range is wide and Q6s has decent equity (top‑pair potential, backdoor flush/straight possibilities) and good implied odds with deep stacks. I don’t want to 3‑bet a marginal suited hand out of position and bloat the pot, so I’ll defend by calling and see a flop; I’ll proceed cautiously if I miss and look for value/plays when I connect.

I check. Q♦6♦ missed the flop K♦–8♣–2♣ — I have no pair and only two‑card backdoor diamond potential (very small two‑card-runout equity). In heads‑up play the button’s opening range is wide so he will c‑bet often, but the K hits a lot of his range and the two clubs make the board a bit wet, so bluffing here is not as clean. I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand, so I’ll control the pot and see what Flash does. Plan: if he fires a small c‑bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈200) I can call to float and see the turn; fold to large sizing (>~1/2 pot) unless I improve or get a clear read. If he checks back I take a free turn card and re‑evaluate.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Six offsuit and found a King-high flop with two clubs. While I only have Ace-high, as the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture (Kd-8c-2c) is generally better for my perceived range than the big blind's calling range. Since Minni checked to me, I will put in a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to represent the King and try to take the pot down immediately, while also holding the Ace of Clubs as a useful blocker and potential backdoor nut flush draw.

Flash's flop c-bet is 300 into a 900 pot (1/3 pot). I'm out of position with Q♦6♦ on K♦–8♣–2♣: no pair, only a very small two-card backdoor diamond possibility (runner-runner) and a few single-card outs (queens). Calling 300 gives me 1200 to win, so I need ~25% equity to justify a call. Versus a wide button c-bet this small, his range will include many hands I have reasonable equity against (broadways, small pairs, missed draws), so a call to float and see the turn is the best play. I don't want to 3-bet OOP with a marginal hand and bloat the pot. Plan: call the small sizing, evaluate the turn — fold to a large turn bet unless I pair/up or pick up a clear draw; continue/raise only if the turn improves my hand or gives a realistic bluffing line.

Out of position with Q♦6♦ on 2♣–8♣–K♦–A♥. I missed both flop and turn — I have no pair and only about 6 clean outs (≈13% to pair on the river). The turn A♥ significantly improves the button’s range (lots of Ax, Kx, two‑pair and strong value hands), and bluffing OOP here has poor fold equity and can get me raised. I want to keep the pot small and avoid building a big pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll check and control the size. Plan: check‑fold to any meaningful bet on the turn (call only a ridiculously tiny probe if I have a strong read); if Flash checks back I’ll take the free river and reassess (look to value/blur only if I actually pair or pick up clear equity).

The Turn card is the Ace of Hearts, which gives me top pair. Minni has checked to me for the second street in a row after calling my flop continuation bet. Since the board now contains a King and an Ace, it is highly likely that I have the best hand unless Minni is slow-playing a set or has an Ace with a better kicker. However, I need to bet for value against King-x hands and to charge any remaining flush draws (though I block the nut flush draw with the Ace of Clubs). I will bet 600, which is half the pot, to continue extracting value while keeping the pot controlled.

Pot 1800, to call 600. I hold Q♦6♦ on 2♣–8♣–K♦–A♥: no pair and only about 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 sixes) ≈13% to improve on the river. Calling 600 to win a 2400 pot requires roughly 25% equity, so this is a clear negative expected-value call. Flash’s line (open, flop c‑bet, turn bet) is consistent with lots of value (Ax, Kx, two‑pair/sets) and the turn A greatly strengthens his value combinations; I have very little fold equity OOP and raising as a bluff with no pair or real draw is unattractive. Per my plan to check‑fold to any meaningful turn bet, I will fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with AsJs — a premium heads-up hand that plays extremely well raised. I want initiative, fold equity, and to isolate the big blind rather than giving a cheap flop. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 balances extracting value from worse broadways/suited connectors and keeps the pot manageable versus deeper stacks (Flash ~15100). Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites cheap flops and more frequent 3-bets; 300 gives good leverage and leaves room to adjust if he 3-bets. Plan: C-bet favorable textures, control size vs aggression, and use position postflop to maximize EV.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d Th and facing a button raise to 300 from Minni. My hand is weak and does not play well post-flop out of position against a player who has a significant stack advantage. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have K9s on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni. K9s is a strong hand in this context, well above the average. Minni has been quite active and has a larger stack, but I have a solid stack of 150 BBs. Raising here is the standard play to take the initiative and build the pot with a hand that has good high-card value and flush potential.

Heads-up as the big blind with A♥Q♠ (AQ offsuit) facing a 300 button open — this is a strong hand against a wide opening range. I want to play aggressively to get value and fold equity rather than passively calling out of position. A standard 3-bet (~3x the open) keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks while applying pressure. Raising to 900 forces Flash to call 600 more to win ~1800, gives me good fold equity, and leaves plenty of chips for post‑flop play if called. If Flash 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate; otherwise I take the initiative with a 3-bet to 900.

I started by raising with King-Nine of spades, a strong suited hand in heads-up play. Minni responded with a 3-bet to 900. While Minni has shown aggression, K9s is too strong to fold here, especially with the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to see the flop and utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot too much pre-flop with a 4-bet.

I have A♥Q♠ on J♦Q♥6♠ — top pair with the nut ace-kicker. I was the preflop aggressor (3-bet) so my range is strong; betting now gets value from worse Qx, Jx and charges any backdoor draws while denying free cards. Pot is 1,800, so a half-pot continuation bet applies pressure without bloating the pot (keeps stacks manageable) and is likely to get called by worse hands. If Flash raises large I’ll respect that as a strong range (sets/two-pair/strong Jx) and re-evaluate (likely just call small raises, fold to a shove-sized 3-bet depending on sizing).

On a flop of Queen-Jack-Six, I currently only have King-high with a backdoor flush draw and a backdoor straight draw. This board texture heavily favors Minni's 3-betting range, which likely includes strong Queens, Jacks, or overpairs like Aces and Kings. Minni's half-pot lead on the flop shows continued aggression. Calling 900 chips with very little current equity and only marginal drawing potential is not a profitable play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity to engage.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Ad Jd — a very strong, highly playable hand. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want the initiative and to build a pot with a hand that plays well postflop in position. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 accomplishes that: it prices out the weakest holdings, gets value from worse A-x and broadways, and keeps the pot manageable against a potential 3-bet given deep stacks (my 25.75k vs his 14.1k). Plan: if called I’ll use position to c-bet good flops and extract value; if met with a large 3-bet I’ll reassess based on sizing and opponent tendencies (fold or proceed cautiously).

Pocket eights are a very strong hand in a heads-up match. Given Minni's frequent opening from the button/small blind, I want to take the initiative and build a pot for value. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard sizing that allows me to put pressure on their opening range while retaining plenty of maneuverability post-flop with a pocket pair that often plays well against missed broadway cards.

Heads‑up on the button with A♦J♦. I opened to 300 and Flash 3‑bet to 1000 (pot 1300, 700 to call). AJs is a strong, playable hand in position with good equity versus a likely wide-ish 3‑bet range and excellent postflop maneuverability. The 3‑bet sizing is moderate and not hugely polarizing, so I prefer to keep the pot and use position rather than 4‑beting and bloating the pot OOP. I will call the 700 (total commit 1000) to see a flop and plan to c‑bet on favorable textures (A/J/diamond draws), control the pot on dry boards, and re-evaluate if Flash applies heavy aggression (large 4‑bet/shove).

I have a medium pocket pair on a board with two overcards (King and Jack). Although I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture is quite favorable for a 3-bet calling range, which often includes hands like KJ, KQ, or AJ. By checking, I can control the size of the pot and evaluate Minni's reaction. If Minni checks back, I may still have the best hand; if Minni bets, I can decide whether to continue based on the bet sizing and the turn card.

Flop Kc 5h Js — I hold A♦J♦: a made pair of jacks with the best possible kicker against a large portion of Flash’s 3‑betting range (and I block some AK/AJ combos). Flash checked to me, which polarizes his line into Kx, medium pocket pairs, floats/missed broadways, and some bluffs. I want to extract value from worse Jx, A‑x, and small pairs and deny free cards to turn/river equity, while keeping the pot manageable given stacks and the preflop action. A bet of about 1,200 into the 2,000 pot (~60%) accomplishes that — it gets value and fold equity without overcommitting. Plan: if called, re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value on A or J, pot‑control or check behind on scary cards like K/Q, and fold to very large aggression). If Flash check‑raises big, treat it as a polarized, strong line and strongly consider folding.

Pocket eights are now a vulnerable mid-pair on a board featuring two overcards, the King and Jack. These cards significantly overlap with the range of hands Minni would use to call a pre-flop 3-bet (such as KQ, KJ, QJ, or AJ). Minni's large bet of 1200 into a 2000 pot (60% pot) indicates a strong desire to take down the pot or extract value. Continuing with eights here would likely lead to difficult decisions on later streets, as many turn and river cards further improve Minni's potential range or make it harder for me to call a second or third barrel. I choose to fold and wait for a more favorable board texture.

I am on the button with A4o. This is a standard opening range hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has a significantly larger stack, but I still have over 130 big blinds, which is deep enough to play normally. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage the strength of my Ace-high hand.

Heads-up vs a 3x open from the SB, Q7o is a playable hand with decent showdown value and a useful queen-blocker. Calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity with deep stacks rather than committing out of position with a thin 3-bet. A min-raise to 500 would be a weak-sized 3-bet that gives little fold equity and would often put me in tough OOP spots; a larger 3-bet is an option as a polarized bluff but isn’t necessary here. Plan: call to see the flop (300 total). On a Q or favorable dry board I’ll play for value/initiative; on coordinated/ace-heavy boards I’ll exercise pot control and fold to large action if I miss.

Check. Heads‑up out of position vs the SB who opened 3x preflop. The flop Ac 3h 6s is dry and heavily favors the raiser — I have no pair and no real draws with Q7o (only a weak queen blocker to some AQ combos). Leading out OOP is unattractive; I want to keep the pot small and see how Flash reacts. If he checks I’ll take a free turn; if he makes a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) I’ll consider a call to realize equity and deny pure bluff equity; if he fires a medium/large bet I’ll fold since his range will be rich in Aces and made hands. Pot control and preserving stack equity is the priority.

On the flop of Ac 3h 6s, I have top pair with a weak kicker (Ad 4s). This is a strong hand in heads-up play, but I need to be careful of better Aces. Minni checked to me. I will bet 300 (half pot) to extract value from draws or middle pairs and to protect my hand. A continuation bet is standard here on this dry board.

Call 300. Heads‑up out of position on a very dry Ac 3h 6s flop — Q7o has some pure showdown value and a useful queen‑blocker against Ax combos. Flash’s c‑bet is small (~1/3 pot) and represents a wide continuing range, so calling is the best way to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and deny him pure bluff equity. Pot odds are favorable (calling 300 to win roughly 1500 ≈ ~20% break‑even equity). I’ll call to see the turn and re‑evaluate: if he fires a sizable turn barrel representing A/x or better I’ll fold; if the turn brings a Q or he checks, I can start extracting value or take the lead, otherwise continue pot control.

I'm out of position with Qh7d on a very dry Ac 3h 6s 8d board. Pot is 1,200 and effective stacks ~12,600 (SPR ≈ 10) — my hand has essentially no showdown improvement and only a queen-blocker to AQ. Flash c-bet small on the flop and checked the turn, which keeps a wide continuing range but also includes a lot of Ax and medium pairs that dominate me. Betting OOP here to try stealing is thin and exposes me to check-raises or costly calls on later streets; pot control is the priority. I’ll check back the turn, realize equity cheaply, and re-evaluate the river: if I pair a Q I can target value, if he fires a small bet on a blank I’ll call for pot odds, and I’ll fold to a large river barrel representing A/x or better.

I have top pair with an Ace and a weak kicker (4) on a very dry board. I bet the flop and Minni called. On the turn, the 8d doesn't change much. Checking back here is a solid pot-control move. It prevents me from getting check-raised off my hand and keeps the pot manageable, given that my kicker is weak. I can likely call most river bets or value-bet myself if checked to again on a safe river.

I made a pair of queens on the river (Qh7d on Ac 3h 6s 8d Qc). Villain opened from the SB, made a small flop c-bet (300) and then checked the turn and checked the river – a line that indicates a lot of pot‑control A‑x, medium pairs and plenty of missed hands. My Q blocks AQ combos and turns my hand from a pure bluff-catcher into thin value: I’m ahead of many missed holdings (Kx, single overcards, some small pairs that will check) and behind most Ax and any made two‑pair/trips. Checking back hands up the showdown against obvious A‑x, but misses value and fold equity. I’ll exploit the checked line by betting a modest value/steal size (≈ half pot) to extract from worse hands and fold out pure air. Bet 600 keeps the risk controlled — it gets called by worse occasionally and folds enough bluffs/missed hands to be +EV. If Flash raises big I’ll assume he has an Ace or better and fold to preserve stack equity.

I have top pair with an Ace on a very dry board. I checked the turn for pot control, which might have encouraged Minni to lead the river. Her 600 bet into an 1200 pot (making it 1800 total) represents a 1/3 to 1/2 pot-sized bet. While she could have improved to two pair (AQ, A8) or have a better kicker (A5-AJ), folding top pair in a heads-up scenario after checking the turn is generally too exploitable. I need to be right only 25% of the time to break even. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (also small blind) I should open a very wide range — 2♦5♦ is weak but playable in position with backdoor diamond/straight potential. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to complete; a standard 2xBB open to 200 buys initiative and applies fold equity while keeping the pot small relative to our deep stacks. I'll raise to 200 (total) to take the lead; plan to c-bet on favorable or dry flops, use position to realize equity on diamond/low runouts, and concede/pepper down when the board heavily favors opponent ranges.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6c 8c, which is a decent suited connector. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, getting 3-to-1 odds. This hand has good playability and I should defend my big blind against a small open, especially since we are heads-up.

I have completely missed this flop with 8-high and no real draws. This board (Ks Qh 9s) is very favorable for a pre-flop raiser's range, which likely includes many Broadway cards and suited connectors. Acting first, I will check and plan to fold to most bets, as I have almost zero equity in this pot.

I opened wide from the button and took the initiative. The flop K♠ Q♥ 9♠ favors my raising range (lots of Kx/Qx/broadways) and Flash’s check shows weakness or a draw — good spot to c-bet. My 2♦5♦ has almost no showdown value but a tiny backdoor diamond/straight plan; I’ll use a medium continuation bet to buy the pot or get called by worse. Betting half pot (200) preserves fold equity, keeps the pot controllable with a marginal hand, and allows me to fold to a large check‑raise while still getting value or equity if called. If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to significant aggression unless I pick up clear equity.

I have completely missed this flop with 8-high and no draws on a very coordinated board of Ks-Qh-9s. This board heavily favors my opponent's pre-flop raising range, which includes many Broadway cards. I have no equity to continue, so I will fold to the bet.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo), which is a very strong premium hand in heads-up play. Standard procedure is to raise for value and to take control of the hand. I'll raise to 300, which is 3x the big blind, to build the pot and see how Minni responds.

Heads-up versus a wide button open to 300: J♠8♣ is a playable but non‑premium hand. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives good pot odds (≈33%) and lets me realize equity with deep stacks while keeping the pot manageable. A min‑3bet to 500 would be a thin bluff out of position and unnecessary here. Plan: call, see the flop, proceed with top pair/two pair/strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

I have top pair (8s) with a decent Jack kicker on 7♠-8♥-7♣. Heads‑up the opener checked, which is often weakness or a trap; with position I want to extract value and deny free cards. Pot is 600 so a ~50% pot c‑bet balances value and pot control while pricing worse pairs/overcards to call. If Flash check‑raises strongly I’ll proceed cautiously (big check‑raise usually represents trips/sets and I’ll likely fold to large aggression; I’ll call small raises to see a turn).

On a board of 7s-8h-7c, my Ace-Queen high still has significant value against a wide range of hands that Minni could be leading with from the Big Blind. Minni's lead for 300 into a 600 pot (half-pot) could represent a variety of hands: a pair of eights, a seven (unlikely given I have an Ace and a Queen, though possible), or more likely a draw like 9-10, 6-5, or even just overcards like mine. Since I have two overcards and the board is paired, my hand is currently ahead of most of her bluffs and drawing hands. I will call to see the turn, maintain pot control, and keep her bluffs in the hand. Raising here might force out the hands I beat and only get action from hands that have me crushed.

Turn Q♥ changes the texture — it’s an overcard to my J kicker and it helps some hands that wouldn’t have called the flop. I have top pair (8s) with a decent J kicker, but villain called a small half‑pot flop bet so his calling range is heavy on hands that include a 7 (trips), single‑8 combos and some broadways. Betting again builds the pot and either folds out the weaker 8s I want value from or gets check‑raised by trips/strong hands putting me in a very uncomfortable spot. With pot control and stack preservation in mind I’ll check to keep worse 8s and bluffs in, realize my equity, and only call a small turn bet — folding to a large, polarized raise. Check and re‑evaluate on the river if he checks back.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts is an excellent card for me as it gives me top pair with an Ace kicker. Minni's check after leading out on the flop (a 'donk bet') often indicates she was either bluffing, has a marginal hand like a pair of eights, or is now afraid of the overcard. I have a clear value bet here to get paid by eights, smaller pocket pairs, or straight draws like 9-10 or 6-5. I will bet 600, which is half the pot, to build value while still being a size she can comfortably call with a weaker made hand or a draw.

I hold J♠8♣ — on 7♠-8♥-7♣ that’s actually two pair (8s and 7s). The Q♥ on the turn is an overcard to my kicker but doesn’t beat my two pair; the only realistic hands that crush me are full houses/trips (77, 88). I checked the turn to keep worse 8x hands and bluffs in and to control the pot. Flash’s bet is small (600 into a 1,800 pot) — calling 600 to win a ~3,000 pot requires only ≈20% equity. My two pair is ahead of the bulk of his continuing range (Qx as a thin value, single 8s, floats and bluffs), so calling is the most +EV, lowest‑risk line: it keeps bluffs/worse hands in, avoids bloating the pot vs the rare full house, and lets me re‑evaluate on the river. Plan: call 600 now; on the river extract thin value if checked to, call small bets, and fold to a large, polarized shove unless pot odds strongly favor a call.

I hold J♠8♣ — two pair (8s and 7s) on 7♠-8♥-7♣-Q♥-2♥. Very few hands beat me (quads 77, full houses from 88/7+8/Q7 or pocket QQ), but the bulk of Flash’s continuing range (single 8s, Qx without a full house, floats and bluffs) is behind or will call a small value bet. He checked the river (to_call = 0), which is common with hands that will call a modest bet but not raise. Pot is 2400; a ~50% pot value bet keeps the pot controlled vs the rare full house/quads while extracting from worse two‑pair, single 8s, Q8, and bluffs. I will bet a half‑pot to get thin value and avoid bloating the pot against a polarized range — if he check‑raises big I’ll assume a very strong full house/quads and fold; I’ll only continue to small resistance sized raises.

I have Top Pair, Top Kicker (AQ on a 7-8-7-Q-2 board). Minni's betting line is unusual: she donk-bet the flop, called my value bet on the turn when the Queen hit, and is now leading the river for half-pot. In heads-up play, Ace-Queen is very strong here. While it's possible she has a 7 for trips, her line also strongly suggests a weaker Queen (like QJ or QT) or a mid-pair (like 99 or 1010) that she's trying to get to showdown with or use as a 'blocking bet'. Given that I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, I have a very easy call to see the showdown. Raising would likely only get called by a hand that beats me (a 7 or a full house).

I have 3♥9♦ — a very weak offsuit hand. In heads-up play from the button (I already posted SB=50) I have a big positional advantage and it only costs me 50 more to see a flop. The pot is 150 so calling 50 (100 total committed this round) gives good odds to see a flop. Raising to 200 is an option as a light steal, but with this hand it has limited fold equity and will often be called, putting me in multi-street pots with a marginal holding. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot small and use position post‑flop. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o. The Small Blind (Minni) has just limped in, and I have the option to check or raise. J3o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Given that Minni has a larger stack and has been playing aggressively in previous hands, there is no reason to inflate the pot with a sub-par hand. Checking is the standard play here to see a free flop and potentially realize some equity if I hit a Jack or a favorable board.

I am first to act on the flop after a limped pot pre-flop. I have Jack-high on a 2-7-8 rainbow board. While I have two overcards to the board, this is a very weak hand with no direct draws. In a small pot, there is no need to bluff or lead out. Checking is the most prudent play to see how Minni responds and to potentially see a free turn card if she checks back.

Heads‑up on the button with position and the BB having checked — standard spot to apply pressure. My hand (9♦3♥) has almost no showdown value: no pair, only a weak backdoor diamond/straight draw (flush by river ~4%). The flop (2♥ 7♦ 8♦) is somewhat connected and two‑toned, so I don’t want to overcommit if I face aggression. I’ll make a standard positional continuation bet to fold out unmade hands and pick up the pot now. Size ~50% pot (100) preserves fold equity while keeping my risk low; if Flash raises I’ll fold and conserve the deep stack for better spots. If called I’ll proceed cautiously and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have Jack-high on a 2-7-8 board with no draws. Minni has led out for half the pot. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, my hand is extremely weak and has very little chance of improving to the best hand or winning at showdown. Folding here is the most disciplined play to avoid wasting chips in a pot where I have negligible equity.

I am on the button with 8s 2s. This is a weak hand, but it is suited. In heads-up play, almost any suited hand has some value from the button, especially since I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop. However, Minni has been playing aggressively and has a larger stack. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play with such a weak hand, but given the pot odds (3:1), calling is also an option. Since I want to preserve my stack and wait for better spots against a chip leader, I will fold this hand and look for a stronger starting range to engage with.

Heads-up on the button with 8♣7♣ — a suited connector that plays very well in position. Pot is 150, I need to put in 50 to call; minimum raise is to 200. In heads-up the button should be raising wide to seize initiative and apply pressure; 8-7s is a clear open-raise candidate because of its straight/flush potential and good postflop playability. I’ll raise to the minimum 200 (2×BB) to build the pot in position, induce calls from worse hands, and keep the pot controllable versus Flash’s aggressive tendencies. Plan: if called use position to C-bet favorable club/connected boards and control on dry overcards; fold or pot-control versus significant resistance; re-evaluate vs any 3‑bet based on sizing and stack depth.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Six offsuit is strong enough to defend the big blind against a 2x raise from the small blind/button. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 300), which requires roughly 25% equity. Given Minni's wide opening range in this position, I have sufficient equity to see a flop and evaluate, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

On this Ace-high board (As-Jc-5s), my hand (Qd-6h) has no real connectivity and very little showdown value. In heads-up play, the button (Minni) often continuation-bets this flop regardless of their actual holdings because it favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Since there is no current bet to call and I have no pair or draw, checking is the standard play to see what Minni does. If she bets, I will likely fold unless the bet size is extremely small, as I have no equity to continue.

Check. I raised pre‑flop with 8♣7♣ but this flop (A♦ J♣ 5♠) misses my hand — I have no pair and only a backdoor club draw that needs both turn+river to complete. This is a dry overcard board that hits a large portion of Flash’s defend/call range (A/x, Jx, strong broadways, pockets), so I want to keep the pot under control and use my position to gather info. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve; if he bets a small/medium size (roughly ≤½ pot ≈200) I’ll call to realize equity and see a cheap turn, but I’ll fold to large pressure unless I pair or pick up a club.

On the turn board of 5s-Jc-Ad-9c, I still have no pair and no draw with Queen-high. Minni checked back the flop, which often indicates she lacks an Ace or a strong Jack, but she could still have a small pair (5s, 44, etc.) or a medium hand she's playing for pot control. Since I have no equity, I will check to see the river for free. If Minni bets, I will likely fold as Queen-high is unlikely to be the best hand if she shows interest in the pot.

I raised preflop with 8♣7♣ and the action checked through on the flop (A♦ J♣ 5♠). The turn (9♣) gives me a strong semi‑bluff: I now have four clubs (two in hand + J♣ and 9♣ on board) — 9 outs to a flush (~19.6%) plus some extra straight/pair outs. Heads‑up in position I want to apply pressure to fold out ace‑high/jack‑high and other marginal hands while building the pot when I have good equity. Betting also denies a free river. I’ll bet about half the pot to balance fold equity and pot control (200 into 400). Plan: if Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I go to the river — bet for value if I hit the flush, otherwise be prepared to check/call small bets and fold to large river aggression. If he check‑raises a small amount I’ll call for my equity; if he check‑raises big (committing a large portion of stacks) I’ll fold.

On a board of As-Jc-5s-9c, I am holding Queen-high with no draws. Minni checked the flop behind me, which could indicate a lack of an Ace, but her 200-chip bet on the turn shows interest in the pot. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, Queen-high has almost no showdown value and no equity to improve to a winning hand unless I hit a Queen on the river, and even then, I could be behind to a better Queen or any Jack/Ace. Calling here would likely lead to me being forced to fold to a river bet anyway. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a weak hand (94o). In a heads-up situation, while I can occasionally open or complete from the button to put pressure on the big blind, Minni has been playing fairly aggressively and has a larger stack. Folding 9-high offsuit in this position is a standard, low-variance play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 5♥4♦ — a marginal but playable, connected hand in position. With deep stacks and only one opponent, opening wide from the button is profitable: it wins the blind and gives me initiative postflop. I will raise to the minimum allowed (200 total, 2x BB) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (this hand plays poorly out of position vs 3-bets), but if Flash calls I’ll play the flop in position, c-bet favorable textures and check/control dry or heavily overcard boards. This balances steal attempts and risk, maximizing EV over many hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d3d. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. This is a 2x raise, requiring me to call 100 more into a pot of 300. My hand is suited and has some playability post-flop, and the pot odds are 3-to-1. Given the deep stacks and the relatively small raise size, I will defend my big blind and see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d3d. The flop is Ks 9h Qh, which is a very coordinated board that hits a lot of the Button's raising range (broadway cards, pairs, and heart draws). I have no equity here—no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Since I am first to act and have nothing, I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

I opened from the button with 5♥4♦ as a steal; on K♠ 9♥ Q♥ I have no pair and only a single heart (a very weak backdoor). This flop hits the BB’s calling range hard (Kx/Qx/broadways/hearts), and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a large pure bluff. Checking controls the pot, keeps my range from being too bluff-heavy on a board that favors the caller, and lets me see how Flash reacts. Plan: check and evaluate the turn — if Flash checks back I get a free card and can consider a delayed bluff on a very favorable card; if he bets small (a probe ≤ ~25% pot) I may call to see a turn, but I’ll fold to medium/large bets (~50% pot or bigger) since my equity is low.

The turn 6h puts three hearts on a very coordinated board. I have no equity with 8-high and no heart in my hand. While Minni's check on the flop suggests weakness, I have no showdown value and very little fold equity against any pair or draw she might be holding. I will check and plan to fold to any bet.

I opened the button with 5♥4♦ and checked the flop; on the turn the board is K♠ 9♥ Q♥ 6♥ and Flash checked. I only have a one-card heart draw (5♥) — 9 outs to make a flush (~9/46 ≈ 19.6%) and no pair. The pot is tiny (400) and I have position, so betting here would bloat the pot with very little equity and risk wasting chips against calls. Checking controls the pot, keeps my range balanced, and gives me a free river to realize equity or bluff later if a favorable card arrives. If Flash leads on the river I can reevaluate based on size (call small probes, fold to medium/large), if a heart hits I can bet for value, and if a blank comes I can consider a targeted bluff only on an appropriate card. Given the situation, check and evaluate the river is the +EV line.

I have 8-high with no showdown value on a very coordinated board (Ks 9h Qh 6h 5s). Minni checked back both the flop and the turn, which could indicate a weak made hand or a missed draw. While a bet might occasionally force a fold from Ace-high or a weak pocket pair, I have no credible blockers to the flush or the straights, and the pot is small (400). In this situation, the risk of a bluff being called outweighs the potential reward, as Minni likely has at least a better high-card hand. I will check and concede the pot.

I opened from the button with 5♥4♦ and checked the flop and turn on K♠ 9♥ Q♥ 6♥. The 5♠ on the river gives me a very weak pair (pair of 5s). The pot is tiny (400) and Flash has shown passive lines (check-check through); betting here would be thin: better hands (Kx/Qx) that beat me will usually call a small bet, and worse hands that I can extract value from are unlikely to call — so a value bet is low EV. Likewise a bluff is unnecessary given the small pot and opponent’s passive line. I’ll check and go to showdown to realize my equity without building the pot with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with 9s3c. This is a very weak hand, but I only need to call 50 more to see a flop or I can raise. Given the tournament context and the fact that Minni has been quite aggressive and successful in building her stack, I will choose to fold this hand to avoid unnecessary losses with a sub-par starting hand, even in a heads-up situation.

I'm on the button (small blind) in a heads-up pot — optimal strategy is to open-raise very wide and take the initiative. 8♣2♣ is a weak holding but suited and playable in position. Raising to the min (200 total = 2xBB) is a small, standard sizing that maximizes fold equity vs the big blind, keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand, and lets me play the flop in position if called. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold this hand without risking much; if he calls I can leverage position to realize equity or apply further pressure. I prefer raising to limping because it avoids giving cheap odds and keeps the initiative.

In a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind against a min-raise (2x) is standard with a wide range of hands. Q5o is a marginal hand, but given the 3:1 pot odds provided by the small raise, it is a call. Minni has been frequently raising from the button, and folding here would be too exploitable. I will call and look to play post-flop based on board texture.

I am first to act on the flop as the Big Blind. The board of Kd-Tc-6c is quite coordinated and likely favors the pre-flop raiser's range more than mine, as it contains two broadway cards. Holding only Queen-high with no draws, I have very little equity. My strategy here is to check and most likely fold to any significant bet from Minni, as there is no reason to bluff or defend with such a weak holding on this texture.

I was the preflop aggressor and have position; on K♦6♣T♦ with 8♣2♣ I have no pair and only backdoor club potential. Flash checked, so a small continuation bet should pick up equity against his missed portion of the range while keeping the pot manageable if called. I’ll bet ~50% pot (200) to apply pressure; if he folds I take the pot, if he calls I’ll use position to evaluate the turn and proceed cautiously, and I’ll fold to a substantial check-raise or large overbet with this weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind and defended against a small pre-flop raise. The flop is Kd-Td-6c. I have Queen-high with no straight or flush draws. Minni has continued for 1/2 pot (200 into 400). This board texture strongly favors Minni's button-opening range, which includes many Kings, Tens, and diamond draws. With only Queen-high and no real equity to improve, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

In a heads-up situation from the button/SB, King-six offsuit is a standard opening hand. My opponent, Minni, has been playing aggressively and leading the chip count, but K6o is well ahead of an average random range and should be played for a raise to take control of the pot early. A standard 3x open is appropriate here.

I’m heads-up in the big blind with A♥Q♦ facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). Button opens here a lot and has a wide range: many broadways, suited connectors and medium pairs. AQo is well ahead of that range and is a clear 3-bet candidate — I want to take the initiative, get fold equity, and build a pot with a strong hand. I’ll 3-bet to 900 (≈3x the open): big enough to pressure weaker holdings and isolate, but still keeps post‑flop playability with these deep stacks. If Flash 4-bets very large I’ll re-evaluate (potentially fold to an overcommitment or call a reasonable-sized 4-bet depending on sizing/read).

I opened to 300 with K6o, a standard heads-up button play. Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 900. While Minni is aggressive, K6o is quite weak against a 3-betting range and does not play well post-flop, as it is frequently dominated by better Kings (K8+) or Aces. Given that I am currently out-stacked and Minni has shown a tendency to apply significant post-flop pressure, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than calling a 3-bet with a marginal hand.

Heads-up on the button with 7♣8♥ — a strong, playable connected hand in position. With pot 150 and only 50 to call, the best line is to seize the initiative and raise to the min (200 total). A 2x raise (to 200) applies pressure to the BB, maximizes fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (245 BB effective vs 153 BB). If Flash folds I take down the 150. If he calls I’ll have position to c-bet favorable flops or realize equity on wet boards. If he 3-bets small/standard I’ll likely call and play postflop in position; if he 3-bets extremely large I’ll re-evaluate and lean toward folding to avoid bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 52o, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has raised to 200. Although it only costs me 100 more to call (providing 3:1 pot odds), 52o realizes its equity very poorly, especially out of position. Given Minni's persistent aggression, I expect a continuation bet on most flops, making it unprofitable to peel with such junk. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Eight offsuit is well within the range of hands to open from the Button/Small Blind. My goal is to maintain pressure and not become too predictable by folding marginal hands. Minni has been quite aggressive, but a standard raise to 300 allows me to take control of the pot or see how they respond pre-flop.

I’m on the big blind with 7♥2♥ facing a 3x open to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 to continue (total 300). 7-2 suited is one of the weakest hands: very low high-card value, frequently dominated, and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Although the call is cheap (pot odds ≈33%), my realized equity OOP and lack of strong implied odds make this a negative-EV spot long-term. 3-betting as a bluff could be considered only with a clear read that the opponent folds too often — I don’t have that read. With deep stacks I can pick better, more playable spots to invest chips. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with 4♦7♠ — a weak hand but playable by position. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 to complete. Against one opponent I want to be the aggressor here to steal the blind and leverage postflop position. Table sizing and my history show 300 is a standard open from me, and it gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal holding. Plan: raise to 300 total. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and give up on highly uncooperative boards.

I am in the Big Blind holding 74s. Minni has opened for a 3x raise to 300. While 74s is a suited hand, it is a weak two-gapper that plays poorly out of position against an aggressive opponent who currently holds a significant chip lead. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, the post-flop playability of this hand is low, and I have been frequently forced to fold to Minni's continuation bets in previous hands. I will wait for a better spot to defend my blind or take a stand.

In a heads-up situation, K2o is a hand well within the top 70-80% of hands, making it a standard open from the button/small blind. My goal is to maintain aggression and leverage my position. A standard raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing and should effectively test the big blind's range.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300 — a standard, likely wide heads-up raise. I have JhTd offsuit: a strong, connected broadway hand that does well vs a wide opening range and has good multi-street playability. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call, giving me ~33% pot odds; against a typical SB open my equity with JTo comfortably justifies a call. I'm out of position (BB), so I want to keep the pot controllable and see a flop rather than make a tiny 3-bet to 500 (which buys little fold equity) or a large 3-bet that bloats the pot with a marginal hand. With deep stacks (~150BB effective) there are good implied odds to realize. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — convert to aggression if I hit (pair/top pair/strong draw), fold or proceed cautiously if I miss and face heavy action.

I’m out of position with JhTd on a paired, mildly wet flop (7c 6s 7s). I have no pair and no meaningful draw (no spade, no straight draw), so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or bluff into the preflop raiser who just checked (could be weak or trapping with a set/pair). With deep stacks there’s good implied odds to realize if I hit a J or T later. Plan: control the pot and see a free turn — check. If I pick up a pair/draw on the turn I can press; otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression.

On a paired board of 7c-6s-7s, King-high has some showdown value but very little equity if called after a continuation bet. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture can occasionally hit a Big Blind's defending range (containing more 7x, 6x, and middle connectors like 54 or 85). By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my showdown value, and avoid being forced off the hand by a check-raise or a lead on the turn. This also allows me to see how the board develops for free.

I have JhTd on 7c-6s-7s-5h and missed every meaningful connection — no pair, no spade, no straight draw. We're out of position heads-up vs a player who opened wide preflop and then checked the flop, so betting the turn is a pure bluff with very little fold equity and a high chance of being called by worse pairs or floats. With ~150BB effective I want to control the pot and preserve fold equity for later streets where I can actually improve. Plan: check and go to the river; if Flash fires a small probe I may fold or selectively float depending on sizing, but I won't bloat the pot OOP with air now.

On a board of 7c-6s-7s-5h, my King-high still retains reasonable showdown value against an opponent's air. Minni has checked twice, which often indicates weakness, but the board texture is becoming increasingly coordinated with straight possibilities (like 43 or 89). By checking back again, I continue to control the size of the pot and ensure I reach the river for free, where I can reassess the strength of my hand relative to the final board. Betting here risks a check-raise or being called by a hand that is currently beating me (like a small pair or a 6), whereas checking allows me to realize my equity.

I have J♥T♦ on 7c-6s-7s-5h-Ks and both players checked flop and turn. I missed every meaningful connection and hold no spade or K blockers; my hand has virtually no showdown value and offers very little fold equity versus a preflop opener who can profitably call small river bluffs with any pair. The pot is tiny (600) relative to effective stacks, so bluffing here would risk chips for a small upside and is likely negative EV against this opponent’s range. I’ll preserve stack and not turn air into a pot by betting out of position — check and see the showdown (or induce a river check back).

On the river, the King of Spades (Ks) completes the board (7c-6s-7s-5h-Ks), giving me top pair with my King of Clubs (Kc). Minni has checked on every street (flop, turn, and river), which strongly suggests she does not have a Seven or a straight (89 or 43). By checking the flop and turn, I controlled the pot size and masked the strength of my hand. Now that I have improved to top pair, I have a clear value bet. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to extract value from any 6x, 5x, or Ace-high hands that Minni might be holding and be willing to call a single bet with.

I checked flop and turn to control the pot with J♥T♦ because I had no pair/flush/straight draw. The river K♠ changes things: it gives Flash obvious thin value targets (Kx) and also completes a 3‑spade board so any two‑spade holdings now make a flush. Flash’s small river bet (400 into a 1000 pot) is the size you’d use for thin value or to extract from missed hands — I have almost no showdown value and I don’t hold K or spade blockers. Calling 400 requires ~28.6% equity, which JTo is very unlikely to have versus the realistic value portion of his range. Raising would be a pure bluff with little fold equity and poor blocker coverage. With deep stacks I’d rather preserve chips for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB). I hold 3♥6♠ — a weak, unsuited hand. The price to see the flop is only 50 into a 150 pot, giving very favorable pot odds, and I have position postflop. Rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand, I’ll take the cheap option to realize equity and keep my options open. If the BB 3-bets I’ll fold; if we see a flop I’ll proceed cautiously and only barrel if I pick up equity or the board is favorable.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the Button (Small Blind), has limped in. While Q9o is a respectable hand in heads-up play, Minni will have position on me for the remainder of the hand. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position. I'll take the free flop.

On this flop of 10s-5d-6d, I have Queen-high with no direct draw. In a limped pot, I am out of position against Minni. Checking is the most prudent action here to see how Minni reacts. I have some backdoor potential, but I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that currently has no pair and no draw.

I have middle pair (6s) with a weak 3 kicker on a Ts-5d-6d flop. Heads-up the BB checked, so his range is wide (many missed overcards, some pocket pairs, occasional T or slowplayed strong hands). Betting a small-sized bet (half-pot) achieves multiple things: extracts value from worse hands (5x, weaker pairs, some Ax/Kx), folds out a lot of pure air/overcards, and denies free cards that could outdraw my pair. It also keeps the initiative and gives info if he reacts strongly (I can re-evaluate if he check-raises). I’ll make a half-pot bet of 100 chips.

On this flop of Ts-5d-6d, I have Queen-high with no pair and no real draw. Minni bet 100 into a 200 pot. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, I am out of position and have very little equity on this board. Without a diamond in my hand or a straight draw, there are very few turn cards that improve my hand significantly or allow me to take the lead comfortably. Continuing with air against an aggressive opponent like Minni is likely to result in further losses on later streets. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
In a heads-up situation, Queen-Nine offsuit is a strong hand that should be played aggressively from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the big blind.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with A♥9♥ — a strong heads-up hand with both nut-flush potential and good top-pair upside. It’s only 200 to call into a 400 pot (~25% break-even equity), and against a wide button opening range A9s has solid equity and plays well postflop. I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot without a stronger read; a small 3-bet is an option, but flat-calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me realize equity cheaply. I’ll call and re-evaluate on the flop (look to bet/raise when I hit A or a big heart draw, pot-control or fold when I miss).

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (A♥9♥ on 3♥-9♣-8♦) against a wide button opening range — a very strong heads-up holding. The flop is fairly dry but does contain straight/connector possibilities, so I want to charge draws and get value from worse pairs and many broadway/ace hands. Betting about half the pot (~300 into 600) accomplishes that: it extracts from 9x, 8x, A‑x and draws, denies free cards, and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: if Flash calls I will reassess on the turn (value-bet/cc on A/9 or slow down on blanks); if he raises strongly I’ll give credit to sets/two‑pair and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a 9-8-3 rainbow board. Minni has led into me for 300 (a donk bet) after I was the pre-flop aggressor. This bet could represent a draw (like 7-6 or J-10), a middle pair, or a weaker 9. While I could raise for protection and value, calling allows me to keep Minni's bluffs in her range and keep the pot manageable while I have a strong but not invulnerable hand. I will call and see how the turn develops.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (A♥9♥) on 3♥–9♣–8♦–2♦. The turn is a relatively harmless blank for my hand but it does pair the board with a 2♦ and brings two diamonds on board, so there are some completed diamond draws and a few two‑pair/set combinations that can beat me. Flash called my flop bet from a wide button opening range, so his calling range still contains many worse pairs (8x, 9x with worse kickers), A‑x, and some draws. I want to charge those hands and deny free river cards while keeping the pot manageable out of position. Betting ~half the pot (~600 into 1200) accomplishes that: it gets value from worse pairs and A‑x, prices out some equity from diamond draws, and keeps weaker holdings and bluffs in. Plan: if Flash folds we take it; if he calls we re‑evaluate on the river and size our river decision based on his tendencies and the river card; if he raises large (polarized sizing) I’ll give credit to two‑pair/sets and fold, while a small raise may be a bluff/overcall that I can continue against.

I have top pair with a strong kicker on a relatively safe board. Minni has led out again on the turn after leading the flop. While her line is aggressive, my hand is too strong to fold to a half-pot bet. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. Raising might scare off her bluffs or worse 9s, so I will call to keep her range wide and evaluate the river. The 2d is a blank that doesn't significantly help her range unless she has exactly pocket deuces.

I have A♥9♥ — top pair with the best kicker. The river (3♥ 9♣ 8♦ 2♦ 8♣) pairs the board with an 8, which does create trip/full‑house candidates (any 8 or a pocket 9 is now very strong), but those hands likely would have shown more aggression earlier. Flash has been check‑calling my flop and turn bets, a line consistent with one‑pair hands, A‑x, or missed draws that will call a modest river price. I want thin value and to charge worse pairs/A‑high hands while keeping the pot from getting bloated against the polarized part of his range. Bet about half the pot (1200 into 2400) — big enough to extract from K9, other 9x with worse kickers, A‑x and some pocket pairs, but small enough to fold to a large raise that represents a full house/quads.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker. The board has paired the 8 on the river, but there are no completed flushes and the most likely straight draws (7-6, J-10) missed. Minni has led into me on every street for 50% of the pot. While this line could represent an 8 or a better 9 (A9, K9), it could also be a missed draw or a weaker 9. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call (1200 to win a total pot of 4800). Given the heads-up dynamics and the strength of my top pair, folding is too conservative, but raising would only be called by hands that beat me. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button I have position after the flop, so I can open a wide range. 9♠5♣ is weak but playable in position and a raise will often win the blinds or let me play the flop with the initiative. The table’s normal open size has been around 300, and that sizing is tiny vs our deep stacks (I have 26,800; Flash 13,050). I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so +250) to apply pressure. Plan: if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold — this hand has poor equity vs a polarized 3-bet — but if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate on dry or dangerous boards.

I am in the Big Blind with K9o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. With the pot at 400 and needing 200 to call, I am getting 3:1 odds. K9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Button open in a heads-up situation. I will call and see a flop.

On this low, somewhat disconnected board of 3-7-5 with two spades, my King-high (K9o) hasn't improved but still holds some showdown value against her bluffs. As the defender from the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will see how Minni reacts to the board before deciding whether to continue.

Heads-up I opened preflop and got called; on 3♦ 7♠ 5♠ I hold 9♠5♣ — a pair of fives with a decent 9 kicker and a backdoor spade possibility. Flash checked, so I should take the initiative and c-bet to get value from worse hands and fold out many unpaired overcards and some weak holdings. The board is relatively dry but can be vulnerable to two-card spade draws; I’ll use a small, efficient sizing (~1/3 pot = 200) to keep the pot controllable if he applies pressure. Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; if he check-raises big I’ll fold most of the time since my pair is marginal against sets/two-pair/strong spade combos.

I have King-high with two overcards on a low board. Minni's continuation bet is small (200 into 600), giving me 4:1 odds to call. Given her aggressive play and the wide range she likely opens from the button, King-high still has significant equity, and catching a King or a Nine on the turn could put me in a very strong position. Folding here would be too exploitable given the price.

On the turn, a Queen of Hearts has appeared, which is an overcard to the existing board and likely favors the range of the pre-flop aggressor. As I am out of position with King-high and no significant draw, leading out into the aggressor is not advisable. My hand has some marginal showdown value against her air or missed draws, but it is not strong enough to value bet. I will check to see how Minni responds to this card and evaluate the strength of her range based on her bet sizing if she decides to continue her aggression.

I hold 9♠5♣ on 3♦-7♠-5♠-Q♥: a marginal one-pair hand (pair of fives) with a weak kicker and only a backdoor flush possibility. Flash called my small flop c-bet, so his range heads-up includes many hands that now beat me (Qx, 7x, sets, medium pairs) plus some floats and single-spade holdings. The queen on the turn is a scare card for my line and betting here would bloat the pot with little fold equity — I’ll often get called by better and only fold out the weakest holdings. With position and a vulnerable made hand, pot control is the prudent line: check the turn, keep the pot manageable, and let him bluff or show weakness on the river. Plan: if he bets the river small I’ll call lighter for value/catch bluffs; if he fires a large, polarized bet I’ll fold.

The river is a 6h, making the final board 3d-7s-5s-Qh-6h. On the turn, the action went check-check, suggesting Minni likely has a marginal hand with some showdown value or a missed draw. While my King-high (K9o) is weak, I have checked to her to see if she wants to check back and go to showdown or try a late bluff. Betting here as a bluff is risky because she might call with any small pair (7x, 5x, or 6x) given the pot size and her position. I will check and likely fold if she puts in a significant bet, as my hand is unlikely to be the winner here.

I have a marginal one-pair (5s) with a weak kicker on 3♦-7♠-5♠-Q♥-6♥. Opponent called my small flop c-bet, then checked turn and checked the river — a line that contains many hands that now beat me (Qx, 7x, sets, some two-pair/overpairs) as well as some floats and weak pairs. Betting here has very little fold equity versus better hands and will only bloat the pot when I have thin value; checking preserves pot control and realizes my showdown equity. Plan: check to showdown; if Flash fires a small river bet I will call for value/light bluffs, but fold to a large polarized shove. Current action: check.

I have been dealt pocket Sevens (7s 7d) on the Button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, which has been a consistent pattern in our previous hands.

Heads-up vs the Button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Js6h — a marginal but defensible hand in heads-up play. Calling 200 (300 total) gets me good pot odds (I need ~33% equity) versus a wide opening range from the button. 3-betting out of position with an offsuit J is unnecessary and too often leaves me OOP vs stronger hands; folding is too tight and surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to see the flop and continue only with favorable boards (jack/6, strong draws, or backdoor spade); otherwise play cautiously and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

I flopped a pair of sixes (6h) with Js6h on 2h‑5h‑6c heads‑up. The button opened wide preflop and checked the flop, so his range contains lots of missed overcards, weak one‑pair hands and some draws. Betting protects my middle pair against 3/4 straight and two‑heart combos and extracts value from worse pairs and Ax/Kx hands that will call. A half‑pot bet is a good balance — large enough to charge draws but small enough to get called — so I’ll bet 300 into the 600 pot. If he raises substantially I’ll proceed cautiously given my OOP middle pair.

I have pocket sevens, which is an overpair to this low, coordinated board of 2-5-6. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into the 600 pot. This bet often represents a marginal hand like a pair of sixes, a pair of fives, or a straight/flush draw. I want to raise here to protect my overpair against the various draws (hearts, any 3, 4, 7, or 8) and to extract value from her weaker made hands. Raising to 900 total (a 600 raise over her 300) is a standard value-building and protection play in this spot.

I opened the pot by betting 300 on the flop with Js6h to protect my middle pair and charge draws. Flash’s check‑raise to 900 is large and polarizing in this heads‑up spot — it often represents strong value (sets: 22/55/66, single‑6 combos, or very strong two‑pair/trips-style hands) and is less consistent with pure multi‑street bluffs on this fairly dry 2♥‑5♥‑6♣ board. I’m out of position, my kicker is weak, and I have only limited backdoor outs; while the pot odds to call (~25% required equity) are tempting, my read and the sizing indicate I’m likely behind. Preserving a big stack in position for better spots is the correct long‑term play here, so I’ll fold and concede the small bet rather than commit more chips to a marginal hand.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 6♦5♥ — a speculative, well-connected hand that plays much better postflop in position than as a preflop aggressor. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call; calling gives ~4:1 immediate pot odds and keeps my range wide while preserving stack depth. Flash has shown aggression in earlier hands, so a min-raise to 200 would risk a 3‑bet and force a difficult decision with a marginal hand. I will call the 50 (total commit 100) and evaluate the flop: continue aggressively when I make a pair/two‑pair/strong draw, use position to bluff selectively, and check‑fold to large pressure on dry, overcard flops; fold to a sizable preflop 3‑bet unless pot odds/implied odds justify calling.

In Hand #83, I am in the Big Blind with 9h 6s. Minni (Button/Small Blind) has just limped in (called 50). This is a heads-up situation. My hand is a weak offsuit connector. While I could raise to try to win the pot immediately, Minni has been quite active and has a larger stack. Given the low stakes of the pot (200) and the nature of my hand, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play to conserve my stack and potentially hit a favorable board.

I have flopped trips with a 6 kicker on an Ace-high paired board (9c 9s Ac). Since the pot is small (200) and I am out of position against Minni, I will check to disguise the strength of my hand. This allows Minni to potentially bluff or bet if she has an Ace or a smaller pair, building a pot I can extract value from on later streets.

Heads‑up on the button with 6♦5♥ on 9c 9s Ac — I have no pair and no meaningful draw, so my only realistic chance to win this pot is fold equity. Flash checked the flop, which can be either a missed hand or a trap with an Ace/9, but in heads‑up play a small continuation bet will pick off a large portion of his missed range (Kx, Qx, small pairs, air). I’ll make a half‑pot bluff (100) to apply pressure while keeping the pot small if I’m called. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll pot‑control on the turn and fold to significant aggression unless I improve. If he raises, I fold — I don’t want to bloat a pot with air.

I have flopped trips with a 9, which is a very strong hand. Since Minni limped pre-flop, her range is quite wide. Her bet of 100 into a 200 pot on the flop could represent an Ace, a flush draw, or a pure bluff. If I raise now, I risk folding out her air or weaker holdings. By calling, I keep her range wide and disguise the strength of my hand, allowing her to potentially commit more chips on the turn if she has a marginal hand or decides to continue a bluff.

I currently hold trips with a 9 on a board of 9c-9s-Ac-2h. My kicker (6) is relatively weak, but trips are a very strong hand in a heads-up limped pot. I checked and called a small bet on the flop to keep Minni's range wide and disguise my strength. On the turn, the 2h is a blank. I will check again to induce another bet from Minni, who has shown aggression in previous hands. If she has an Ace or is bluffing, she is likely to bet, allowing me to either call again to trap or check-raise to build the pot. Checking remains the best way to extract value from her weaker holdings.

I have 6♦5♥ on 9c 9s Ac 2h — no pair and no meaningful draw. I c‑bet the flop and was called, so Flash’s continuing range likely contains Ax, pocket 9s, and some small pairs or K/Q type hands; the 2h on the turn doesn’t change much. My prior plan was to pot‑control on the turn and fold to significant aggression unless I improve, so I’ll check to keep the pot small (400) and avoid bloat with a pure bluff here. If Flash leads into me I’ll fold to large bets (or call very small probes rarely); if he checks back I’ll evaluate the river for a targeted bluff or a fold depending on the runout. Preserves stack and keeps my options open for the river.
