I have trips (Nines) on a board of 9-9-A-2-7. After checking and calling a small bet on the flop, and the turn going check-check, I am highly confident I have the best hand. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely holds a weak Ace, a small pair, or was bluffing. I will lead out with a value bet of 300 into the 400 pot to extract value from her marginal holdings. Checking again would likely result in her checking behind, which misses an opportunity to increase my stack.

Flash leads 300 into a 700 pot on the river after he called my 100 flop c‑bet and both of us checked the turn. To call 300 I’d need ~23% equity (call 300 to win 1000). With 6♦5♥ I have no pair, no meaningful draw and virtually zero showdown value — I only beat very thin, unlikely river bluffs. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, lead river) is much more consistent with value (Ax, pocket 9s, or small pairs turned into a value probe) than with a high enough bluff frequency to make a call +EV. Raising would be reckless with no showdown chops. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold this marginal situation.

I am on the button with 75s, a solid speculative hand in heads-up play. Opening for a standard 3x raise is the professional approach to maintain pressure and build the pot with a hand that has good playability post-flop.

Call to 300. Facing a 3x button open heads-up, K♥4♦ is marginal but has reasonable equity against a wide raising range and the call is cheap (pot ~400, need 200 more — ~2:1). Folding is too nitty and 3-betting OOP with this hand is a weak bluff. Plan: play cautiously postflop — check and fold to heavy pressure on dry boards unless I hit a pair or strong draw; be more aggressive if I pair the K or pick up a 4 or a profitable draw.

Heads-up on Ad Kd 3s I have K♥4♦ — second pair but a weak kicker. Flash opened preflop so his range is wide (Ax, Kx, broadways, pockets, some suited connectors). The board has two diamonds, so there are plausible flush draws and combos that will outdraw me, and plenty of worse Kx and medium pairs I can get value from. Checking lets him take the lead and gives free cards to his draws; a small-to-medium c-bet protects my hand and extracts value. Bet sizing: half-pot (300 into 600) prices out many draws, gets called by worse kings/pocket pairs and some floats, and keeps the pot manageable if he shows strength. If he raises big I’ll assume a strong Ace/set and fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

On a flop of Ad-Kd-3s, Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into the 600 pot. My hand, 7s5s, has virtually no equity on this board and no real draws. While an A-K high board often favors the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's lead indicates she likely has a piece of the board (a King, a weak Ace, or a 3) or is comfortable defending this texture. Bluffs on such a static, high-card board against a lead are low-frequency and high-risk. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button with KK — almost an automatic raise for value and to isolate the lone big blind. Flash is a frequent raiser/caller, so 3xBB (raise to 300 total) is standard here: it extracts value from A-x, K-x and medium pairs, discourages random flats, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Flash merely calls I’ll play the flop aggressively; if he 3-bets I’ll evaluate size and likely 4-bet or call depending on the sizing. Raise to 300 total chips.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding K-2 offsuit in the big blind. My opponent, Minni, has opened with a standard raise to 3x the big blind. Although K-2 is a relatively weak hand, it possesses 'King-high' value which is significant in heads-up play. Given that I have over 13,000 chips and the pot is 400, calling the 200 additional chips is a reasonable defensive play to see a flop, as folding would be too tight and raising with a weak deuce kicker is unnecessarily risky against an opponent who has been fairly active and aggressive.

On this Ten-high flop of Tc-5h-6c, my King-high (Kc-2d) has missed completely. In heads-up play, the standard move is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I don't have enough equity to lead out or check-call a substantial bet, so I will check and likely fold if Minni puts out a standard continuation bet.

Heads-up on a very safe flop — Tc 5h 6c — and I hold KK: a clean overpair. Flash checked, so I should size for value and to charge any straight/club draws (7-8 type straight draws, or two clubs in hand). Flash is a frequent raiser/caller and will call with worse Ks, medium pairs, and Ax hands, so a firm c-bet extracts value while denying free equity. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to get value and protect (keeps weaker hands and many draws paying to chase). If he raises I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (small raise -> call and see turn; large shove -> consider range and fold only to very polarized lines). Bet 400 (≈2/3 pot).

I missed the flop completely with King-high. The board is relatively coordinated (Tc-5h-6c), offering straight and flush possibilities that likely hit a wide button-opening range. My opponent, Minni, has been active with continuation bets, and 400 into 600 is a significant bet indicating strength or a standard high-frequency bluff that I cannot profitably call with just King-high and no real draws. I will fold and preserve my stack.

In heads-up play, the button (SB) should be opening a wide range of hands to maintain pressure. 6h 7d is a reasonable hand to open with. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes, to see how Minni responds from the big blind.

Heads-up vs a button opener I should defend very wide. Td3d is a playable, suited hand with backdoor straight/flush potential and reasonable postflop playability. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200, so calling gives me 600 total in the pot (200 investment) — about 3:1 pot odds (~33% equity required). With deep stacks and an aggressive opponent who opens wide from the button, I prefer to call and see a flop rather than 3-bet with this marginal hand or fold and lose good implied odds. Plan: play cautiously out of position postflop — continue with middle/strong draws and pairs, fold to heavy action when completely missed, and use position to exercise pot control.

I hold Td3d on a 3s-7h-Ts flop — that’s two pair (Tens and Threes) using both hole cards. Heads-up vs a wide button opener this is a very strong made hand; I want to extract value and deny free cards to any backdoor spade combos or single-card straight/overcard draws. The pot is 600, so I’ll lead for ~2/3 pot (400) to get called by worse Tens, 7x, and floats while protecting my hand. If called I’ll re-evaluate turn texture (bet again for value on safe cards, control or pot-control on dangerous runouts). If I face a large check-raise I’ll strongly consider calling given my two pair but will fold to extreme overbets or if the board runs out very scary.

I have middle pair (7s) on a 10-7-3 board. Minni has led out for 400 into a 600 pot. In heads-up play, middle pair is a solid bluff-catcher and strong enough to continue against a lead. Raising might overplay the hand if Minni has a Ten or a better Seven, so I will call to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn develops.

I have two pair (Tens and Threes) heads-up vs a wide button opener who called my flop lead. The turn 9d is a relatively safe card — it adds some straight combos (J8, 86) but doesn’t complete a diamond or a spade flush by itself. With deep stacks I want to extract value from worse Tens, 7x hands and charge any single-card spade/straight draws, so I’ll continue barreling for value/protection. The pot is 1400, so a bet around two‑thirds pot (~900) is a good balance: it gets called by worse hands and prices out too many free cards. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll re‑evaluate (lean to calling medium raises with two pair, fold to extreme overbets or very polarized action).

On the turn, the board is 3s-7h-Ts-9d. My pair of sevens, which was middle pair on the flop, has now been demoted to third pair with the arrival of the Nine. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn (donk betting), which often indicates a made hand or a strong draw in heads-up play. Given that the board is becoming increasingly connected (7-9-10) and there is a spade flush draw present, my hand (7d-6h) is very vulnerable. Minni's bet of 900 into a 1400 pot is significant (approx. 64% pot), and calling here would likely lead to an even more difficult decision on the river. To preserve my stack and wait for a better spot, I will fold.

AQo on the button (heads-up) is a clear open-raise — strong hand with position postflop and good equity against a wide BB calling/defending range. I’ll raise to the minimum legal open (200 total) to seize the initiative, fold out weaker hands and keep the pot controllable with deep stacks (we still have maneuverability if Flash 3-bets). Postflop plan: c-bet most A/Q-high and dry textures, exercise pot control or check back on very wet/connected boards, and evaluate any 3-bet by sizing and range (call smaller 3-bets, fold or proceed cautiously vs very large/committed 4-bets).

Hand #87: I am in the big blind holding 8s 4h. Minni, who is currently the chip leader, has opened with a 2x raise to 200. Although I am getting 4:1 odds to call (100 to win a total pot of 400), this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given my current stack size and Minni's aggressive post-flop play, it is better to fold junk like this and wait for better opportunities to defend my blind or initiate a pot.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-four suited is a premium hand. I'm on the button and should exert pressure by raising. A standard 3x open to 300 chips is consistent with my previous play and allows me to build a pot with a hand that has significant equity and playability.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 (SB) — he will have a very wide raising range. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (total 300 including my 100 BB), so my immediate pot odds are ~33% (200/(400+200)). 6h8c is a marginal but playable hand heads-up with decent straight-upside vs a wide opening range and it’s low-risk to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet light out of position and bloat a pot OOP with a mediocre hand, so I’ll call and evaluate the flop — continue aggressively if I connect (pair or good draw), otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy pressure.

Check. I have 6♡8♣ on a paired 7♠7♦K♠ board and no made hand or meaningful draw — very little showdown equity versus the SB opener’s wide but continuing-heavy range (Kx, any 7, many pairs). I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with air or give them easy bluffs to call; pot is 600 and there’s no bet to face, so pot-control is the prudent play. Plan: check and see how the opener reacts — call small continuation bets to see a turn when pot odds justify it or fold to large pressure; only start bluffing/raising if I pick up equity or clear blockers on later streets.

On a paired board of 7s-7d-Ks, I have the nut flush draw with my As4s. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage here, as my range contains more Kings and high-pocket pairs than Minni's defending range. Minni has checked to me, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure, capitalize on my range advantage, and build the pot in case I hit my flush or Ace on future streets.

Call 300. Heads-up the button opened very wide so his c-bet on 7♠7♦K♠ will include a lot of air, but I have no pair or meaningful draw with 6♥8♣. The flop bet is 300 into a 900 pot, so calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds. My raw chance to pair a 6 or 8 by the river is ~24% (and real usable equity is a bit less because many villain holdings (Kx, 7x) still beat those pairs). Raising as a bluff OOP with no blockers is thin and will bloat a pot I’m likely behind in; folding outright surrenders potential edge vs a wide c-bet. I’ll take the inexpensive look: call to keep the pot manageable, re-evaluate on the turn, and concede to heavy turn pressure unless I hit a pair or pick up a clear equity-improving card (then turn/river play aggressively).

Check. Pot is 1,200 and I have 6♥8♣ on 7♠7♦K♠T♥ — no pair and only 6 outs (three sixes + three eights) to hit by the river (~13% raw equity). I’m out of position versus a very wide opener who has shown aggression; betting OOP with air here just bloats the pot and puts me in difficult spots. Pot-control is correct: check and fold to meaningful turn aggression. Pure pot-odds math says I could only profitably call very small bets (≈≤180 chips) given my ~13% equity; anything larger I should fold. If Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and only continue if I actually improve or get a clear bluffing opportunity with good blockers.

On the turn, the board is 7s-7d-Ks-Th. I still hold the nut flush draw with my As4s. Minni checked for the second time, which could indicate weakness or a pot-control move with a King or a 7. While I could continue betting as a semi-bluff, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see the river without facing a potential check-raise that would force me off my draw. If a spade or an Ace hits the river, I will be in a strong position to value bet or call.

I never improved — 6♥8♣ on 7♠7♦K♠T♥2♦ has no pair and no meaningful blockers. Opponent opened wide and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, so his line is consistent with Kx, some slow‑played pair/7x, or missed air. With the river completed I have essentially no showdown value; a profitable bluff would require substantial fold equity (half‑pot ~33% folding, pot‑size ~50%+) which I don’t trust against this opponent given his tendencies to call down. I also have a healthy stack and no need to gamble chips in a marginal spot. Plan: check and fold to a value river bet — only bluff here if I had clear blockers or a much better read that he folds enough. If he checks back we’ll see showdown (likely lose but preserve stack discipline).

On this board of 7s-7d-Ks-Th-2d, my nut flush draw has missed, leaving me with Ace-high. Minni has checked to me for the third time. While Ace-high has some showdown value against completely missed hands like QJ or lower spade draws, it loses to any pair. However, Minni's line of check-calling the flop and then checking the turn and river suggests either a weak made hand (like a small pair or a Ten) or a complete miss. Given that she called a flop bet on a paired board with a King, she is unlikely to fold a pair to a single river bet now. I will check back to realize my showdown value and avoid losing more chips in a spot where a bluff is unlikely to succeed against her sticky defending range.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should open a very wide range and take the initiative. 5♥8♦ is marginal but has enough playability in position to raise for fold equity and to see a flop with the lead. A min-raise to 200 (2x BB) is a standard, low-variance heads-up open: it pressures the BB, keeps the pot manageable if called, and preserves most of my deep stack if Flash 3-bets big. Plan postflop: c-bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously vs heavy aggression and fold to large 3-bets with this hand.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Minni has raised to 200, requiring me to call 100 more. While the pot odds are 3:1, Q2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against an opponent who has been consistently aggressive. I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity or a hand with better post-flop playability to defend my blind.

I am holding King of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation. While K2o is a marginal hand, in heads-up play, any King has significant value and is generally an opening hand from the Button. My opponent, Minni, has been playing aggressively, but I need to maintain my own pressure to prevent being blinded down, especially given the current stack disparity. I will put in a standard 3x raise to take control of the pot pre-flop. If Minni re-raises, I can easily fold this marginal hand, but a raise here allows me to win the pot immediately or see a flop with the lead.

Heads-up I’m facing a standard button open to 300 with 8♦2♠ — a very weak, poorly connected offsuit hand and out of position postflop. The extra 200 to call into a 400 pot gives me ~33% pot‑odds, and against a wide SB/button opening range my equity is roughly in that neighborhood. I don’t want to 3‑bet with pure garbage (too easy to get committed OOP or face a shove), so I’ll take the cheap look. Plan: call 200 more (300 total), see the flop and fold to significant continuation‑bet pressure unless I connect (pair or a real draw).

I called preflop with 8♦2♠ intending to fold to a meaningful c‑bet unless I connected. I didn’t hit — flop K♠9♠3♥ gives me no pair and only a double‑runner spade possibility (need both turn+river spades; ~4% to make). Flash checked the flop, which can be weakness but also a trap with Kx. OOP with essentially air I can’t profitably barre as a standard bluff against a wide but often calling/raising button range. With deep stacks and many hands left I’ll preserve chips: check and see the turn. Plan to fold to a real bet unless I pick up a pair or a legitimate draw.

I have flopped top pair with King of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds on a King-Nine-Trey board. While my kicker is weak, top pair in a heads-up pot is a very strong hand and should be played for value. There are two spades on the board, so I need to bet to protect against potential flush draws and to extract value from weaker pairs (like a 9 or a 3) or straight draws (like QJ or JT). Minni has checked to me, so I will make a standard continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to maintain initiative and build the pot.

Out of position with 8♦2♠ on K♠9♠3♥. I checked the flop planning to fold to a meaningful c-bet unless I improved. Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot — calling requires ~25% equity. I have no pair and only a tiny runner‑runner spade chance (~4%) plus weak pair outs that will often be dominated by Kx or better. Against an opener who raises a lot but still has plenty of Kx/broadway value, my true equity is well below the needed 25%. OOP with essentially air, no realistic fold equity and plenty of tournament left, so preserve chips and fold.

Heads-up on the button with A♣2♥ — a hand worth opening for value and fold equity. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is passive; I want to seize initiative and play postflop in position. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB): this sizing gives solid fold equity against a wide BB, isolates Flash, and builds a manageable pot (if he calls the pot will be ~600). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal A‑2o; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet or realize my equity. Raising to 300 balances pressure and postflop maneuverability.

I am holding 2d 3h in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, the hand has extremely poor post-flop playability and equity. Given that I am currently trailing in chips and Minni has been playing aggressively, I should preserve my stack and wait for a better hand to engage with. Defending 23o out of position is generally a losing strategy.

I have a strong pocket pair (99) on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand that should be played aggressively for value. I will raise to my standard 3x open of 300 to build the pot and maintain initiative.

Heads‑up vs Button open to 300. Q♦8♥ is marginal but defendable from the big blind — calling 200 (total 300 committed) gives good pot odds against a wide opening range and lets me play postflop for value and to exploit bluffs. I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with Q8o (the min‑raise to 500 has little fold equity and larger 3‑bets bloat a pot I’d prefer to keep manageable OOP). Folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call and reassess on the flop.

Heads‑up on a dryish Q‑3‑7 with two diamonds. I hold Q♦8♥ — top pair but a weak kicker and I’m OOP, so I want value and to deny free cards. Button opened wide preflop, so much of his range will call a modest c‑bet (we beat worse queens, many Kx/Ax, small pairs and floating hands). A half‑pot continuation bet (~300 into 600) extracts value while keeping the pot controllable if he raises. If he raises large I can re‑evaluate (likely fold to a very large sizing; call smaller raises), and if he calls I’ll see the turn and plan accordingly. Bet 300.

In this heads-up situation, I have an overpair to the low cards on the board (7 and 3), with only one overcard (the Queen) present. Minni has decided to lead out (donk bet) for half the pot. While this could represent a Queen, it frequently represents a middle pair, a draw (like the diamond flush draw), or a tactical lead to prevent me from continuation betting. Folding is too weak given the strength of pocket nines. Raising might bloat the pot and force out hands that I am currently beating, while potentially getting me into trouble if she holds a Queen. Calling allows me to control the pot size, evaluate the turn, and keep her range wide, including her bluffs and weaker pairs.

I have top pair (Q) but a weak 8 kicker and I’m out of position. Opponent called my flop c‑bet, so his range contains worse queens, medium/small pairs, floats/Ax/Kx and some two‑diamond combos with a flush draw. The turn (3c) pairs the board which introduces the possibility of trips, but many of those combos would have acted differently on the flop. I want to both extract thin value from worse Qx and small pairs and deny equity to diamond draws, so I’ll make a half‑pot value/protection bet. If I get a large check‑raise I’ll strongly consider folding given my weak kicker; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously against heavy action.

The 3c on the turn pairs the board, which is largely a blank card in this context. Minni has led out for half the pot on both the flop and the turn. While she could certainly hold a Queen, her range also includes many draws (like diamond flush draws or straight draws with 56) and middle pairs (like 7x) that she is playing aggressively. Pocket nines are too strong to fold at this stage, especially given the pot odds and Minni's history of high aggression. Raising would likely isolate me against a better hand (Qx), so calling is the best path to control the pot size and see a river while keeping her bluffs and weaker value hands in the pot.

I check. I have Q♦8♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Flash cold‑called my flop C‑bet and my half‑pot turn bet, so his continuing range is wide but includes Qx (many with better kickers), medium pocket pairs (9s/7s/etc.), floats/Ax/Kx, and the dangerous trip combos (3x) that the paired turn makes possible. The river 9♣ gives him some additional pair combos but doesn’t remove the trip risk. Betting here would extract thin value from worse Qx and some second pairs, but it also bloats the pot and lets trips or a better Q check‑raise/shove put me in a very uncomfortable, likely losing decision given my weak kicker and the effective stacks. With the pot already small (2,400) and deep stacks behind, I prefer pot control and a showdown. I’ll check to avoid getting raised off my hand; plan is to call a modest river bet from a calling‑station line but fold to a large overbet/shove that represents trips or a very strong Qx.

On the river, I have improved to a full house (Nines full of Threes). Minni has led out on the flop and turn but has checked the river. This check often indicates either a missed draw or a marginal value hand like a Queen that is now afraid of being beat by a slow-played monster or a back-doored draw. Given the strength of my hand, I must bet for value. A bet of 1500 into a 2400 pot is a standard value-sized bet that is likely to be called by any Queen (Qx) or potentially even a Seven (7x) if she suspects a bluff. While she could technically have QQ or 33, these are statistically unlikely, and I must maximize value from the more probable hands in her range.

I checked the river to control the pot with top pair/weak kicker. Flash’s line (open, call flop, call turn, now raise 1,500 into ~2,400) is consistent with value hands that improved on the river (9x, better Qx) and some two‑pair/trips/full‑house combos, but it also can include missed diamond draws turned into bluffs. The raise size is a standard value/targeting sizing — not a polarizing overbet — and calling 1,500 to win ~3,900 requires ~38% equity. Folding top pair here is too nitty given the frequency villain will have worse Qx and some thin value/paired‐river hands; a raise would unnecessarily bloat the pot and commit with a weak kicker. I’ll call the 1,500 to get to showdown and then re‑evaluate if Flash jams for a much larger amount (in which case I’ll fold to a polarizing shove).

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should open very wide. 8♦3♦ is marginal but suited and playable in position — it has backdoor flush/straight potential and plays well when I have the betting lead. I’ll raise to the min-open (200 total = 2x BB) to take the initiative, build the pot with position, and put pressure on the big blind. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet on many dry flops and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards. Calling 50 is too passive in this spot, so I prefer a standard open to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with As2s, a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni opened with a min-raise to 200. While calling is an option to keep the pot small, As2s has great playability and nut potential (flush draw). I will 3-bet to 600 to take control of the hand and build a pot with a range-advantage hand. Minni has been opening wide, so applying pressure pre-flop is a solid strategy.

I opened wide from the button to 200 and Flash 3-bet to 600 (a standard 3x vs my sizing). 8♦3♦ is weak but suited with backdoor straight/flush potential and plays well in position. Calling 400 more (600 total) gives good pot odds and keeps the pot manageable so I can realize equity and take initiative post-flop. A small 4‑bet or shove would over‑commit with a very marginal hand; folding is too nitty against what is likely a fairly wide heads‑up 3‑bet range from Flash. Plan: see the flop in position, c‑bet many dry boards to seize the initiative, and fold to significant resistance on highly coordinated or big‑card flops/turns.

I am the pre-flop 3-bettor and the board of K-4-5 is generally favorable for my perceived range, which includes hands like AK, KQ, and AA. I hold Ace-high with the nut backdoor flush draw and a gutter to the wheel (needing a 3). Since I have the range advantage and some equity to improve, I will lead out with a standard continuation bet of 1/3 the pot. This size allows me to apply pressure to Minni's air and small pocket pairs while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called.

Facing a small 400 c-bet into a 1,600 pot on K♦-5♣-4♠ with 8♦3♦. I have no pair and only backdoor diamond/straight possibilities (backdoor flush by river ≈ 4%, overall raw equity well below the ~20% break‑even the pot odds give). I’m in position with a lot of post‑flop maneuverability, so the right play is to keep the pot controllable, realize my equity, and use position to make better decisions on the turn. Folding is too nitty given the cheap price; raising would be an overbluff with little fold equity versus a 3‑bettor’s range and would commit chips with a weak hand. I’ll call 400, then re-evaluate: if the turn brings a diamond or a profitable straight card I can continue aggressively; if the turn is a blank and Flash shows strength I’ll concede the pot.

The turn brings an Ace, which is an excellent card for my pre-flop 3-betting range. Having improved to top pair, I want to continue representing strength and extract value from hands in Minni's range that might have called the flop, such as a King, a lower pocket pair, or straight draws. While my kicker is weak, the Ace is a significant scare card for most of Minni's holdings. I will lead out with a half-pot bet to build the pot while keeping it under control in case she has a better Ace or a slow-played set.

Fold. Flash 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn for 1,000 into a 3,000 pot — calling 1,000 requires roughly 25% equity to be +EV. My 8♦3♦ has no pair and the backdoor diamond draw that existed on the flop is dead after the A♣ turn, so I have essentially no clean outs. Flash’s line is weighted to Kx/Ax/top pairs/sets and some thin bluffs, but not enough to justify calling or risking a bluff‑raise (min raise to 2,000) with nothing. Preserve stack and fold to this turn bet.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Jd 6c. This is a mediocre hand, but in heads-up play, it's generally worth opening or calling to maintain pressure. The stack sizes are relatively deep (Flash: 16400, Minni: 23450). I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop, as Minni has been playing somewhat cautiously to raises in the past.

Heads-up in the big blind with 5c5h vs a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total committed 300). With effective stacks ~16k (~160 BB) the spot is perfect for set-mining and exploiting a likely wide opening range from Flash. Calling gets me ~33% pot-equity break-even (200 / (400+200) ≈ 33%) and huge implied odds if I hit a set; 3-betting a small pocket pair OOP risks getting 4-bet or bloated pots without initiative. Plan: flat-call, see the flop; if I hit a set I’ll extract value (check/lead or induce depending on board and villain’s action); if I miss, fold to heavy action on overcard-heavy/draw-heavy boards or call small C-bets on dry flops to realize equity. Action: call to keep the pot controlled and maximize implied odds.

I have pocket 5s on a dry Q-4-2 rainbow — an underpair to the queen. Heads-up vs a wide button opener, I'm often ahead of pure air and many overcards, so checking for pot control and to realize equity is best. Betting OOP here bloats the pot without initiative and risks getting raised off the hand; instead I’ll check and call a small/standard c-bet to see a turn (folding only to large, polarized raises). If villain checks back I keep showdown value; if I improve to a set I can extract value later.

On a very dry flop of Q-4-2 rainbow, I have missed completely with Jack-high. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet. Minni has shown a tendency to fold to aggression when she doesn't have a strong piece of the board. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to take down the 600 pot immediately. If she calls, I will likely check back on most turns unless I improve, but right now the goal is to win the pot uncontested.

I flat-called preflop to keep my range wide and preserve set-mining implied odds. On Q♥‑4♦‑2♣ (rainbow) I have an underpair (5♣5♥). Flash’s small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot) gives me ~25% break-even equity to call and likely represents a wide, high-frequency continuation range on this dry board. I’m ahead of lots of air and some overcards will still check back; raising OOP with a small pair is unnecessarily bloating the pot and risky. With deep stacks I can realize equity or win a big pot if I hit a set. Action: call 300 to see the turn; fold only to a large polarized raise, call small/medium turn bets to showdown, and extract maximum value if I improve.

Heads-up on the button vs an aggressive opener. I have 5♣5♥ on 2♣‑4♦‑Q♥‑3♦ — a small underpair on an extremely dry, low board. With deep effective stacks I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand that is beaten by any Qx or better. Flash has a wide opening and continuation range, so checking keeps those hands in and allows me to call small turn bets or induce bluffs on the river. Plan: keep the pot small — check and evaluate Flash’s action. Call small/standard bets to see a river, fold to a large polarized shove/huge bet, and extract maximum value only if I improve to a set.

On a board of Q-4-2-3, I have Jack-high with no significant draws. Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, which suggests she has some piece of the board, likely a pair of Queens, 4s, or 2s, or perhaps a pocket pair. The turn 3 doesn't change the board state significantly for my range, though it technically completes a straight for 56 or A5. Since she checked again, I have the option to fire a second barrel, but with zero showdown value and limited equity, checking back allows me to see a free river and potentially catch a Jack or simply avoid losing more chips to a hand that is likely calling down. I will check to keep the pot manageable.

Pot is 1,200, I have 5♣5♥ on 2♣-4♦-Q♥-3♦-4♠. My pocket fives are a marginal made hand — better than lots of missed overcards and some bluffs, but behind Qx, any pocket pair 6+ and any 4 (which makes trips). Flash’s line (preflop open, small flop c‑bet, check the turn) smells like a wide, often medium-strength/air range and is consistent with checking turn to pot-control with Qx or giving up with air. Betting OOP on the river here bloats the pot when I’m frequently behind and also risks getting check‑raised by the polarized part of his range (trip 4s/very strong hands). I prefer to keep the pot small and realize my equity — check to showdown. If Flash now bets a small amount I’ll call for pot odds; I’ll fold to a large polarized shove.

On the river, the board has paired 4s. I currently hold Jack-high, which has virtually no showdown value. Minni has check-called my flop continuation bet and then checked both the turn and the river. While a bluff might occasionally work here, Minni has shown in previous hands (like Hand #92) that she is willing to call river bets with marginal hands. Since I checked the turn, a river bet might look suspicious and induce a call from any pair or even a strong Ace-high. I will check and concede the pot, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♦Q♣ is a strong, positionally advantaged hand. I’ve already posted 50 and the big blind is 100; calling 50 is an option, but raising to take the initiative and apply pressure is preferable. Against a wide BB defending range (based on past aggression from Flash), a standard 3x BB open is good: it gains fold equity, builds the pot when I have a hand that plays well postflop, and keeps the sizing straightforward with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets I can continue selectively (call or 4-bet based on sizing/reads); versus a call I’ll have position for multi-street play. Raise to 300 (total committed) to open the hand and seize initiative.

In heads-up play, Ace-Four offsuit is a strong defending hand against a standard button raise. Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises. I will call the additional 200 to see the flop, as 3-betting might inflate the pot unnecessarily with a hand that has good showdown value but mediocre playability in large pots if I don't hit an Ace.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a K-9-4 board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking to see their reaction is the standard play. I have some showdown value but don't want to over-commit to a pot where I might be behind a King or a Nine.

I have top pair (K) with a Queen kicker on a K♠-9♦-4♠ flop heads-up. Flash checked — I should take the initiative to extract value from worse kings, pairs, and Qx hands and deny equity to any backdoor spade/straight draws. The pot is 600; a half-pot bet (~300) is a standard, balanced sizing: it gets called by worse, keeps the pot controllable against an aggressive opponent, and leaves room to maneuver on turn/river. I'll bet 300 to build value and protect my hand.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a King-high board. In heads-up play, this is a strong enough hand to continue against a standard continuation bet. Minni's 300-chip bet into a 600-chip pot gives me 3:1 odds, requiring 25% equity. Given her aggressive history, her range includes many bluffs and weaker hands that I am currently beating. Calling is the best option to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn develops without over-committing to a marginal hand.

On the turn, the board has become slightly more connected with the 7c. I still hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker. In heads-up play, this remains a decent bluff-catcher, but it is not strong enough to bet for value against Minni's likely continuing range. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to continue with her aggressive tendencies, which might include bluffs or semi-bluffs with spade draws or straight draws. I will evaluate her action if she chooses to bet, but for now, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity.

I have top pair (K) with a good Queen kicker heads‑up and I was called on the flop. The 7♣ on the turn is a blank — it doesn’t complete common draws — and Flash checked, showing either weakness or a slow‑play. Betting for value is the best line: I want to get called by worse Ks, 9x, and small pairs/Qx while charging unlikely two‑spade combos and denying a free river card. A half‑pot bet keeps the pot manageable and preserves fold equity against marginal hands. Bet 600 and re‑evaluate on the river if called; fold or proceed cautiously if check‑raised depending on sizing.

On the turn, the 7c is a relatively safe card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold bottom pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). In heads-up play, this is a reasonable hand to use as a bluff-catcher against an opponent as aggressive as Minni. Her half-pot bet of 600 into 1200 gives me 3:1 pot odds, meaning I only need about 25% equity to make a profitable call. Given her wide range of potential semi-bluffs (spade draws, gutshots like 5-6 or 8-6) and air, I have enough equity to continue to the river. I will call to see the final card and evaluate the situation then, rather than folding or turning my hand into a bluff by raising.

I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board that hasn't changed significantly on the river. My hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking, I allow Minni to either check back with her marginal hands or attempt a bluff, which I will then have to evaluate. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me (Kings, Nines, or better). I am in bluff-catcher mode and will check to see her action.

I hold K♦Q♣ for top pair with a strong kicker on a 4♠-9♦-K♠-7♣-6♥ board. Flash check‑called a small flop bet and a half‑pot turn bet — a line consistent with medium strength (9x, 7x, small pairs) or missed draws (spade/gapper combos). Very few river hands legitimately beat me (mostly rare 5+8 straights or concealed two‑pair combos). With the pot at 2400 I want to extract value from worse kings, 9x and small pairs while still pricing out only the weakest hands; a half‑pot river bet of 1200 is a balanced size to accomplish that. If Flash folds I collect the pot; if called I’ll usually be ahead and can reevaluate only if they raise large (in which case I’d assume a very strong made hand and fold to extreme aggression). Bet 1200 to get value and deny equity to missed draws.

I am faced with a river bet of 1,200 into a 2,400 pot (making the total pot 3,600). I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. My pot odds are 3:1, meaning I need to be right at least 25% of the time to break even. Minni has been consistently aggressive throughout the tournament, often firing multiple barrels. The board (4s-9d-Ks-7c-6h) is relatively dry; while she could easily have a King or a Nine, she also has many missed draws in her range, such as various spade combinations (QsJs, QsTs, etc.) or straight draws like QJ or JT that didn't materialize. Given the 1/2 pot sizing she used on every street, this looks like a polarized range. Given her high frequency of aggression and the favorable pot odds, calling with my pair of Fours to catch a potential bluff is a viable strategy in this heads-up situation.

I am on the button with J6s. While not a premium hand, it is a suited semi-connector that plays reasonably well in position. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I want to keep pots manageable unless I hit a strong draw or made hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and apply pressure, which is standard for button play in a heads-up scenario.

Heads-up, Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Q♥6♣ and have 26,450 behind vs his 13,150. Q6o is a marginal but defendable hand heads‑up; I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand by 3‑betting. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and lets me fold to significant turn/river aggression if I miss. Plan: proceed cautiously postflop — continue with any queen, pair plus good kicker, two pair/trips, or strong draws; fold to signs of domination on dry, high boards.

Check. I defended preflop with a marginal Q6o, but on Tc 8s Th I have no pair — only Q-high and a very weak backdoor draw. The board is paired and relatively dry; leading out out-of-position with this hand would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots against tens, sets, or broadway hands that beat me. With effective stacks and heads-up dynamics I want to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: check to keep the pot small and see how Flash reacts. If he checks back I can take a free turn; if he c-bets a small amount (e.g. a probe bet) I may call to see the turn, but I will fold to sizable aggression that represents a made ten or better.

On a paired board of 10-8-10, my Jack-high hand has limited value but some backdoor equity with the heart draw. Minni, as the defender, may have many 8s or even some 10s in her range. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise from an aggressive opponent, and allow myself to see a turn card for free to see if my hand improves or if the board texture becomes more favorable for a bluff later.

Check. I have Q♥6♣ on Tc 8s Th Kh — no pair and no realistic draw (flush impossible with only two hearts on board). Flash checked flop and again on the turn, which either shows weakness or a slowplayed ten/K; either way I have no value and I’m out of position. Pot is only 600 and effective stacks are deep, so leading out here would bloat the pot with a very weak holding and put me in difficult spots against Kx/Tx. I’ll keep the pot small, see the river for free, and then decide: if he bets a small probe on the river I may call on a cheap price only with a decent read or a queen; I’ll fold to significant aggression that represents a made king/ten. For now: check.

I have picked up a flush draw on the turn with the Six and Jack of Hearts. Since Minni has checked twice on a board that is paired and now contains a King, she is showing significant weakness. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot as a semi-bluff. This allows me to potentially take the pot down now while maintaining equity to win the hand if I hit my flush on the river.

Fold. I’m OOP with Qh6c on Tc-8s-Th-Kh: no pair and no realistic draw (only 3 outs to a queen/6 on the river). Flash checked the flop then bets the turn 400 into a ~1000 pot — a line consistent with a turned King or a slowplayed Ten, or at least a value-heavy range. Calling 400 into the pot requires roughly 22% equity (400/(1400+400)=22.2%), but my actual equity to improve is well below that (~6–8%), so a call is -EV. Raising as a bluff is too thin — I have poor blockers and I’m out of position with deep stacks, so I’ll preserve chips and fold to this turn aggression.

I’m on the button (small blind) heads-up with A♥5♣. This is a hand worth opening from the button — in position I get to play postflop and extract value or apply pressure. Pot is 150 and calling costs only 50, but I want the initiative and fold equity so I’ll make the standard min-raise to 200 total. This sizing keeps the pot manageable, denies the BB a cheap flop, and preserves flexibility postflop (c-bet on ace/favorable textures, control pot on raggy boards). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and lean toward folding A5o vs a heavy 3-bet given his aggression and stack depths.

In a heads-up scenario, Queen-Nine suited is a premium hand well above the average opening range. Minni has been opening frequently with min-raises. I will 3-bet to 700 to take control of the hand and build a pot with a hand that has excellent playability and high equity against a wide button range.

I will call to 700. Facing Flash’s 3-bet to 700 heads-up with A♥5♣ on the button, I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use position postflop. My immediate cost is 500 to see the flop against a pot of ~900, so my pot odds (~35.7%) and positional equity justify a call versus a likely wide/aggressive 3-bet range. I considered a small 4-bet to 1200 to pick up the pot, but that bloats the pot and forces a decision out of position on later streets or risks running into a value 4-bet. Plan after calling: use position to realize equity, c-bet for value on ace/favorable boards, control or fold on heavily coordinated boards, and re-evaluate if Flash shows extreme strength. Call 500 more (700 total committed).

As the pre-flop 3-better, this Ace-high board significantly favors my perceived range over Minni's calling range. I will lead with a continuation bet to represent an Ace and put pressure on her pocket pairs or high-card hands that missed. A sizing of slightly less than half the pot is standard for this texture to maintain efficiency.

I have top pair (A♥) with a 5 kicker on A♠-3♠-2♦. Flash 3‑bet preflop and now fires a medium c‑bet of 600 — a line consistent with a wide, aggressive continuation range (Ax, broadways, some pocket pairs, and bluffs). In position I want to realize equity, extract value from worse A‑x / K‑x hands, and keep the pot controlled against sets/two‑pair combos or stronger Ax hands. Raising here risks a check‑raise or committing too much with a vulnerable kicker; folding is too weak since I’m ahead of a large part of his range. I’ll call the 600 to use position on the turn and re‑evaluate based on the card and his action. (Call 600 more -> my total committed becomes 700 preflop + 600 = 1300.)

On this Ace-high board, I lead the flop with a continuation bet to represent the Ace, but Minni called. Her call suggests she either has an Ace, a pocket pair, or a flush draw. The 6c on the turn is largely a blank card that doesn't improve my hand. Since I have no pair and no real draw, and Minni has shown a willingness to call down with marginal hands in previous rounds, double-barreling here as a bluff is risky. I will check to see how she responds; if she bets, I will fold and preserve my stack, as my hand has very little showdown value and almost no equity against her calling range.

I hold top pair (A♥5♣) with a weak kicker on As-3s-2d-6c. Flash 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn — a line consistent with either pot‑control/medium Ax or missed/bluffing parts of a wide 3‑bet range. I want to: (1) extract value from Kx/Qx/mid pairs and worse Ax combos I beat, (2) charge any backdoor draws, and (3) keep the pot manageable versus two‑pair/sets or better Aces. A half‑pot bet (~1,300) accomplishes that — big enough to get value and deny a free river, small enough to fold to a clear check‑raise. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises I’ll strongly consider folding this vulnerable top pair.

I have Queen-high on an Ace-high board and have missed completely. After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting the flop, Minni's call indicates she likely has a piece of the board, such as an Ace, a pocket pair, or a straight/flush draw. My check on the turn signaled weakness, and her bet of 1300 into a 2600 pot puts significant pressure on my air. I have zero showdown value and almost no equity to improve to a winning hand. Given the tournament context and stack sizes, it is far better to fold here and preserve my 12,450 chips for a more favorable spot than to attempt a high-risk bluff or make a losing call.

Ten-Nine offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play. I've been consistently opening for 3x the big blind (300 chips) when in position, and I will continue that strategy here to maintain pressure and pick up the blinds or play a pot with a hand that has decent connectivity and post-flop potential.

Heads-up in the big blind with QQ facing a 300 open from the button. Flash has a wide opening range from the SB and I have a premium hand that plays very well heads-up. Calling 200 (total 300) surrenders initiative and allows the opener to realize equity last; a tiny reraise to 500 would look weak and invite many hands. I want value and fold equity while leaving plenty of stack to play postflop, so I 3-bet to 1200 total. Pot is 400 now — my raise commits 1,100 more and if called the pot will be ~2,400 — a good size to get value from Ax, Kx, weaker pairs and to identify his strength. If Flash 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (QQ often calls or folds depending on 4-bet size), but my default is to take the initiative and build the pot with this hand.

Minni has increased her 3-bet frequency and sizing recently. Ten-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by her 3-betting range. While I have position post-flop, calling a 4x 3-bet with this hand would commit 10% of my stack to a pot where I am likely behind. Given the stack sizes and the remaining hands in the tournament, I will fold and wait for a better spot to contest a large pot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) vs a single big blind. 8♠7♦ is a playable, connected hand in heads-up — it has straight potential and decent postflop playability. Limping is too passive here and hands at this table have shown a wide, often aggressive range, so I want initiative and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (standard ~3x BB) to seize the initiative, build the pot in position, and either take the pot immediately or play last on the flop. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and flop texture; if called I can continue on favorable boards or exercise pot-control on bad ones.

I have 9-3 offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has been very active from the Button/Small Blind, frequently raising. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 into 400), 9-3 is one of the weakest hands in the deck and has extremely poor playability out of position. I've been folding similar hands to her aggression lately and will continue to do so here to protect my stack and wait for a better spot.
In a heads-up situation, King-five suited is a strong hand that should be played aggressively from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300 from the SB. I’m in the big blind with A♥K♥ — a premium, suited hand that fares very well against a wide button opening range. Pot is 400 and calling 200 more (total 300) would be passive and leave me out of position postflop. I want value and fold equity, so I’ll 3‑bet to a size that gets action from worse broadways/suited Aces but still leaves plenty of stack to maneuver. Raising to 1200 (total) is a balanced heads‑up 3‑bet: it’s well above the minimum (500), gives good fold equity, and keeps postflop playability against calls and turn/river decisions if Flash continues. If he 4‑bets big I’ll re‑evaluate (AKs can call or fold depending on sizing/reads; consider shove only against polarized mega 4‑bets). Plan: 3‑bet to 1200 to build value and leverage my strong holding.

In this heads-up situation, I opened for 3BB and Minni responded with a large 3-bet to 12BB. While King-five suited is not a premium hand, it performs reasonably well in position and has good playability post-flop. Minni has shown significant aggression recently, and folding here would make me too easy to exploit. With a healthy stack of over 110BB, I will call the 3-bet to see a flop and use my positional advantage to navigate the rest of the hand.

I’m first to act with A♥K♥ on 7c 3d Jd after 3‑betting preflop and getting a call. I have two overcards and decent equity vs Flash’s calling range, and this flop is fairly dry (though there are diamond draw possibilities). A medium continuation bet applies pressure to his weaker hands and draws while extracting value from worse Jx and Qx/broadway holdings. I’ll c‑bet ~50% pot to keep the initiative and control the size of the pot: if he folds I take it down, if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn, and if he check‑raises big I’m prepared to release AK since I currently only have overcards.

On this flop of 7c 3d Jd, I have a King-high flush draw. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and is now continuation betting 1,200 into a 2,400 pot. I need 25% equity to call, and a flush draw alone provides approximately 35% equity. Additionally, I have one overcard (the King) which could be live if Minni is holding a hand like Ace-Jack or a lower pair. Given the pot odds and my positional advantage, calling is the most profitable play. Raising is an option but could lead to a difficult decision if Minni shoves, whereas calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially take control of the pot on later streets if I hit my draw or if Minni checks.

I paired my King on the turn (board 7c 3d Jd Ks) giving me top pair with the best Ace kicker after 3‑betting preflop and barreling the flop. Villain’s flop call widens to Jx, medium pairs, some A‑x with diamond draws and suited broadways — many of which I now beat. Checking surrenders equity to diamond/straight draws and misses value from worse hands (Jx, Qx, A‑x, small pairs) that will likely call. I’ll make a value/protection bet to charge draws and extract from worse while keeping the pot manageable: ~60% pot. If he gives a large, polarized check‑raise I’ll strongly consider folding (that line usually shows two‑pair/set), but vs a call I keep the initiative and re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the King of Spades provides me with top pair to accompany my nut flush draw, making my hand exceptionally strong. Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout this hand, starting with a 12BB 3-bet pre-flop and a half-pot continuation bet on the flop. Her 3000 bet on the turn is significant, but with top pair and the nut flush draw, I have immense equity against her entire range, including sets and better Kings. Calling is the preferred play over raising because it keeps her bluffs and weaker Jacks in the pot, while allowing me to safely realize my equity. If the river completes my flush, I am in a perfect position to play for her entire stack, and if it blanks, my top pair still has significant showdown value in a heads-up scenario.

I have top pair (K) with the best Ace kicker after barreling flop and turn. Flash’s line (opened from BTN, called my 3‑bet, called my flop c‑bet and my turn bet) is weighted toward single‑pair hands (Jx), medium pairs and some A‑x / diamond draws — but the river Ten does complete a few dangerous Broadway straights (AQ and Q9) and turns JT into two pair. I also block a fair number of AQ combos since I hold A and K. Given that, I want a value‑focused, non‑polarizing sizing: a small/medium river bet will extract calls from worse one‑pair hands (Jx, some Qx/pocket pairs) and charge missed draws while keeping the pot controllable if he has a made straight/two‑pair. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot to maximize thin value and reduce the frequency of a hostile river shove; if he jams all‑in I’ll fold because a shove here is highly polarized toward straights/two‑pair that dominate my hand.

On the river, the board is 7c-3d-Jd-Ks-Ts. I have top pair with Kings, although my kicker is weak (5) and my flush draw missed. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, starting with a 3-bet pre-flop and following through with bets on every street. Her river bet of 3,600 into a 10,800 pot gives me pot odds of 4:1, meaning I only need to be right 20% of the time to break even. Given Minni's high frequency of aggression and the possibility that she is bluffing with missed diamond draws or over-valuing a hand like AJ or QQ, folding top pair at these odds in a heads-up match would be too exploitable. Despite the river card potentially completing a straight for AQ, I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with 4♦8♦ — a playable, suited hand with good postflop playability. In heads-up I should open a wide range to take initiative and apply pressure; raising gains fold equity and builds the pot to play in position. Flash is relatively short (~29.5bb) so a standard 3x BB open to 300 is appropriate: it's consistent with my table image, big enough to fold out weak holdings and isolate, but not so large that I’m risking unnecessary stacks preflop. If Flash calls I’ll have position and can continue aggressively on many flops; if he 3-bets/shoves I’ll reassess based on his sizing and range. Raise to 300.

I have Pocket Tens in the Big Blind with a stack of 29.5 big blinds. This is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been very aggressive as the chip leader, frequently opening the button. Given my stack size, calling and playing out of position is less ideal than 3-betting to build the pot and take control. A raise to 900 (3x her open) is a strong semi-commitment that allows me to comfortably call a shove or continue on most flops, maximizing the value of my high-equity pair.

I opened to 300 with 4♦8♦ (a suited, low connector) and Flash 3-bet to 900. With heads-up dynamics and me on the button, I have a large positional edge and the call is cheap relative to my stack. Calling 600 more (900 total committed) gives me ~3:1 pot odds (600 to win ~1800), which is enough to continue with this speculative, playable hand. A 4-bet or shove would overcommit with a marginal holding and is unnecessary against a short-stacked opponent whose 3-bet could be wide; folding would be too tight given the favorable price. I’ll call to realize equity in position and re-evaluate on the flop — c-bet/press when I hit or fold to heavy aggression on very unfavorable boards.

I have an overpair (Pocket Tens) on a board that is relatively low (8-7-4) but contains a flush draw and some straight possibilities. My stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is approximately 1.2, which means I am mathematically committed to this hand. By betting 900, I am committing nearly half of my remaining stack to the pot, pricing in draws and setting up a shove on any turn. Given Minni's aggression, I want to charge her to see the next card while I still likely have the best hand.

Flopped two pair (8s & 4s) on 7c 4c 8s — a very strong hand heads-up. Flash 3-bet preflop and now fires 900 into the pot; his range is wide (overcards, 8x, 7x, clubs, some pocket pairs/sets). The only hands that currently beat me are sets (77/44/88), which are relatively rare. With Flash so short, raising to commit him maximizes value and denies equity to club draws and single-pair hands that would continue cheaply. Calling keeps marginal hands and draws in, but allowing cheap turn cards risks losing equity; I want to isolate now and extract value from worse pairs/draws. I’ll raise to put him all-in and scoop a large short-stack — this is the highest-EV line given stack dynamics and board texture.

I have an overpair (Pocket Tens) on a low board (8-7-4). I've already committed a significant portion of my stack, and the remaining 1,250 is a trivial amount to call given the pot size and the strength of my hand. Minni is an aggressive player who could be pushing with a wide range of draws, top pairs, or even smaller pairs. Even if she has a set or a straight, the pot odds are overwhelming. I must call to realize my equity and double up.

In a heads-up situation with a 60BB stack, J4s is a standard opening hand from the button. I want to maintain an aggressive posture and attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position. Given Minni's large stack and recent aggression, I will raise to a standard 3x to test the waters.

Heads-up in the big blind with 9♣5♣ facing a 3x open to 300 from the SB. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total committed). 9-5 suited is a speculative but playable hand heads-up — it has flush/backdoor straight potential and reasonable equity versus a wide opening range. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a weak, multi-way-style hand and risk bloating the pot or conceding fold equity to a shorter-stacked opponent; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity and play position on later streets. I’ll defend and see a flop.

Heads-up on the flop I have a pair of fives (9♣5♣) on Q♥6♥5♦. Against the button’s wide opening range this hand has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to Qx and heart draws. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (300) to extract value from worse hands and charge heart/overcard draws while keeping the pot manageable versus Flash’s ~5.8k stack. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises or shoves big I’ll fold most of the time since I don’t have the equity to call a large raise.

On a flop of Qh-6h-5d, I have Jack-high with no significant draws or backdoor potential. Minni's lead for 300 into the pre-flop aggressor (me) often indicates a piece of the board or a draw that she wants to define her hand with. Given that I have virtually no equity and a stack of 58 BBs, there is no reason to float or bluff on this texture. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining chips.

Heads-up on the button with 8♠7♣ — a playable, connected hand that plays very well in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 250 more to make a standard 3x BB open. Versus one opponent (Flash, 57bb) I want to seize initiative, gain fold equity, and avoid letting him see a cheap flop. A raise to 300 total is a standard, balanced sizing: it applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable if he calls, and gives me positional leverage postflop. If he 3-bets big or shoves I can re-evaluate knowing he’s representing strength; limp/call would be too passive. I’ll raise to 300.

In a heads-up situation, King-Six offsuit is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with against a standard 3x raise. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 600). Given the current stack sizes and the frequency of Minni's opens, calling is the most standard play to see a flop and evaluate the hand's strength.

On a very dry, paired board like 7-3-3 rainbow, I am checking to the pre-flop aggressor. My King-high has some showdown value in a heads-up pot, and checking allows me to see how Minni proceeds. I don't want to lead into the raiser here as it polarizes my range unnecessarily.

I have top two pair (7s and 3s) on a paired flop (7♠ 3♣ 3♥) and I'm in position heads-up. Flash checked, so betting for value and protection is correct — my hand is strong versus his wide calling range but is still vulnerable to any 3 or a pocket 7. I want to extract value from worse sevens, single pairs and weaker hands while denying freebies. A half‑pot bet is a balanced size here: big enough to get calls from worse and keep the pot manageable if I’m behind. Bet 300 into the 600 pot. If he check‑raises large (showing strong/locked range), I’ll re‑evaluate cautiously; a small/standard raise I’ll consider calling, an all‑in shove from him is probably too strong and will require folding or a very close call depending on reads.

On a very dry, paired board of 7-3-3, King-high still holds significant relative value in a heads-up pot. Minni's continuation bet is standard for almost her entire range. Calling 300 into a pot of 900 gives me 3:1 odds, which is sufficient to see a turn. My King-high often leads against her air, and I have six outs to improve to a pair that likely wins the hand. If she checks the turn, I can potentially reach a cheap showdown or take the pot down.

On the turn, the board pair of threes remains the most significant feature. My King-high still has some showdown value against Minni's potential bluffs or lower high-card hands. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on this board doesn't serve much purpose, as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse. I will check to see how Minni proceeds; if she checks back, I get a free river, and if she bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing.

I have 8♠7♣ on 7♠ 3♣ 3♥ 5♥ — top two pair in position. Flash checked the turn after calling my flop bet, which signals a wide range: single pairs, pocket pairs, floats and some bluffs; strong full‑house/quads combos are possible but less frequent (they usually raise/flatted differently on the flop). I want value and to deny a free river that can turn a full house (3 or 7) or pair up a weaker hand into something that beats me. A half‑pot turn bet is balanced: big enough to charge worse pairs (5s, 4s, 6s, small pocket pairs, floats) and thin calls, but not so large that villain folds all worse hands or forces me into an uncomfortable, committed spot versus a big check‑raise. Bet 600 into the 1200 pot. If Flash makes a small/standard check‑raise I will re‑evaluate and likely continue; if he jams all‑in, I will give him credit for a very strong hand (full house/quads) and fold rather than hero call into a polarized shove.

On the turn, the board is 7s-3c-3h-5h. I have King-high with no draw. Minni has fired a second barrel of 600 into a 1200 pot. While my King-high had some value on the flop, calling a half-pot bet on the turn with no pair and no draw is significantly more expensive and risky. Given my stack size of 5,200, I need to be more selective with the hands I take to showdown. Minni's range here includes 7x, pocket pairs, and Ace-high hands that all have me beat. Without even a backdoor draw to rely on, I cannot justify a call.

I am on the button (SB) with 5h 6c. In heads-up play, this is a speculative but playable hand that fits into a standard opening range. Given the current stack sizes, I want to maintain aggression and use my positional advantage. Minni has been very active and aggressive, but I need to continue playing a solid range of hands to avoid being blinded out or bullied. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to take control of the pot early or fold cheaply if she 3-bets.

Heads-up vs a wide button open to 300. 7♦8♥ is a playable, connected hand with good flop equity and post‑flop maneuverability — especially given my deep stack (34,700) vs Flash’s 4,900. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call into a 400 pot → ~33% required equity), so defending is correct. A 3-bet bluff risks getting shoved or called by better hands; calling keeps the pot controllable and lets me realize equity and apply pressure on favorable flops. Plan: call to see the flop, c‑bet or continue when I hit or have strong draws, and fold to overwhelming aggression on dry boards.

I have top pair (7s) with an 8 kicker on a thin 7c-4c-3d flop. Flash opened wide from the button preflop and is very short-stacked (4,900) — my hand is ahead vs a lot of his range but there are club draws and some two-card straight possibilities. I want value and to deny equity to two-card club draws, so I’ll bet a medium-large flop: 1,000 into the 600 pot (~1.7x). A bet ≥ ~700 prices many two-card draws out of a profitable call to the river, while still keeping worse pairs/overcards and draws in to either call or shove. If Flash shoves I’ll call (I can comfortably call his shove with top pair given my big stack); if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I can continue reading turn action. This size maximizes EV — extracting value, protecting my pair, and leveraging my deep stack vs his short stack.

I have the current nuts with a straight (3-4-5-6-7) on a flop of 7c-4c-3d. Minni has led with a large overbet of 1000 into a 600 pot. This aggressive donk-bet suggests she either has a strong made hand like a set or two-pair, or a high-equity flush draw. Given my short stack and the presence of a flush draw, I want to get as much money into the pot as possible while I have the best hand. Raising to 2500 is a strong play that commits over half of my stack, likely inducing a shove or a call from an aggressive opponent like Minni, while protecting against potential draws on the turn.

Heads‑up on a dry, low flop I have TPTK — top pair (7) with an 8 kicker — and a huge stack advantage (33,500 vs Flash’s ~2,400). Pot is 4,100 and it costs 1,500 to call (~26.8% immediate pot odds). Flash is very short and his flop raise is consistent with a polarized short‑stack line (sets/very strong pairs and a lot of club draws / shoves or bluffs). Folding here would be too nitty — top pair is too good vs a wide short‑stack raising range. Raising to 4,000 is unnecessary: it won’t fold many of his value hands and only risks bloating the pot against hands that crush me; he can (and likely will) just shove his remaining stack anyway. I’m happy to call the 1,500 now and call any shove — I can comfortably commit more with my deep stack and calling keeps his bluffs/draws in to realize equity. Plan: call 1,500, call a shove if it comes, then re‑evaluate on the turn for value/protection.

Board: 7c 4c 3d 9c. I have top pair (7d) with an 8 kicker. Pot is ~5,600 and Flash is very short (≈2,400). On the flop I bet 1,000, he repolarized by raising to 2,500 and I called — his flop raise is consistent with a polarized short‑stack line (sets/very strong pairs and a mix of semi‑bluffs with club/straight equity). The turn (9c) is a dangerous card because it brings a third club to the board and now completes any two‑club hands; it also gives villain little reason to check a made nut hand (he may be pot‑committed if he has a made 9/sets/two‑pair or just trying to induce). Betting here risks bloating the pot and getting shoved on by a made flush or strong value hands; a small bet will often fold out the worse hands I want value from and a larger bet just invites an all‑in I can call but that needlessly escalates the pot. With my huge stack advantage I prefer pot control and to let the short stack take the lead: check and call any shove. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate the river and can still extract value or fold river‑only against clear strength. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and to induce/fold shoves from his polarized short‑stack range.

I have a straight (3-4-5-6-7) on a board of 7c-4c-3d-9c. While the nine of clubs on the turn completes a potential flush, my stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is very low at approximately 0.43 (2400 remaining into a 5600 pot). In this situation, I am mathematically committed to the hand. Minni's check after calling my flop raise could indicate she is slowing down with a one-pair hand or a set, or she could be trapping with a flush. Regardless, checking behind allows her to see a river card for free which might be a fourth club, further complicating the board. By betting all-in now, I get maximum value from her sets, two-pair, or high-equity draws that will not fold given the pot odds.

Flash is extremely short and his flop+turn line is polarized — sets/two‑pair/turned 9s and a lot of club semi‑bluffs or bluffs. The turn raise (essentially an all‑in for him) costs me 2,400 into a ~8,000 pot; calling wins a pot of ~10,400 so I need ~23% equity. My top pair (7s) with an 8 kicker has reasonable equity against his wide, polarized short‑stack range and extracts value from worse pairs, single‑club hands, and many bluffs; folding would be too passive and surrender a lot of EV. Raising is unnecessary — it only bloats the pot against hands that dominate me (flushes/sets) and won’t fold many better hands. I’ll call the 2,400, seeing the showdown and only losing a relatively small amount if he has a flush or trips.

Heads-up on the button with 6♥5♣ — a speculative, positionally-playable hand. Limping is cheap but passive; opening here applies pressure and folds out worse hands. I'll raise to a standard 3xBB (300 total) to take the initiative, build a pot when I hit, and use postflop position. If Flash 3-bets large I will fold this hand; if he calls I'll proceed with C-bets on favorable flops and pot-control or check-back on dry, dangerous boards.

In Hand #105, I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Spades and Nine of Diamonds. Minni, who is currently the chip leader and playing quite aggressively, has raised to 300 from the Button. The pot odds for a call are 3:1 (200 to call into a 400 pot), which makes J9o a standard defending hand in heads-up play. While Minni is aggressive, J9o has enough playability to see a flop. 3-betting is an option but might lead to a difficult situation out of position against a deep stack, so calling is the most prudent move to preserve chips while staying competitive.

On a board of K-7-2 with two spades, my Jack-high hand has missed completely. As the Big Blind playing against an aggressive chip leader who raised pre-flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I have very little equity here, and unless Minni checks back or offers extremely favorable odds on a small bet, I am likely looking to fold this hand to further pressure.

I opened to 300 from the button with 6♥5♣ and got called. Flop K♠7♠2♥ is a dry but “dangerous” board for my air: a King (an overcard to a lot of my raising range) and two spades that give the BB useful backdoor/flush equity. I have no pair, no spade and negligible equity, and Flash checked — so rather than bloating the pot with a naked bluff against a caller-heavy opponent, I’ll control the pot and see a turn. Checking preserves stack depth, keeps my checking range balanced (I can still turn barrels on the right card or bluff river), and lets me fold cheaply to real hostility. If Flash fires a small probe on the turn I can fold or selectively continue when I pick up equity; if he checks back we get a free card and can re-evaluate.

On this paired board of K-7-2-7, I still have only Jack-high. Minni checked behind on the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a trap. Regardless, I have no reason to bet into the chip leader with no equity. I will check and see if she checks behind again to give me a free river.

I raised preflop and was called; both of us checked the K♠7♠2♥ flop and Flash checked the turn when the board paired (7d). With 6♥5♣ I have no pair, no spade, and essentially negligible equity vs a likely calling range (Kx, 7x, pocket pairs or a spade draw). Checking the turn controls the pot, keeps my checking range balanced, and avoids getting check‑raised or committing chips with air. Plan: check back and re-evaluate on the river — bluff or value bet only when the river is favorable (I pair up or see a free card that misses his range); fold to meaningful aggression on this street.

The board is K-7-7-2-2. My hand, Jack-high, currently plays the board: Two Pair (Sevens and Deuces) with a King kicker. Because the board has a King, any non-paired hand like Ace-high or Queen-high also plays the board, resulting in a split pot. Any pocket pair (33 or higher) or any King would have me beaten. Given that Minni checked both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely she has a King. However, she could easily have a small pocket pair. Betting serves no purpose because I will only be called by better hands or hands I am already splitting with. Checking is the most logical move to reach a showdown for free.

Board: 2♥ K♠ 7♠ 7♦ 2♦. I hold 6♥5♣ with zero showdown value — the best 5‑card hand for me is the board. Both players checked flop and turn and Flash checked the river, showing weakness or pot‑control with marginal Kx. Checking down here hands me a chop when he missed and loses when he has K/7/2; my only realistic path to win more is a bluff. A pot‑size bet (600) credibly represents trips/full house (7x/2x) and forces Kx and many marginal hands to fold. Break‑even fold frequency for a pot bet is ~50% (600/(600+600)), which is reasonable given Flash’s passive checks. If he calls I likely lose or chop; if he raises I’ll release (don’t want to get check‑raised into a real full house). I’ll make a pot‑sized river bluff to try to take down the pot.

The final board is K-7-7-2-2, providing two pair (Sevens and Deuces) with a King kicker. My hand, J-9, does not improve this, so I am playing the board. Any pocket pair (33 or higher) would beat the board by making a higher two-pair (e.g., 33 makes 7-7-3-3-K). Minni raised pre-flop and then checked both the flop and turn. While the checks suggest she doesn't have a King, her pot-sized bet on the river after the board double-paired is polarizing. She is likely betting for value with a pocket pair she held or she is trying to push me off a split pot. Since I can only win a split pot at best (and only if she has no pocket pair and no Ace) and will lose the call 100% of the time if she has any pair, calling a full pot bet here is mathematically unsound. I will fold and preserve my stack.
In heads-up play, 96s is a strong starting hand from the Button. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and exert pressure, as is standard for my position in this stage of the tournament.

Heads-up vs button open to 300; I’m in BB with J♠3♣. Calling 200 (total 300) is the cheapest, most practical option — pot odds (~2:1) and the button’s opening range heads-up are wide enough that J3o has reasonable equity to see a flop. I’m out of position, so 3-betting with this junk is unnecessary and folding is too nitty for heads-up. Plan: call and play the flop cautiously — fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss, continue for value if I pair the J (or pick up good backdoors). If Flash jams a large all-in preflop, I’ll fold this hand.

I missed this flop — J♠3♣ on 9♥6♦5♦: no pair, no diamond, no meaningful straight draw. I'm out of position versus the preflop raiser who will have lots of pairs and diamond draws that connect with this board. Pot control and discipline are my priorities: check and give Flash the opportunity to show aggression. Fold to any meaningful bet (no reason to invest more with essentially zero equity), re-evaluate only if I pair a J/3 or pick up a clear backdoor draw on the turn. Checking keeps the pot small and preserves my deeper stack leverage for better spots.

I have flopped top two pair (nines and sixes) on a coordinated board containing both straight and flush draws. Since Minni checked to me, I must bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. A bet of around two-thirds of the pot is appropriate here to build the pot while the board is still relatively safe for my specific holdings.

Flop: 9♥ 6♦ 5♦. I hold J♠3♣ — I completely missed this board (no diamonds, no pair, no real backdoor). Flash opened wide preflop and is continuation-betting a modest 400 into a 1000 pot, which represents a fairly wide range heads-up. Calling 400 gives me roughly 22% equity price (400 to win ~1800), so it’s cheap enough to see a turn and realize equity rather than folding out too often. I don’t want to check‑raise/bluff out of position with such a weak hand; my plan is to call and keep the pot controlled, then fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pair up or pick up a clear draw. Call 400.

I completely missed the turn — J♠3♣ on 9♥-6♦-5♦-2♠ has no pair, no diamond or realistic draw. I called the flop as a cheap equity realization, but out of position with essentially zero showdown value I should keep the pot small.
