Check to control the pot and give Flash the chance to either show down or fire a bluff; fold to meaningful turn aggression (no reason to bloat the pot with J3o). If he checks back I take a free showdown; if he bets big I release.

I have top two pair (nines and sixes) on a board that remains coordinated with straight and flush draws. The 2s on the turn is a relatively safe card for my hand. Minni has checked again, and I must continue betting for value and to charge any drawing hands like diamonds, 78, or 47. A bet of 1,000 into the 1,400 pot (approx 70%) is large enough to extract value from one-pair hands like A9 or K9 while making it expensive for her to chase draws.

I have J♠3♣ and completely missed the board (9♥-6♦-5♦-2♠). I checked the turn to control the pot and let the button show aggression; Flash bets 1000 into a 2400 pot. Calling 1000 would make the pot ~3400, so I need ~29% equity to justify a call, but with only J3o I have essentially six clean outs to pair on the river (~13% equity) and no diamond or straight backdoors. Flash’s line (open, small c-bet, then turn barrel) is consistent with many made pairs and semi-bluffs — not enough clear evidence of a pure bluff. Out of position and facing meaningful aggression, calling is a losing price; raising is nonsense with no equity. Preserve my 29,200 stack for better spots and fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) — open-raising a wide range is standard and profitable. Jc5d is a marginal hand but perfectly fine to raise here: I want the initiative and some fold equity against Flash's blind, while keeping the pot small with deep stacks. I’ll make a standard 2bb open (to 200). If he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (often folding unless the sizing is small and I can play postflop), if called I’ll c-bet favorable low/unjammed boards and proceed cautiously on heavy/highly-connected flops.

Holding 8s 3d in the Big Blind, I am getting 3:1 odds to call a small 100-chip raise (100 to win a total pot of 300). While the hand is weak and 3-offsuit is generally a fold against most raises, Minni has been quite aggressive and this is a relatively small open. However, 83o is near the bottom of any defending range and playing out of position against a large stack like Minni's will be difficult post-flop. I'll preserve my stack and fold here.

I am holding 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Even in a heads-up situation on the button, this hand has very little playability. With a significant stack disadvantage compared to Minni, I should avoid entering pots with bottom-tier hands. Folding is the best choice to preserve my chips for a more favorable spot.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦J♣ — a strong, playable hand that does very well when taking the initiative. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is too passive; min-raising to 200 is too small and gives the BB good odds to see a cheap flop. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure, seize position postflop, and fold out weaker holdings. If Flash calls, I plan to c-bet favorable flops and use positional advantage; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold vs a large 3-bet, consider a call or 4-bet bluff vs a small, frequent 3-bettor). Stack depths (29.3k vs his 10.55k) allow an aggressive but controlled sizing here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 3d. Minni, who is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively, raises to 300 from the Button. 93 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, this hand realization is poor and will likely lead to difficult spots post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better hand to commit my chips.

I am on the button with KJo, a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Historically, Minni has been quite aggressive, so I want to start by building the pot. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to take control of the hand and value-bet against her wider range.

Facing a standard button open to 300 heads-up. I have Q♦8♦ in the big blind — a suited broadway connector that plays well against a wide opening range. I’m out of position regardless, and 3‑betting this marginal hand OOP isn’t necessary; I prefer to keep the pot reasonable and realize equity. Calling keeps weaker parts of his range in, preserves my stack depth, and lets me continue with top pair, flush/straight draws or favorable texture flops. I’ll call the extra 200 (100 already posted) to put 300 total into the pot and evaluate the flop, folding to heavy action without equity.

Check. On T♦ 9♥ 7♥ with Q♦8♦ I have an open‑ended straight draw (J or 6 — ~31% to hit by river), a backdoor diamond look and an overcard. The flop heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range, and I’m out of position, so betting here would bloat the pot with a drawing hand. I’ll check to control the pot and let Flash act first. Plan: call a standard continuation bet and re-evaluate on the turn; become aggressive if I pair or complete the straight/diamond, or fold to heavy pressure on a dry turn. With a big stack advantage I can apply pressure later instead of forcing the action now.

On a flop of Td 7h 9h, I have an open-ended straight draw with my K-J (needing an 8 or Q). This board is quite coordinated and likely connects with Minni's big blind calling range, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative. A continuation bet allows me to win the pot immediately if she missed, while I still have significant equity to improve on the turn or river. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot.

Call 300. On T♦9♥7♥ with Q♦8♦ I have an open‑ended straight draw (J or 6 ≈ 31% to hit by the river), two diamonds for backdoor flush potential, and an overcard that can pick up additional showdown value. Facing 300 into a 900 pot gives ~25% break‑even equity — my straight draw alone clears that, and the pairing/backdoor outs plus implied odds make a call profitable. Flash’s small c‑bet is a standard continuation from a wide button range; 3‑betting OOP with a drawing hand bloats the pot unnecessarily. I’ll call to realize equity, keep worse hands in, and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to heavy, non‑committal pressure if I miss, and be aggressive (raise/pressure) if I pair, complete the straight, or pick up a diamond draw.

I have Q♦8♦ on T♦–9♥–7♥–7♦: I turned both an open‑ended straight draw (J or 6) and a 4‑card diamond draw after the turn — roughly 15 unique river outs to make a straight or flush (15/46 ≈ 32.6%), and another ~6 cards to pair either hole (total ≈ 21/46 ≈ 45.7% to improve to at least a pair). Pot is 1,200 and there’s no bet to me, so a half‑pot semibluff makes sense: it buys equity outright against some of the button’s wide raising range and still builds the pot when I’m drawing. A bet of 600 gives me decent fold equity (need ~33% to break even on a bluff: 600/(1200+600)=33.3%) while keeping the size reasonable OOP. Plan: bet 600 as a semi‑bluff — if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I go to the river with ~33% to hit my main outs and will evaluate river play; if he raises substantially I will generally fold the turn (or only continue to very small raises that give correct pot odds), because I don’t want to commit OOP with a draw against strong made hands.

On the turn, the board pairs the seven. Minni leads out with a 600 bet into a 1200 pot. I currently have an open-ended straight draw (needing an 8 or a Queen) and two overcards. While the seven pairing is a scary card that might have improved her range if she was defending with middle pair or a seven pre-flop, the pot odds are favorable. I need to call 600 to win a total pot of 2400, giving me 4:1 odds (25%). With 8 clean outs to a straight, I have roughly an 18% chance of hitting on the river, and my King or Jack might also be good if she is betting a weaker pair or a heart draw. Given the implied odds if I hit my straight, calling is the most mathematically sound play.

I rivered a diamond flush: Q♦8♦ on T♦–7♥–9♥–7♦–2♦ gives me a Q‑high diamond flush. Pot is 2,400 and Flash (button) called my turn semi‑bluff, so his range contains lots of single‑pair hands (Tx, 9x), some two‑pairs/sets, and a sprinkling of diamond combos. My flush is ahead of most of that range; only higher diamond flushes (A♦/K♦ combos) or very disguised full houses/quads beat me — those are relatively uncommon given his passive call line. I want to extract value from top‑pair and worse diamond hands while not overcommitting, so I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to get called by T/x, 9/x and worse diamonds but fold to a large raise that represents the rare full house/higher flush. Bet 1,600 chips (≈2/3 pot).

On the river, the board completes with a deuce of diamonds, which is essentially a blank card. I have missed my open-ended straight draw (8 or Queen) and am left with only King-high. Minni led the turn when the board paired the seven and has now followed up with a 1,600 bet into a 2,400 pot on the river. This line (check-call flop, lead turn, lead river) is typically very strong, often representing trips or a full house. While Minni has shown aggression, calling with King-high here requires me to believe she is bluffing more than 28% of the time with a range that I beat. Given my current hand strength and the board texture, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my remaining stack for better spots.

Raise to 200 (min-raise). In heads-up the button should open extremely wide — J♥2♣ is weak in a full-ring spot but acceptable heads-up because of position and fold equity. Limping gives Flash a cheap chance to see a flop; a small raise to 200 seizes the initiative, often wins the pot preflop, and keeps the pot small relative to our deep stack if we face resistance. Flash has a history of defending and calling wide, so if called I’ll play postflop in position (c‑bet on favorable dry flops, check/back on coordinated boards). If Flash 3‑bets large, J2o is a clear fold — preserve our big stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 54o. Minni, who has a significant stack advantage and has been playing aggressively, has min-raised to 200. While the pot odds are 3-to-1, 54o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a big stack. Given that we are on hand 111 of 150 and I have about 92 big blinds, I should prioritize preserving my stack for better opportunities rather than defending with the bottom of my range.

Holding Td 7d on the button in a heads-up scenario is a standard opening hand. With a stack of 92BBs, I want to maintain aggression and take control of the pot early. Raising to 300 is a standard 3x open that allows me to see how Minni reacts and potentially take the blinds or set up a post-flop play.

Heads‑up in the big blind with Ad3h facing a standard open to 300 from the button. A3o is worth defending — it blocks many Ax combos and has solid equity versus a wide opening range. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) is cheap relative to the pot and keeps the pot under control while preserving post‑flop flexibility; 3‑betting is an option but with this marginal hand out of position I don’t want to face a 4‑bet or inflate the pot unnecessarily. I’ll call to see a flop and plan my line based on the board texture and Flash’s tendencies.

Heads‑up on a fairly dry Q♠‑9♥‑6♥ flop. I hold A♦3♥: Ace‑high with one heart (backdoor potential) and decent blocker to many A‑x combos. Flash (button/SB) opened wide preflop, so his range contains a lot of air, single‑paired hands and broadways. Betting here takes the initiative, folds out a bunch of Kx/Jx/complete air, and charges any heart draws or turn equity. I don’t want to inflate the pot with just Ace‑high, so I’ll fire a half‑pot c‑bet to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn for pairs/draws; if he raises large I’ll respect that and fold (or call a very small raise to probe), because my hand has limited showdown value.

I am currently holding a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8 to complete a ten-high straight) and have position on my opponent. Minni has led into me with a 'donk bet' of 300 into a 600 pot. Given that I am getting 4:1 pot odds (300 to win a total of 1200), and I have position to potentially outplay my opponent on later streets or check back if I miss, a call is the most mathematically and strategically sound play. Folding would be too weak given the equity of the draw and the odds offered, and raising would unnecessarily bloat the pot with a marginal hand.

Board: 9♥ Q♠ 6♥ J♣. I hold A♦3♥ — ace‑high only after the turn. I fired a half‑pot c‑bet on the flop and Flash called, so his continuing range is weighted toward made hands (Qx, 9x, 6x), some Jx combos that just improved, and occasional floats/draws. The J on the turn removes my realistic heart/flush outs (only one card left, so flush is impossible) and improves many hands in his calling range. Betting here would be largely a blocker/semibluff that will get called by better hands and fold out only the weakest holdings — and it also opens me to a check‑raise that I don’t want to face out of position with essentially ace‑high. With a big stack advantage I prefer pot control: check and allow him to bluff or show weakness. If he checks back we go to a cheap showdown; if he bets a small amount I can call and re‑evaluate on the river; if he puts in a large polarized raise I’ll fold. So I’ll check to control the pot and keep options for the river.

On the turn, the board is 9h-Qs-6h-Jc. I am holding Td-7d, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8 for an 8-9-T-J-Q straight). Minni led into me on the flop but has now checked the turn. This check often indicates a marginal hand or a 'one and done' bluff. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking back is a safer play that allows me to realize my equity for free. If I hit an 8 on the river, I will have a very disguised straight. If I miss, I can evaluate based on the river card and Minni's final action. Checking also protects me from a check-raise that would force me to fold my draw.

Board: 9♥ Q♠ 6♥ J♣ A♣. I hold A♦3♥ — top pair with a weak kicker. Flash called my small flop c‑bet and checked the turn, which weights his continuing range to Qx, 9x, Jx, missed heart draws and a number of broadway/AX combos. The A on the river improves me to a showdown favorite vs lots of his calling hands (Qx, 9x, Jx) but also gives some Ax combos and two‑pair/trips that beat me. I want thin value and to deny a free showdown to bluffs/marginal pairs, so I’ll make a small value bet rather than check back (checking invites bluffs or gives him a cheap showdown when he often has worse). A half‑pot bet (600 into 1200) is large enough to get called by Qx/9x/Jx and some floats, but small enough to fold out pure air and avoid bloating the pot vs the few stronger Ax or two‑pair combos. If he raises large I’ll concede (he’s representing a very strong hand); if he calls, I expect to win most showdowns.

I missed my gutshot straight draw on the river. The final board is 9-Q-6-J-A, and I am holding T-7, which is only ten-high. Minni led on the flop, checked the turn, and has now bet 600 on the river Ace. While the Ace is a common bluff card, I have no showdown value whatsoever. Even if she is bluffing with a hand like K-T or a missed heart draw, many of those hands still beat my ten-high. Calling is not an option, and raising as a bluff into a large stack on an Ace-high board is too risky. I will fold and preserve my remaining stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Q♥7♠. In heads-up the button should open a very wide range — Q7s is well worth raising. I have a big stack advantage (31.3k vs his 8.55k) so I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to his wide BB calling range. I’ll raise to 300 (total) — large enough (≈3x BB) to get folds from weaker hands and build the pot when I’m ahead, but not bloated. If he calls I’ll play the flop in position (c-bet favorable textures, check/control dry boards); if he 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand against heavy aggression.

With 9s 5d in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x raise from the chip leader, Minni. While it is heads-up and a wider defending range is typically warranted, 95o is near the bottom of that range and plays poorly out of position. Minni has been very aggressive and leveraging their stack advantage effectively in recent hands. With approximately 85 big blinds remaining, I have enough depth to wait for a better hand to contest a pot rather than calling with a weak hand that will often miss the flop or be forced to fold to continued aggression.

In a heads-up situation with an 85 BB stack, 8-7 offsuit is a standard hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. While Minni has a significant chip lead, I need to maintain an aggressive posture to avoid being blinded out or bullied. Raising to 300 (3x) is a standard opening size that allows me to take the initiative or win the pot immediately if Minni folds.

I’m the Big Blind with 7♣3♥ facing a Button open to 300 (to‑call 200). Heads‑up I’ll be out of position postflop and 7‑3o has almost no playability — it’s dominated by most of the button’s opening range and rarely realizes equity on later streets. Calling 200 only gets ~33% immediate pot odds but I won’t have the postflop tools to continue profitably; 3‑bet bluffing with this exact hand is a poor choice unless I have a very strong exploitative read that Flash folds too often. With a healthy stack I preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Heads-up on the button with A6o — this is a clear open-raise spot. I want to seize the initiative, charge the big blind for drawing, and build the pot with positional advantage. A standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) is natural at this table (many players open to 300), big enough to gain fold equity and get value from worse aces/king-x, but small enough relative to my deep stack (31,300) and the opponent’s (8,550) to keep postflop flexibility. I’ll raise to 300 total (add 250 more chips); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures, and if 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate versus his range.

I am in the Big Blind holding 54o. Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead, has raised from the Button to 300. While I should defend the Big Blind frequently in heads-up play, 54o is at the bottom of the range and plays poorly out of position. Given my current stack size relative to Minni, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to commit chips.

Holding 6c 4h on the button is an extremely weak hand. While standard heads-up strategy suggests playing many hands from the button, my current stack size relative to Minni's and her aggressive play style make this a clear fold. I want to avoid unnecessary chip bleed with junk hands and wait for more favorable equity spots given there are still 35 hands left in the tournament.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 5♦8♦ — a suited, playable hand with good postflop potential and position. Pot is only 150 and it costs me 50 to complete; instead I want the initiative and fold equity. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to pressure the BB, deny good odds to float, and play position if called. Plan: if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this speculative hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or play draws and pairs aggressively.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 3s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely difficult to play out of position and has very little potential to improve. Given that I have 84 big blinds remaining, there is no reason to defend with the bottom of my range against an aggressive opponent like Minni. Folding is the best way to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

In a heads-up situation, any King-high hand is relatively strong, especially on the button. I have 83.5 big blinds and want to maintain an aggressive posture to prevent Minni from simply bullying me with her larger stack. Raising to 300 is a standard open that puts pressure on the big blind while allowing me to play the hand profitably post-flop if called.

Heads-up vs the button I should defend a wide range from the big blind. T♠5♥ is a marginal but playable hand with decent flop equity and backdoor possibilities, and calling 200 more (total commit 300) gives good odds to see a flop while keeping the pot small out of position. I don’t want to 3‑bet this offsuit holding — Flash is relatively short (8,100) and a raise invites a shove or unnecessary confrontation with a weak hand. Plan: call, evaluate the flop, fold to heavy aggression, or continue on a favourable board.

Check. I’m out of position with T♠5♥ on 7♣ 9♦ 6♦ — no pair but some equity. I have 4 clean outs to an 8 for a straight (≈8.5% to hit the turn, ≈16.5% to river) and 3 tens that make top pair (so 7 raw outs total → ≈14.9% to hit the turn, ≈27.9% to river), although tens can be dominated so effective equity is lower. The board is connected with two diamonds so the preflop raiser will c‑bet a lot; pot control is the right approach with a marginal hand OOP. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me call a small continuation bet to realize equity, and fold to heavy aggression (short stack shove) rather than inflating the pot with a weak holding. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to re-evaluate (or barrel medium turns that improve my equity). If he bets a normal c‑bet I'll call to see the turn; if he jams or applies heavy pressure I’ll fold. Action: check.

The flop of 7c-9d-6d is very coordinated and generally favors the range of the Big Blind caller. With King-high and no direct draws, my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Betting into a board that hits many of Minni's potential holdings (6x, 7x, 9x, and various straight draws) might invite a check-raise or a call that puts me in a difficult spot on later streets. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and keep the pot small while preserving my stack against the chip leader.

Turn analysis: I have T♠5♥ on 7♣-9♦-6♦-7♥ — I have no made hand, only a gutshot to an 8 plus three tens (7 outs ≈15% to hit the river). The turn paired the board, increasing the chance the Button has a pair or better, and I’m out of position. Betting here would bloat the pot with very weak equity and invite calls from better hands (or a shove) that I can’t comfortably play for stacks. Pot control is the right plan: check to keep the pot small, realize my equity cheaply, call a modest turn bet to see the river, and fold to heavy pressure. If Flash checks back I’ll get a free river to either hit or take a more credible bluff line.

On a paired and highly coordinated board (7c-9d-6d-7h), my King-high (Kc 4d) still holds some showdown value against Minni's range of missed draws and lower high cards. Since Minni has checked twice, she is likely either giving up on a draw or playing a marginal made hand (like a 6 or a 9) cautiously, or potentially trapping with a 7. Given her significant chip lead and the wet nature of the board, betting into her is risky, as she could easily check-raise with her draws or trips. Checking back the turn allows me to control the pot size and see a free river card, maximizing the chance of winning the hand at showdown if the river is a blank.

Board: 7c 9d 6d 7h 2d. I hold T♠5♥ — I missed the river. Flash opened preflop then checked the flop and turn, which is a very passive line and strongly suggests a wide, marginal checking range (missed broadways, small pairs, some single-card diamonds), not many turned/rivered monsters or frequent two‑diamond flushes (those hands would often have taken a stab earlier). Pot is 600; a half‑pot bluff of 300 needs only ≈33% fold equity to be profitable (ignoring my tiny showdown equity), which is realistic given his passive line. I’ll make a modest river bluff (bet 300) to credibly represent a 7/9 or a completed diamond and try to pick up the pot. If Flash raises or shoves, I will fold — I have no showdown value. If he calls, I can accept the loss; overall this small-sized bluff exploits his checking frequency while keeping my downside limited.

Minni's line of checking the flop and turn before leading out for half-pot on the river (7-9-6-7-2) often represents a marginal value hand like a small pair (6x, 9x, or even 2x) or a slow-played monster (a 7 or a flush). While King-high beats some of her missed straight draws (like 8-5 or 5-4), those draws represent a small portion of her range compared to the many hands that beat King-high, including any Ace-high. Given the pot odds, calling with K-high is a high-variance play that is likely losing in the long run against Minni's current aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 81BB stack.

Heads-up on the button with Js9d — this is well inside a standard opening range. Pot is 150 and it’s a clear spot to take the initiative rather than limp-call. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to apply pressure, collect the blinds when BB folds, and play the hand in position when called. Size rationale: 300 gives solid fold equity while keeping the pot and future commitment reasonable with ~80BB effective vs Flash (8000). Plan if action changes: flat small 3-bets and play postflop in position; fold to very large 3-bets. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable textures, control the pot on bad boards, and value-bet when I pair up or improve.

I am holding 9s 2h in the Big Blind. Minni, who has a significant stack lead and has been playing very aggressively, raises to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 92o is one of the weakest hands in poker and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given my stack size of 8000 (80 BBs) and the remaining hands in the tournament, I should look for better opportunities to defend or counter-attack rather than committing chips with a bottom-tier hand.

I am on the button with T3o and a relatively short stack of 79.5 big blinds compared to Minni's 319 big blinds. This hand is very weak and doesn't play well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation where the opponent has been quite aggressive. Fold is the safest play here to conserve chips for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Q♢4♣ — marginal but perfectly playable. In heads-up play the button should open a wide range; raising to 2xBB (200 total) preserves initiative, creates fold equity and lets me play the postflop position. I have a large stack (32k) vs Flash’s 7.85k, so I can apply pressure without overcommitting; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand, if he calls I’ll use position to c‑bet or probe depending on the flop.

In a heads-up situation, K5o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind against a small 2x raise. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 100 to win a 300 pot), which makes it a mathematically sound call despite being out of position. Given the stack disadvantage and the stage of the tournament, I want to see a flop and evaluate my hand strength before committing more chips.

On a paired board of 3s 3c 7c, I have King-high which has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out with a bet. Checking is the standard play for the Big Blind on this texture to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. If Minni bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking allows me to control the pot and potentially see a turn card cheaply.

Heads‑up on the button I opened a wide range preflop. Flop 3♠‑3♣‑7♣ is very dry and paired; Flash checked, which is consistent with a wide range of missed hands, weak overcards, small pairs or occasional sets. My Q♦4♣ has no pair but a high-card (Q) plus a tiny backdoor club possibility — not a hand I want to slow-play, but it's perfect for a continuation bet to take the pot now or charge draws. I’ll c‑bet ~1/2 pot (200) to apply pressure and fold out lots of unpaired hands while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check back many blanks, target value on Q/T if I improve); if I face a sizable check‑raise or shove I will strongly consider folding—I don’t want to inflate the pot with no made hand.

On a paired board of 3s-3c-7c, my King-high (Ks 5h) still holds significant relative value against Minni's wide button-opening range. Minni's bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet that she would make with almost any two cards on this texture. Given the pot odds of 3:1 and the fact that I am ahead of many of her bluffs and have six outs to a pair that could be the best hand, I will call to see the turn. I'm looking to see if she checks back on the turn, which would indicate weakness, or if I can improve my hand.

On this paired board of 3s-3c-7c-Th, my King-high (Ks 5h) retains some showdown value against a wide button-opening range, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. As the Big Blind, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how she responds to the Ten appearing on the turn. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially reach showdown cheaply if Minni decides to check behind with her own marginal hands or missed draws.

Check. I hold Q♦4♣ on 3♠-3♣-7♣-T♥ — no pair and only a small club blocker. Villain called my flop c-bet (wide calling range: floats/overcards, small pairs, some 7x and rare 3x) then checked the turn, which is consistent with either weakness or a trap; the turn is a blank for my hand and didn’t complete any realistic draw. Betting here would be a pure bluff into many hands that currently beat me (7x, sets) and into draws that still have live outs; with the pot at 800 and deep stacks I prefer to control the pot and keep my options. I’ll check and evaluate the river: if I pair or the river is a great bluff spot I can lead for value or a delayed bluff; if I face aggression on river I can fold without bloating the pot.

On a board of 3s-3c-7c-Th-Jh, my King-high has very little showdown value. Minni's check on the turn after I called her flop bet suggests she has a marginal hand like a 7, a Ten, or possibly a high Ace that she's looking to take to showdown. While I could attempt to bluff, it's unlikely she would fold any pair on this board given the action. Checking is the most prudent move, allowing me to see if she checks back or if I need to fold to a river bet. I am unlikely to win this pot without betting, but the risk of a bluff being called is too high given the board texture and previous action.

I c‑bet the dry flop to take it down or charge floats; Villain called and then checked the turn and river. My Q♦4♣ never improves on T♥/J♥ — I only have Q‑high and a tiny club blocker. Villain’s line (call on flop, passive on turn and river) is consistent with a range containing sets (3x), pairs of 7s, Jx/T x and a bunch of missed floats. A river bet would be a pure bluff into many hands that actually beat me (sets, any J, many 7x) and into several hands that are willing to call light. The pot is small (800) relative to my deep stack (31,650 vs his 7,550), so there’s no need to manufacture risk here. I’ll check and take the cheap showdown — if he barrels I can fold and preserve chips; if he checks back I avoid losing extra with no showdown value.

I am holding Ah Qh on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand. Given the stack sizes and the need to build a pot with a strong range, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and extract value.

Facing a 300 open from the SB, my 4♦7♠ is very weak but not hopeless heads-up. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (total commitment 300), so I get pot odds of 200 to win 600 — I need ~33% equity. Against a wide SB opening range this hand has roughly that ballpark equity and calling keeps the pot manageable out of position. 3-betting with 47o is spewy (small 3-bets won’t fold enough hands, big 3-bets commit me with a bad hand) and folding outright is too tight given the pot odds and Flash’s wide opens. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: check/call or fold to heavy aggression unless I connect (pair/clean draw) or pick up a good read.

I have 4♦7♠ and the flop is 7♣ K♥ T♠ — I made a weak pair (7s) with a terrible kicker and I'm out of position versus the preflop raiser. Flash's opening range heads-up is wide and contains a lot of Kx/Tx and many overcards that currently beat me, so my hand has showdown value but is very vulnerable. Pot is 600 and there's no bet to call right now, so the least-committal, highest-expected-value play is to check and control the pot size. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium c-bet (rough guideline: call flop bets up to ~2/3 pot ≈ 400) to see a turn, because the opener will c-bet a lot with air and some bluffs; but fold to pot-sized/large raises or a shove which strongly represent a made King/Ten or a set. If Flash checks back I will re-evaluate on the turn (check behind most safe turns; bet small only if I improve or If villain shows weakness on later streets).

I have Ah Qh on a Kh Ts 7c board, which gives me two overcards and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts. Minni has checked to me. I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (50% of the pot) to maintain my table aggression and capitalize on the fold equity, while also building a pot for when I potentially hit my draws on later streets.

Call 300. I have top pair (7s) but a very poor kicker and I’m out of position, so I want to control the pot. Flash is a wide heads‑up opener who c‑bets a lot; the flop bet is small (300 into the current pot), so calling gives me ~25% break‑even equity (300 to win ~1,200) which is easily in range for top pair vs a wide c‑betting range. 3‑betting here is overly thin OOP and folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the turn and reevaluate — call small/medium turn continuation bets (≈≤2/3 pot) but respect large pot‑sized raises or shoves that very strongly represent Kx/sets; if Flash checks back I’ll consider a small delayed value bet on safe turn/rivers or check‑behind to keep the pot manageable unless I improve.

I’m OOP with 4♦7♠ on 7♣ K♥ T♠ A♠ — I have second pair with a terrible kicker and the turn Ace heavily improves the raiser’s range (many A/K/T combos now ahead). Flash is a wide, aggressive preflop opener who c‑bet a lot; that makes my 7 usable for showdown but not strong enough to build the pot out of position. Betting as a bluff here is thin and could get me raised/shoved; leading for value is pointless with such a weak kicker. I’ll check to control the pot, realize showdown value vs bluffs/weaker K/Tx, and react to Flash’s sizing: call small–medium turn bets (roughly ≤2/3 pot ≈ 800) to see a river, but fold to pot‑sized raises or all‑ins that strongly represent AA/AK/Kx/sets. If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and consider a small delayed value bet only if the river is safe.

On the turn, the Ace of Spades is a very strong card for my range. I have now paired my Ace with a Queen kicker, and I still hold a gutshot draw to the nut straight (Broadways). Minni has checked to me again. While the Ace completes a potential straight for QJ, I hold one of the Queens, reducing the likelihood of her holding that specific hand. I will bet 600, which is 50% of the pot, to extract value from weaker Kings, Tens, or pocket pairs, and to protect my hand against various draws that might be calling the flop.

Call 600. I have second pair (7s) with a terrible kicker and I’m out of position, so I want to control the pot rather than build it or fold too often. Flash is a wide, aggressive HU opener who c-bets and turns small bets into bluffs/pressure — calling 600 into a 1800 pot requires ~25% equity (600/(1800+600)=25%), which my hand has vs a wide c-betting range and it preserves showdown value against bluffs and worse K/T combos. Raising OOP with 4♦7♠ is too thin and folding is too nitty given the price. Plan: call to see the river; call small/medium river bets for value, but fold to pot‑sized/all‑in river shoves or very large raises that strongly represent A/K/sets.

I have 4♦7♠ — second pair (7s) with a very poor kicker on 7♣ K♥ T♠ A♠ 2♦. Flash opened wide, c‑bet both flop and turn but checked the river, which looks like either a marginal value hand or a missed/turned‑off bluff. Betting OOP here has very little fold equity versus the parts of his range that beat me (A/K/T/sets) and will often get called by better hands while folding out very few worse hands. The pot is 2400 and I don’t want to bloat it with a marginal hand; checking preserves showdown value, keeps the pot small, and is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line. I’ll check to take it to showdown (and only continue vs a very small bet on a cheap price; fold to large river aggression).

I have Top Pair with a strong Queen kicker on a board where many of the draws have arrived. Minni has check-called on every street, suggesting she has a marginal hand like a weaker Ace or a King. The river 2nd is a brick. I will place a value bet of 1200 (50% of the pot) to extract maximum value from her likely range. If she raises, I will have to evaluate if she has the straight (QJ), but a call is most expected from her showdown-value hands.

Pot is 3600 and Flash bet 1200 on the river — calling 1200 would make the total pot 4800, so I need ~25% equity to break even. I have second pair (7s) with a terrible kicker and I’m out of position, but Flash is a wide, aggressive HU opener who triple-barreled small sizing. A 1/3‑pot river bet is consistent with thin value and a nontrivial bluff frequency (missed draws, light aggression), so I expect enough worse hands/bluffs in his range to justify a call. Raising OOP here is too thin (won’t fold enough better hands and bloats the pot with a weak kicker); folding is too nitty given the cheap price and my showdown value. I’ll call 1200 to see showdown and fold to any much larger raise/shove.

Heads-up on the button with A♣5♥ — a profitable, playable hand. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and the pot is 150; calling 50 (total 100) is passive and gives the BB a free look. Raising to the min (200 total = 2x BB) gains fold equity, defines Flash’s range, and builds a pot I can play in position. Flash has shown frequent preflop aggression but also folds to pressure; with deep stacks I can comfortably apply pressure while risking only a small amount. Standard, exploitative heads-up play: raise to 200.

In a heads-up situation, T8o is a well-above-average hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 2x open. The pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call into a 300 pot), meaning I only need about 25% equity to make this a profitable call. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding such a playable hand against a frequent raiser like Minni would be too exploitable. Raising is unnecessary as T8o doesn't benefit much from a 3-bet. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or Jack completes the straight) on a board of 9-7-4. Being out of position as the pre-flop caller, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has significant equity and I plan to continue if Minni bets, but checking allows me to see how she approaches this texture and keeps my range protected.

I was the preflop raiser in position with A♣5♥. The flop 7♣-4♥-9♠ is very dry and rainbow — I currently only have Ace-high, but I hold an Ace blocker and Flash checked to me, which signals weakness or a marginal holding. In heads-up play the preflop raiser should c-bet frequently on a board like this to seize the initiative and fold out many hands that called preflop. The stacks are deep (29,050 vs 10,550) so a medium-sized continuation bet gives good fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet ~62% of the pot (250 into 400). If Flash check-raises large I’ll give it up; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only with improved equity or good turn cards.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with Ten-Eight on a Nine-Seven-Four board. Minni has bet 250 into a 400 pot. To call, I need to commit 250 to win a total pot that will be 900. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1, meaning I need about 28% equity to break even on a call. With 8 outs to a straight, my equity to hit on the turn is roughly 17%, and nearly 32% by the river. While the direct odds to hit on the turn are slightly lower than the pot odds, the implied odds of winning a larger pot if I hit my straight against an aggressive opponent make this a very standard call. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a board of 9-7-4-3. The turn 3s is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly alter the board texture, although it does introduce a flush draw for the opponent. As I am out of position, the most standard and effective play is to check to the pre-flop and flop aggressor. This allows me to see if Minni continues her aggression or takes a free card. If she checks, I get a free look at the river to hit my straight. If she bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but generally, an open-ended straight draw is strong enough to call another bet given the potential implied odds if I hit on the river.

I raised preflop and c-bet the dry 7♣-4♥-9♠ flop; Flash called. The turn 3♠ doesn’t improve my A♣5♥ — I’m still only ace-high. Flash checked to me, which shows weakness, but his flop-call range likely contains many made hands (9x/7x/4x, pocket pairs) and some floats. Betting here would be thin for value and just builds the pot out of position with a marginal hand; my fold equity is limited. Checking controls the pot, preserves my Ace-blocker/shown equity, and keeps my options open for the river (I can call reasonable river bets or barrel/lead if a favorable card comes). I’ll check back and re-evaluate on the river.

On the river, the 8d gives me second pair (8s) on a 9-high board. Looking at the board texture (9-8-7-4-3), the only completed straight is held by 6-5. Minni's check on the turn after betting the flop suggests she likely doesn't have a strong hand like a 9 or a set, or she is playing a draw cautiously. By checking, I can realize my showdown value against her missed draws or low pairs. If she bets, I have a relatively easy call given the pot odds and the likelihood that my middle pair is ahead in a pot that was checked on the turn. Betting myself doesn't achieve much, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and would only be called by better hands like a 9.

I opened preflop and c-bet the dry flop; villain called, then checked the turn and checked the river. Board (7♣-4♥-9♠-3♠-8♦) is very non-threatening — no completed straights or flushes — and Flash’s line (call flop → check turn → check river) shows a lot of weakness: floats, overcards, or small pairs that don’t want to build the pot. I hold the A♣ which blocks many Ax combos that would beat me. Checking will just take us to showdown with a marginal Ace-high and will give up the initiative; betting gives fold equity against a lot of hands in his calling range.

I’ll apply pressure with a meaningful but controlled bluff: bet ~2/3 pot (600). That sizing is big enough to fold out many missed hands and floats while capping my loss if called. If he check-raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll concede we’re likely behind to a pair but the combined fold equity and blocker make this the +EV play here.

I have second pair (Eights) on a board of 9-7-4-3-8. The turn went check-check, which often indicates that the aggressor (Minni) doesn't have a top-tier hand like a strong 9 or a set. By betting 600 into 900 on the river, she is representing either a 9 that she checked for pot control or, more likely, a polarized range containing missed draws (like JT or KJ) or thin value. The pot odds are 2.5:1 (600 to win 1500), meaning I need to be right about 28.5% of the time. Given that the 8 improved my hand and Minni's aggressive history, this is a standard call with a bluff-catcher that beats her missed straight draws and potentially some overplayed lower pairs.

I have Ace-Four suited on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand for this position and depth. I will put in a standard open raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up vs a button (Flash) open to 300. I’m in the big blind with T♥7♠ — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (total 300). Versus a wide button opening range T7o has reasonable equity and playability; the call requires ~33% equity and I expect to have at least that vs a standard heads-up raise. My stack (28,100) is much deeper than Flash’s (11,500), so I don’t need to 3‑bet light and bloat the pot out of position. I’ll call to see a flop, realize equity, and play post‑flop based on texture (look to value when I connect or take free cards / pot control with misses).

I have T♥7♠ on J♦7♥Q♠ — second pair (sevens) heads‑up. Flash opened a wide button range preflop, so on this dry, rainbow J‑Q‑7 flop many of his hands are missed broadways or single overcards (A/Kx) that I beat. I want to extract value from worse hands (A‑x, K‑x, some Jx bluffs) and deny him cheap turn cards, while keeping the pot manageable versus hands that have me crushed (Qx/Jx/pocket pairs). A small c‑bet ~1/3 pot is ideal: it gets called by worse and folds pure air. Bet 200 into 600; if he raises I’ll re‑evaluate (likely behind vs big raises but can call smaller raises to continue), if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

I have Ace-Four of clubs, giving me Ace-high and a backdoor nut flush draw on a Queen-Jack-Seven board. Minni has led out for 200 into a 600 pot. This 'donk bet' is relatively small, giving me 4-to-1 pot odds to call. With an overcard to the board and strong backdoor possibilities (any club, and potentially a King or Ten for straight equity), folding would be too tight. Raising is unnecessary and potentially expensive against the chip leader. I will call to see how the turn develops.

I have T♥7♠ on J♦-7♥-Q♠-8♣ — second pair (sevens) with a middling ten kicker. The 8 on the turn is a dangerous card: it completes 9-10 straight draws and generally increases the number of hands that now beat me (9-10, any Jx/Qx I’m behind to, and some pocket pairs). Flash called my small flop c-bet, so his range is wide and includes those straight draws and broadway holdings. With pot control in mind and no clear reason to inflate the pot OOP with a vulnerable second pair, I’ll check and keep the pot manageable. If he bets a small amount I’ll call to realize equity and extract from worse; if he barrels very large I can fold to preserve my deep stack and pick up future opportunities.

On the turn, the board is Queen-Jack-Eight-Seven. I have Ace-high. Minni led the flop for a small amount (200) and has now checked the turn. This board is very coordinated and hits a lot of a Big Blind defending range (straights with T9, various pairs, and draws). While Ace-high has some showdown value, it is unlikely to be the best hand if I am called. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and potentially see a cheap showdown if the river is favorable or goes check-check. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands given how well this board connects with Minni's range.

Heads‑up on the river, pot is 1000 and I’m first to act. I hold T♥7♠ (second pair, sevens) on J♦ 7♥ Q♠ 8♣ Q♣. Flash opened wide pre‑flop, called my small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn — his checking range contains a lot of unpaired broadways and medium hands (A/Kx, missed broadways, some Jx) plus the occasional Qx/Jx/pair. The river pairing the queen does create many hands that now beat me (Qx trips, two‑pair/full houses, bigger pairs), but I still beat a sizable portion of his checking range. I’ll make a small value bet (≈1/3 pot) to extract from worse hands (A/Kx, some Jx and weaker pairs) while keeping the pot controlled and preserving fold equity against pure air. If he raises big I’ll give it up; a very small raise I may call depending on sizing, but I won’t inflate the pot out of position with only second pair.

Minni led the flop for a small amount, checked the turn, and has now led the river for 300 into a 1300 pot. This sequence often indicates a marginal made hand (like a pair of Jacks, eights, or sevens) or a hand that improved on the river (like a Queen). While the price to call is small and provides good pot odds, Ace-high is rarely the winning hand given that Minni has shown interest in two out of three streets in a heads-up match. Since she is the chip leader and has demonstrated an aggressive post-flop style, I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with A♠8♦ — a solid heads-up hand. With deep stacks (28650 vs 11200) and position post-flop, I want the initiative and to avoid giving the big blind cheap odds to float. A standard 3x BB open is appropriate: big enough to isolate and punish weak calls but small enough to keep the pot manageable vs a potential 3-bet. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing; if he calls I’ll take advantage of position. I’ll raise to 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 7h. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, Q7o is a marginal hand to play out of position against a stack that significantly covers me. Given Minni's persistent aggression and my recent losses in small-to-medium pots, I will fold this weak hand and wait for a better opportunity to defend or three-bet.
I am on the button with Ace-Ten suited, which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take advantage of my positional edge.

Heads-up vs a 3bb open from the button. I’m in the big blind with K♣9♦ — a strong defending hand heads-up that fares well against a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 more (total commitment 300) for good pot odds and to see a flop out of position. I prefer to flat-call rather than thin 3-bet: it keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize equity, and gives flexibility postflop (bet when I connect, float or fold to heavy aggression when I miss). With my big effective stack I can apply pressure on favorable runouts, so calling and playing the flop is the standard, +EV line here.

Heads‑up vs a wide button opener — defending K♣9♦ preflop was correct. Flop T♠ 3♥ 8♥: I have no pair and no heart; the board has two hearts and a Ten so it’s relatively coordinated. I’m out of position and stacks are deep (I can apply pressure later), so I prefer pot control here. Betting as a pure bluff is possible, but against this opponent’s frequent continuation/float range he will often call or raise and put me to a difficult decision with just K‑high. By checking I keep weaker holdings in, preserve fold equity on later streets, and can call a small c‑bet or fold to heavy aggression unless I pick up a pair or a clear redraw. Plan: check, call small bets to see a turn, fold to large raises without improvement, and apply pressure if I improve or get a good read.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board of Ts-3h-8h. While there are some potential draws (flush draws and straight draws like 9-7), my hand is very strong in a heads-up context. Minni has checked to me, and I want to bet for value and protection. A bet of 300 (50% of the pot) is a standard continuation bet that forces weaker hands and draws to pay to see the next card.

I defended K♣9♦ preflop vs a wide button open and checked the flop (T♠ 3♥ 8♥). Flash's 300 is a small ~1/3‑pot c‑bet into 900; calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds to realize ~24% equity to pair by the river against a wide continuing range. Out of position I want pot control and to see a turn — call keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, lets me fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and gives opportunities to apply pressure or extract value if I pair up. Call 300 to see the turn.

I improved to top pair on the turn (Kc9d on T♠ 3♥ 8♥ K♥) but the board is very wet — three hearts — and I hold no heart and a weak kicker. Flash opened wide and c-bet the flop small, so his continuing range contains lots of two‑heart combos that now make a flush, as well as Tx/8x and broadways. Out of position with a marginal top pair, I don’t want to inflate the pot and get check‑raised or committed versus a made flush or a dominating Kx. Pot control + extract maximum from worse hands is the priority: check and let him show his hand. If he bets a small/medium amount I’ll call to river and reassess; if he fires a large polarizing bet/raise I’ll fold to avoid getting stacked by a nut heart or a better K. If he checks back and the river is a blank (non‑heart) I can take a stab for value; if it’s a heart I proceed very cautiously.

The Turn card (King of Hearts) is quite problematic for my hand. While I had top pair on the flop, the King is an overcard that fits well into Minni's calling range. Furthermore, the third heart completes potential flush draws. Having been called on the flop, my hand has now been downgraded to second pair with no redraws to a flush. Betting here doesn't achieve much; better hands (Kings or flushes) won't fold, and weaker hands are unlikely to call. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and utilize my position to see a river card and evaluate whether I can get to a cheap showdown.

I have top pair (Kc9d) on a T♠ 3♥ 8♥ K♥ 4♣ runout. Flash bet the flop but checked the turn and checked the river — that line polarizes toward a lot of missed hands and medium strength hands rather than a locked-in monster. With pot = 1200 and stacks deep, a small river stab is the highest EV line here: it denies free showdowns with a wide range, extracts value from hands that will call a small bet (many broadway/Ax/medium single-pair/river-caught hands), and folds out pure air. I don’t want to bloating the pot with a marginal kicker so I’ll use a modest sizing to maximize fold equity while keeping loss size controlled if I’m called. Plan: bet small (~1/3 pot = 400). If Flash calls I’ll see showdown; if he raises I’ll concede the pot rather than risk getting stacked by a better K or a turned/rivered two-pair/boat.

The board is Ts 3h 8h Kh 4c. I have Td Ad for second pair with the best possible kicker. On the river, Minni leads for 400 into a 1200 pot after the turn went check-check. This small 1/3 pot bet is often a 'thin value bet' with a weaker Ten or an Eight, or potentially a bluff attempting to take down the pot after I showed weakness by checking the turn. Given the pot odds (calling 400 to win 2000 total, which is 5:1), I only need to be right 20% of the time to make this a profitable call. In heads-up play, second pair with an Ace kicker is too strong to fold to such a small bet, even with the flush completing on the turn. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with pocket nines — a strong, value hand. I want to build the pot and take the initiative while keeping position postflop, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total). This sizing folds out weaker hands, gets value from worse pairs/overs, and keeps worse hands and draws in. If Flash calls I’ll play the flop in position; if he 3-bets small I’m happy to call and realize equity; if he 4-bets shove or makes a huge 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on pot odds and his tendencies.

In a heads-up situation, Q8o is a standard hand to defend the big blind against a 3x open. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), and my hand has sufficient equity against a typical button opening range to justify a call. I will see a flop and evaluate my position based on the board texture.

I have completely missed this flop with Queen-high and no significant draws. The King on the board is a card that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. As the first to act on the flop, the most standard and disciplined play is to check and see how the opponent responds. I am not in a position to lead out with a bluff here.

I hold 9♦9♣ on K♥-4♦-5♦ in position. This is a medium pair (under the King) against a wide heads-up calling range — Flash called preflop a lot and just checked the flop. A small-to-medium c-bet extracts value from worse pairs (44/55, smaller pockets) and many single‑card overcards (A/Q/J) that will fold, while keeping the pot manageable against a King or big hand. I’ll take the initiative with a half‑pot bet (300 into 600). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously if a King/diamond/overcard hits); if he check‑raises big I’ll give credit to a K/set and fold unless the raise is small enough to offer correct pot odds to call.

I have completely missed the flop [4d 5d Kh] with my Queen-high and have no draws to improve my hand. The King on the board is a very strong card for the pre-flop aggressor's range. Continuing in this hand by calling or raising with zero equity would be a waste of chips. The most disciplined action is to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 43o, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Although we are heads-up and I should be playing a wide range from the button, Minni has shown significant aggression throughout the match, frequently raising pre-flop and putting on pressure post-flop. Given the stack disadvantage and the remaining 22 hands in the tournament, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to commit chips rather than playing a weak hand out of position post-flop or facing a potential raise from the Big Blind.

Heads-up on the button with K♠5♠ — in heads-up you open a much wider range and K5s is perfectly reasonable to raise. Pot is 150 and I’ve already posted 50; calling 50 is passive and gives the BB very good odds to see a flop. I’ll raise to the minimum open (200 total) to 2x the big blind: it applies pressure and generates fold equity, lets me play post‑flop in position when called, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (30k). Plan: if Flash 3-bets big I’ll let this one go; if he calls I’ll continue with standard c-bet strategy on favorable flops and control the pot on wetter boards.

I am holding 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Although the pot odds are 3:1 to call the small raise, defending out of position with such a poor hand against a chip leader is generally a losing play. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 8d 5d, a decent suited speculative hand in heads-up play. Given my recent frequency of folding pre-flop, a raise here should represent strength and allow me to take control of the hand with positional advantage. I will make a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open from the button with 2♠3♦ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I need to put another 200 (total 300 including my posted blind) — roughly 2:1 pot odds to see a flop. 2-3 offsuit is weak but defensible heads-up against a wide raising range; calling is the low-variance play to realize equity and see a cheap flop. I’ll call 200 more (total commit 300) and plan to fold to heavy continuation-bet pressure on dry boards, but continue when I pair, pick up strong draws, or can exploit post-flop weakness. I won’t 3-bet bluff here — the hand lacks blockers and I’d rather keep the pot small out of position.

Heads-up out of position I called preflop and paired the board with 2♠3♦ on Tc 2h Jc — a weak/bottom pair with a poor kicker and no club backdoor. Pot is 600 and Villain (wide opener) will c-bet a lot, so I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to keep the pot small, realize equity, and react to Villain’s sizing: call small-to-medium continuation bets (roughly ≤½ pot) to see a turn, but fold to large bets or raises that strongly represent T/J/top pair or a club draw. If Villain checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and look to extract value or pot-control there — no bluff raise here.

I raised pre-flop and have the positional advantage. Although the flop (Tc 2h Jc) didn't connect with my 8d 5d, it is a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, and JT. By betting 300 into a 600 pot, I am executing a standard continuation bet to capitalize on Minni's check and attempt to win the pot immediately.

Flop: Tc 2h Jc — I have 2♠3♦ for bottom pair with a weak kicker and no club backdoor. Villain (wide BTN opener) c-bets half-pot (300 into 600), which is a standard, frequent sizing. Pot odds are attractive (300 to win the 900 in the pot → ~3:1), and heads-up out of position I want to keep pots small with marginal made hands. Calling a half‑pot bet lets me realize equity, see a turn, and fold to clear show of strength (big raise or large turn barrel). I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP or bluff-3‑bet with this hand — no blockers and poor playability. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn; fold to a sizeable raise or continued heavy pressure unless I improve to trips or pick up additional clear equity/value on the turn.

I have bottom pair (2♠) with a weak 3 kicker on Tc 2h Jc 4s. Villain is a wide BTN opener who c-bet the flop then checked the turn — that line looks like pot‑control with a medium/overpair or a missed hand rather than a strong value line. Out of position with a marginal made hand I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than inflate the pot where I’m often behind and vulnerable to raises or stronger pairs. Checking keeps my options open: I can induce a bluff on the river, call a small-to-medium river bet for showdown, and fold to large aggression. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament there’s no need to force chips now with bottom pair, so I’ll check and re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn 4s, which is a relative blank, Minni has checked to me again. My flop continuation bet was called, which suggests Minni has a piece of this board (likely a Ten, a Jack, or a draw). With only 8-high and no real draw, firing a second barrel into a texture that connects well with a calling range is risky. Checking back allows me to see a free river and potentially bluff if the board becomes more favorable or if Minni checks a third time, while also controlling the size of the pot with a hand that currently has no showdown value.

I have bottom pair (2♠3♦) on Tc 2h Jc 4s Qd — a very marginal showdown hand. Flash opened wide and c‑bet the flop but checked the turn, a line consistent with pot‑control or weakness. I didn’t improve on the river and have no credible blockers to represent a Q/J/A, so betting for thin value is unlikely to get called by worse and bluffing is thinly credible. Out of position with a weak pair, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check and take it to showdown; if Flash now barrels a significant bet I’ll fold since I beat very few hands.

The board is Tc 2h Jc 4s Qd. I have 8-high and no showdown value. Minni has check-called the flop and then checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically suggests a marginal made hand like a Ten or a Jack, or a missed draw like clubs or a straight draw. While the Queen on the river is a scare card that provides a bluffing opportunity to represent a Queen or a slow-played straight, Minni has shown a resistance to folding once she has committed chips to the pot. With only 20 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip deficit, I will avoid a high-risk bluff here and check back, preserving my remaining stack for more favorable spots.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve already posted the SB). Q♦6♥ is a playable heads-up opening hand — too strong to just fold and I want to take the initiative and use positional advantage. Flash (BB) has ~9,050 (~90 BB effective), so we’re deep enough to raise rather than limp. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind total): big enough to generate meaningful fold equity and price out some of BB’s worst hands, but not so large that I bloat the pot unnecessarily. Plan after the raise: if he folds we scoop the 150. If he calls I will continuation-bet on dry/Q-or-A‑high flops and exercise pot control on coordinated/overcard boards. If Flash 3-bets large (significant pressure), I’ll fold Q6o — it’s not worth playing a big pot OOP without a strong hand; against a small 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on size and reads. Overall aggressive open here maximizes our edge and puts pressure on the shorter-stacked BB over the remaining hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Four of Hearts. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak and plays poorly out of position. Given the current stack dynamics and Minni's aggressive history, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better spot to defend my blind, as I have approximately 20 hands remaining in the tournament to make a move.

In heads-up play, King-high hands are quite strong from the button. My opponent, Minni, has a significant chip lead, so I want to maintain aggression and win small pots without overcommitting on marginal holdings. Raising to 300 is a standard play here to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on favorable boards.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a 300 open from the button (SB). I have K♥6♣ — marginal but playable in heads-up. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (to match 300). Against a wide SB opening range this hand has reasonable playability (can make top pair, blocks some A/K combos) and the call is cheap: I’m risking 200 to potentially win a ~600 pot (required equity ~33%).

I could 3-bet as a bluff, but a tiny 3-bet has little fold equity and a larger 3-bet bloats the pot with a weak kicker against many hands. With a big stack advantage I’m happier keeping the pot controllable and playing postflop where I can fold to heavy action or extract value when I pair my king. Plan: call to see a flop, continue aggressively if I hit or the board favors a bluff/float line, otherwise concede to strong aggression.

Check. Heads-up vs a button opener this flop (A♣ T♣ J♦) strongly favors the preflop raiser — lots of A-x, T/J and broadway combos. With K♥6♣ I have no pair, only single club backdoor and roughly six raw outs to pair (≈24% to pair by the river) but many of those pairs will still lose to Ax/Tx. Betting as a bluff here has limited fold equity because Flash c-bets a wide range; a raise or large bet just bloats the pot with almost zero equity. I’ll control the pot and see how Flash acts: if he checks I get a free turn to re-evaluate; if he bets I’ll fold to standard-sized c-bets (only consider calling very small bets as a deliberate float if I judge later streets are favorable). Preserve the big stack and wait for a cleaner spot to invest chips.

On a board like Ace-Jack-Ten, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen for the nuts). As the pre-flop aggressor, I am representing a strong range that includes many Aces and broadway pairs. A continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard play here to maintain the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately if Minni has missed. If called, I can evaluate the turn card and still have my outs to a straight.

I’m facing a small (~1/3‑pot) c‑bet (300 into a 900 pot) on A♣ T♣ J♦ holding K♥6♣. I have no pair but I get very good pot odds to call a small bet and the button’s preflop opening/c‑bet range is wide heads‑up (lots of Ax, broadway and many bluffs). A raise as a bluff has little fold equity and bloats the pot with almost zero equity if called, and folding surrenders good odds. I also block some K‑combos and have a backdoor club possibility. Plan: call the 300 to float and re‑evaluate on the turn — continue only if I improve (K or club) or if the turn gives a good spot to bluff; fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve.

Check. I have K♥6♣ on J♦ T♣ A♣ 7♣ — no pair and only one club in hand. The turn (7♣) didn’t pair my king; it increased backdoor/river flush outs but I still only have four clubs total (3 on board + my one) so I need one club on the river (~9 outs, ≈19.6%) or one of three kings (~6.5%) to meaningfully improve. Flash opened wide and has shown a tendency to continue/barrel, so a bet here would have limited fold equity against his continuing range (many Ax/Tx/Jx and club combos) and would only bloat the pot with a marginal hand. With a big stack advantage and a single card to come, I’ll control the pot and see the river for free — check and re-evaluate on the river (call small bets with reasonable odds if I have equity, fold to large aggression unless I improve).

The turn Seven of Clubs is a significant card, as it completes potential flushes. Having called both the pre-flop raise and the flop continuation bet, Minni's range is likely to include some Aces, Jacks, or various draws. By checking the turn, Minni might be playing cautiously or trapping. My King-high has very little showdown value, and while I still have a gutshot straight draw to a Queen, the board is now very dangerous. I will check back to take a free card and control the pot size, as betting into a coordinated board against a much larger stack with no made hand is unnecessarily risky.

I missed — K♥6♣ on J♦T♣A♣7♣8♠ has no pair and the river didn’t complete my backdoor club. Flash opened preflop, bet the flop and then checked the turn, which is consistent with Ax/Tx/Jx and many hands that dominate any lone king. The pot is only 1,200 and Flash still has plenty of chips; a river bluff here has limited credibility and will be called too often. I conserve my stack and avoid bluffing into a line that looks like at least a pair. Check to showdown and fold to any meaningful bet.

On the river, the board is Jd-Tc-Ac-7c-8s. Minni has checked for the third time (flop, turn, and river). While my King-high has very little showdown value, I must consider if a bluff is profitable. Minni called a continuation bet on an extremely high-card board (A-J-T), which strongly suggests she has a piece of the board, such as a Ten, a Jack, or a weak Ace. Although the turn and river checked through, she likely has enough showdown value to call a marginal bluff. Since I am at a significant chip disadvantage and nearing the end of the tournament, I will check back and take the loss rather than risking chips on a low-percentage bluff against a range that likely has me beaten.

Raise to the minimum (200 total). Heads-up the opening range should be very wide — 4♠7♣ is weak but perfectly acceptable to open on the button to seize initiative and use position postflop. A small, 2x raise keeps the pot manageable while applying pressure to Flash (stack 8,950) and preserves my big stack (30,900). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/shove since this hand plays poorly out of position in a big pot; if he calls, play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable flops, use position to realize equity and pick up pots).

I have a strong pocket pair (88) in a heads-up situation. Minni is raising from the button, which likely represents a wide range. I will 3-bet to 700 to take control of the hand and extract value from her range.

Flash’s 3‑bet to 700 after my min‑open puts me to a choice: call 500 into a 900 pot (need ~35.7% equity) or fold. 4♠7♣ is a very weak, often dominated hand vs a heads‑up 3‑bet range of broadways, pairs and suited Aces — its realistic equity is well below the break‑even point and it plays poorly postflop even in position. I also have a huge stack advantage (≈30,750 vs 8,350), so there’s no need to gamble marginal chips now; better spots to apply pressure will present themselves. A 4‑bet bluff is unnecessary and risky against this opponent/stack size. Fold and preserve the stack for more profitable opportunities.
I have Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand for this format. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up from the big blind facing a 3x open to 300 from the button. I have 5♣2♥ — a very weak, poorly-connected hand that's often dominated. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call, so I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity to break even). Versus a wide button open my hand likely has roughly break-even to modest equity, and the monetary risk (200 into a 400 pot) is tiny relative to my 30,650 stack. I won't 3-bet this garbage — a small bluff raise is a waste vs a sticky, aggressive opponent — so I’ll call and see a flop, then play very straightforwardly: check/fold to significant continuation-bets when I miss, and play aggressively only if I pair or pick up strong draws. Total commitment after calling will be 300 (including my posted BB).

I have 5♣2♥ on A♣ T♥ 4♠ — I completely missed and have no meaningful draws (only very thin backdoors). I’m out of position vs the button who opened wide and will c-bet a large portion of range, often with Ax and broadways that dominate me. Pot is small relative to my 30k+ stack, so there’s no reason to bluff or lead here. Checking preserves chips and lets me fold to a standard continuation bet; I’ll only continue/float if Flash bets very small or gives a clear reason to turn aggression. Therefore I’ll check and fold to a realistic c-bet.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry board. Minni has checked to me, and since I am the pre-flop aggressor and have a strong made hand, I should continuation bet for value and to protect my equity against potential draws like KQ or QJ. A half-pot bet is standard here to extract value from weaker Aces or Tens.

I defended the big blind preflop with 5♣2♥ to see a cheap flop, but on A♣ T♥ 4♠ I have nothing — no pair and no real draw (only remote backdoors). Flash is the button who opened wide and now C-bet ~300 (about half-pot) which is extremely consistent with value (A‑x, T‑x, strong broadways) and will dominate my holding. Calling 300 into the current pot gives ~3:1 pot odds, but my true equity versus his c-bet range is well below the ~25% needed, and raising OOP as a pure bluff is a high-variance, low-ROI line against this opponent and stack configuration. I preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button with Kh9h — a strong, playable hand (suited king, good blockers and postflop playability) and I have position. Pot is 150 with 50 to call and min-raise to 200. Versus one opponent who shows frequent preflop aggression, I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I’m often ahead, and apply fold equity. I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 = 2x BB) to isolate and keep SPR high (if called the pot will be ~400, giving deep postflop playability). Plan: c-bet favorable flops, use position to control size, call small 3-bets and fold to very large 4-bets/shoves to preserve my deeper stack for later hands.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has opened to 200 (2BB) from the button/small blind. With a stack of nearly 95 big blinds, I should 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. AQo is significantly ahead of the range of hands Minni is likely raising from the button. A standard 3-bet size here is approximately 3.5x to 4x the initial raise, so I will raise to 700 to build the pot and apply pressure.

I opened to 200 on the button with K♥9♥ — a very playable heads‑up, suited king with good blockers and postflop playability. Flash 3‑bet to 700 is a relatively small 3‑bet and he’s shown frequent preflop aggression. With position and deep effective stacks I prefer to call small 3‑bets rather than polarize with a marginal hand. Calling keeps SPR healthy, lets me leverage position postflop (c‑bet or float), and avoids committing too much if he jams or 4‑bets big. I’ll call the extra 500 (700 total) and play the flop for value/control; fold to very large shove/4‑bet if I’m completely out of position or miss badly.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting with Ace-Queen. The flop of K-8-2 rainbow is very favorable for my perceived range, which includes hands like AK, AA, KK, and QQ. Although I haven't paired my hand yet, I have the range advantage and want to maintain the initiative. A continuation bet here puts pressure on Minni's likely holdings, such as middle pocket pairs or weaker suited connectors, and can often take the pot down immediately. I will bet approximately 43% of the pot to represent strength while keeping the risk manageable.

Flop: K♦ 8♥ 2♠. I have K♥9♥ — top pair with a medium kicker and position. Flash was the 3‑bettor preflop (aggressive player) and his small c‑bet (600 into ~1,400) looks like a high-frequency continuation that contains both value (AA, KK, AK, some sets/overpairs) and a lot of bluffs/WEAK Kx. Raising here risks playing a bloated pot out of position against the range construction of a 3‑bettor and can induce a shove from his shorter stack when I’m often behind (AA/AK/KK) — not optimal for maximizing my long‑term stack. Calling keeps worse Kx and bluffs in, uses my position to control later streets, and gives correct pot odds to continue. Plan: call 600, evaluate the turn — extract value from worse hands or fold to clear, convincing aggression from him.

On this turn, the 4 of spades is a total brick that doesn't change the board state significantly. However, Minni called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on a dry King-high flop, which strongly suggests she has at least a piece of the board, likely a King or a mid-to-high pocket pair. Since I only have Ace-high and my stack is significantly smaller than hers, firing a second barrel here is risky. If I bet and get called, I'll be left with a very awkward stack-to-pot ratio on the river with no made hand. By checking, I can pot-control, potentially see a free river to hit an Ace or Queen, and gauge her strength based on her action. If she checks back, I might still have the best hand or can re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair K♥9♥ on a fairly dry board (8♥ 2♠ K♦ 4♠) and I have position. Flash 3‑bet preflop and barreled the flop but checked the turn — showing some weakness or pot control. I called the flop to keep worse Kx and bluffs in his range; now I want to extract value from worse Kx/small pairs and charge any backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable against his shorter stack. A half‑pot bet (~1300 into 2600) accomplishes that: it gets value, applies fold equity, and still allows me to fold to a clear shove if he polarizes (AA/KK/AK). I’ll bet 1300 to take the initiative and see how he responds on the river.

After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting the flop, my check on the turn was met with a half-pot bet from Minni. Her decision to call both pre-flop and on the dry King-high flop strongly suggests she has a piece of the board, most likely a King or a middle pocket pair. My Ace-Queen is currently only Ace-high, and against her range of made hands, I have very little equity (approximately 3 to 6 outs). Given the stack-to-pot ratio and Minni's demonstrated strength, calling this bet would put me in a difficult position on the river without a clear path to winning the hand unless I improve. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my 69.5 BB stack.

I am on the button with Q4s.
