While Q4s is not a premium hand, in a heads-up situation, it is strong enough to open from the small blind/button to maintain pressure, especially since I need to pick up chips to rebuild my stack. My opponent, Minni, has been playing aggressively, but a standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take control of the pot early. If she folds, I take the blinds; if she calls, I have a suited queen with playability post-flop.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with 8♦6♠ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400; calling 200 more (total 300 committed) gives ~33% pot odds. Against a wide button opening range in heads-up play this hand has reasonable playability (straight/two-pair potential) and enough equity to defend. I don’t want to 3-bet a weak, offsuit connector OOP — it has poor fold equity and I’d be building a bloated pot out of position. Plan: call to see a flop, continue with pairs, strong draws or good turn equity, and fold to heavy pressure if I miss. Total to commit after action: 300.

Heads-up on a dry flop (Tc 5s 4d) with 8♦6♠ — I have no pair and only very weak backdoor outs (runner-runner straight or spade). Villain opened wide preflop so their range contains many tens, middle pairs and broadways plus some bluffs, but I don’t have enough equity or positional advantage to build a pot OOP. With deep stacks I’ll prioritize pot control and realization of my small equity: check and see how they react. Plan: check; call small continuation bets to realize equity, fold to significant pressure, and only take the initiative if I pick up a real pair or a strong draw on the turn.

I have bottom pair (Fours) with a Queen kicker on a relatively dry flop of T-5-4. After raising pre-flop and being checked to, I will follow up with a standard continuation bet. This bet serves to protect my hand against overcards and can extract value from Ace-high hands or weaker draws that Minni might call with. A half-pot bet of 300 is consistent with my previous play and keeps the pot size under control given her significant chip lead.

Flash c-bet 300 into a 900 pot (≈1/3 pot). Calling 300 to win 1,200 requires ~25% equity. With 8♦6♠ on T♣5♠4♦ I have ~6 clean outs to a pair (~24% to hit by river) and essentially no strong draws — raw equity is marginal but the button’s heads-up raising/c-betting range is wide and a small bet size contains a lot of air. I’m out of position so I don’t want to bluff-raise and I don’t want to fold away all of my live hands for a small price. I'll call to realize equity and see the turn; fold to significant turn aggression if I miss, and if I pair or pick up a real draw I’ll continue (raise/call for value as appropriate).

Check. I’m out of position with 8♦6♠ on a paired turn (T♣5♠4♦ / Td) and have no pair and effectively only six clean outs to a pair (~13% on the river). The turn pairing the board greatly increases the range of hands that now beat me (trip tens, pocket pairs, many turn checks/bets), and betting here would largely bloat the pot when I’m usually behind. Flash opened wide and c-bet small on the flop, so he has a lot of value and some bluffs, but with one card to come my fold equity is low. Best line is pot control: check and see the river for free, then fold to significant aggression or call small river bets occasionally if they look like bluffs. Preserve stack and avoid risking chips OOP with no real equity.

The turn card pairs the Ten, which is the top card of the flop. While I have two pair (Tens and Fours), the Ten is a card very much in Minni's calling range from the big blind. If she has a Ten, she now has trips. If she has a Five, her two pair is superior to mine. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river card for free, preserving my stack for a better spot if needed, while still having some showdown value with my pair of Fours.

Check. I have nothing on T♣-5♠-4♦-T♦-A♥ (8♦6♠): no pair, no meaningful blockers and very thin bluff equity. Flash bet the flop and I called, then checked the turn — that line is consistent with a wide range of weak tens, medium pairs, missed overcards, and some showdown/value hands. Betting river as a bluff is too risky here (I don’t block Aces and many hands will call a reasonable bluff), and I have no value to extract. Pot control and preserving my large stack versus his shorter stack is the optimal line. Plan: check to showdown; fold to a sizable raise, consider calling a very small river probe only if it looks like a pure bluff.

The river card is an Ace, which is a significant overcard. While I have two pair (Tens and Fours), this is a very vulnerable hand. Any Ace in Minni's range (which she could easily have called with on the flop) now makes a superior two pair (Aces and Tens). Furthermore, any Ten makes trips, and any Five makes a better two pair (Tens and Fives). Since Minni has checked, I will check back to see a showdown. I have some showdown value against missed draws or hands like King-high, but betting would likely only result in being called by hands that beat me, and checking allows me to minimize loss while potentially winning a small pot.

I have 9♠2♥ — a garbage hand — but in heads-up I should be opening very wide from the button/SB. The pot is small (150) and the minimum raise to 200 is a low-risk way to apply pressure: if Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I get to play postflop in position. I have a big stack advantage (31,100 vs 8,750), so small preflop aggression is +EV against a shorter-stacked opponent. If Flash 3-bets large or shoves I'll fold this hand because it has very poor equity. Action: make a min-raise to 200 total to maximize fold equity while keeping downside small.

I am in the Big Blind holding 98o. Minni has min-raised from the Small Blind to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. In a heads-up match, 98o is a solid enough hand to defend against a wide opening range from the button, especially at these pot odds. I will call and see a flop.

I have an open-ended straight draw (9-8 on a 7-6-3 board) which is a strong drawing hand. I am out of position against Minni, who has a significant chip lead. On this low, connected flop, I want to see how she reacts. Checking is the standard play here as the Big Blind to see if she continues her pre-flop aggression. If she bets, I have the equity to call and potentially check-raise if I feel she is continuation-betting too wide, but for now, checking to the aggressor is the most balanced move.

Heads-up I'm the preflop aggressor and in position with 9♠2♥ — essentially air on this flop (3h 6d 7s). Flash checked, pot is 400. With a big stack advantage I should apply low-risk pressure to pick up the pot rather than check and let him realize equity for free. A small continuation bet (about half-pot) maximizes fold equity vs his wide calling range while keeping downside limited if he check-raises. Plan: bet 200 to take it down now; if he check-raises I’ll fold (no real equity), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn but will usually give up without improvement.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) on a low, connected flop of 7-6-3. This board texture generally favors my range as the big blind defender. Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 3:1 doesn't strictly cover the 17% chance of hitting the straight on the turn, the implied odds are significant if I hit, and I also have two overcards to the board (9 and 8) which could potentially be good if I hit a pair, though they are weak. Calling is the most disciplined approach to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position against a massive chip leader.

On the turn, the board has become even more connected with the four of spades. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: four 5s and four 10s). As the player out of position, checking to the pre-flop and flop aggressor remains the most prudent strategy to control the pot size while I continue to realize my equity. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on the price offered, but leading out here might force a fold from hands I can beat if I hit, or invite a raise from her stronger range.

I’m heads-up in position with a huge stack advantage (30,750 vs 8,450). I opened wide preflop with 9♠2♥ and c-bet the flop (200) which was called. Flash checks the turn (4♠), showing weakness or a marginal holding. My hand has almost no showdown value, so the correct line is to apply low-cost pressure to try to take the pot now. A half-pot second barrel (~400 into the 800 pot) maximizes fold equity vs his wide calling range while keeping the risk small relative to stacks. If he check-raises very large I’ll release; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river but will usually give up without improvement.

I hold an open-ended straight draw with 98o on a 3-6-7-4 board. Minni has bet 400 into an 800 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. My current equity to hit a straight on the river is approximately 17.4% (8 outs). While the immediate pot odds are slightly below what I need to break even, the implied odds in a heads-up match against an aggressive opponent are high enough to justify the call. If I hit a 5 or a 10, I will likely be able to extract significant value on the river. Therefore, I will call the turn bet to realize my equity.

On the river, the 9h provides me with top pair, although my 8 kicker is only mediocre. The board is quite coordinated; any 5 in my opponent's hand makes a straight (3-4-5-6-7). Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand. By checking, I allow her to continue bluffing with missed draws or to check behind with weaker hands that wouldn't call a bet anyway. If she bets, I will have a tough decision, but leading into the chip leader on this board texture is suboptimal compared to checking to realize my showdown value or induce a bluff.

I just paired the board on the river (9♠2♥; board 3♥-6♦-7♠-4♠-9♥). Flash has been calling my small flop and turn bets, which narrows his continuing range to mostly single-pair hands (3x/6x/7x/4x), some medium pocket pairs and the occasional completed straight (5x) or trips combo. He checked the river, which looks like pot-control or a marginal pair rather than a value shove. Betting for value is correct here — half-pot extracts calls from worse one-pair hands while limiting my downside versus straights/trips. I’ll bet a reasonable amount (800 into the 1600 pot). If he raises big I’ll treat it as a value raise and fold; if he calls I’ll see showdown.

On the river, I hit top pair with my 9 of clubs. Although the board is very connected and any 5 makes a straight, the action is heads-up and I am receiving 3:1 pot odds (calling 800 to win a total pot of 3200). Minni has been aggressive throughout the hand, and her range includes missed draws, lower pairs, or pure bluffs in addition to the hands that beat me (like a 5, two pair, or a better 9). In a tournament setting, folding top pair on the river with these odds would be too tight and easily exploitable, so I will call to see the showdown.

I am holding a pocket pair of sixes (6d, 6s) on the button in a heads-up situation. Pocket pairs are very strong in heads-up play, typically being a favorite against any two random cards. My stack is currently around 104 big blinds, while Minni is the chip leader with nearly 300 big blinds. I want to build the pot and take the initiative in this hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the most effective play here to extract value and potentially end the hand pre-flop if Minni folds, or set up a strong continuation on the flop.

Heads-up vs the button’s 300 open. My hand (8♠3♦) is weak and out of position, so I don’t want to 3-bet/bluff and I don’t want to invest more than necessary. However the raise is small and the pot odds are attractive: calling an extra 200 into a 400 pot (300 total committed) requires ~33% equity. Against a wide button opening range this hand has marginal equity worth seeing a flop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and only continue if I hit a pair, get a strong draw, or see a clear profitable line. Preserve my big-stack advantage and avoid unnecessary variance with a marginal hand.

I completely missed this flop (A♠ 7♣ 4♥) with 8♠3♦ — no pair and no realistic draws (only 6 outs to pair by the river, ≈24%, and many of those pairs will still be behind an Ace/top pair). The preflop raiser’s range is weighted toward Aces and top pairs, so this board favors them. I’ll check to see their action and avoid bluff‑raising out of position; fold to a routine c‑bet and wait for a better spot to apply pressure given my big‑stack advantage.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a very dry Ace-high board. While my pocket sixes have some showdown value, this board structure heavily favors my perceived range. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate here to maintain initiative and represent the Ace. This should force folds from many of Minni's hands that missed the flop, such as random high cards or smaller pocket pairs. If called, I can look to check down later streets and reach a cheap showdown.

Out of position on A♠‑7♣‑4♥ with 8♠3♦ — I completely missed. There are only 6 clean outs (≈12.8% on the turn, ≈24% by the river) and many of those pairs will still lose to an Ace/top‑pair. The pot is 900 and the bet is 300, so I need ≈25% equity to break even on a call; my realistic equity versus a heads‑up c‑bet range (Ace‑heavy) is at or below that threshold. I also have no real draws (a flush is impossible with these cards) and I’m the chip leader (29,250 vs 9,850), so I should avoid marginal, high‑variance spots out of position. Fold and preserve the stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with J♠9♠ — a strong, playable hand heads-up. We have position postflop and a much bigger stack (29.2k vs Flash’s ~10.65k), so I want to take the initiative and apply pressure while keeping the size reasonable. Raising is standard here to deny cheap flops and extract fold equity from the BB’s wide defending range. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind): if he folds I pick up the 150 pot, if he calls the pot will be 600 and I keep positional advantage on the flop. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies, but opening here is the most +EV line.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c5h. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. This is a weak-to-medium hand, and although I'm getting decent odds to see a flop (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), my hand is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position against a player who has shown aggression throughout the match. With only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to look for better spots to commit chips, especially as Minni currently has a significant chip lead. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Ace-Ten suited (AsTs), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My current stack is 10,600 compared to Minni's 29,250. With only 10 hands left in the tournament, I need to look for opportunities to build my stack back up. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to build the pot and take control of the hand pre-flop.

I'm in the big blind with 3♥2♦ facing a standard heads-up open to 300 from the button (SB). This is the absolute bottom of my range, and out of position postflop I'll be dominated or forced into difficult multi-street decisions. The pot is 400 and calling costs another 200 (300 total), but the hand has very little equity and poor playability OOP. A 3-bet to 500 as a bluff is unnecessary given my big stack lead and the remaining hands; preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is higher EV. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 4♦3♠. In heads-up play I should open a very wide range to take the initiative and use positional advantage postflop — 4-3o is marginal but plenty playable in position. I’ll raise rather than limp: a 3x BB size (300 total) is standard here — big enough to deny correct pot odds to many of Flash’s calling hands, builds a pot I can steer in position, and preserves fold equity if he over-defends. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, or continue with a c-bet on favorable flops and use position to exploit his wide defending range.

In a heads-up situation, King-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend from the Big Blind against a button raise. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to call into a 400 pot) and I have a hand that often has equity against Minni's wide opening range. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's potential without overcommitting my stack pre-flop.

I have flopped middle pair (8s) with a King kicker on a very coordinated board (9-8-6). While middle pair is a decent holding in a heads-up pot, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on such a wet board is risky. Checking to Minni is the standard play here, allowing me to see how they react and likely call a continuation bet to evaluate the turn. This keeps the pot manageable while I have a hand with some showdown value and potential to improve.

I opened wide preflop and have position; the flop (9♦ 6♥ 8♣) misses my 4♦3♠ but is a good texture to continuation-bet heads‑up. I only have a one-card gutshot to a 5 (4 outs: ~8.5% on the turn, ~16.5% by the river) and little showdown value, so I’m betting to fold out unpaired overcards and weak holdings from his defending range while keeping the size manageable. A half‑pot c‑bet (300 into the 600 pot) gives good fold equity and keeps pots controllable — I’ll fold to a large check‑raise, and if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn, continuing only with improvement or clear equity/plan to bluff further.

I have flopped middle pair with a King kicker on a very coordinated board (9-8-6). Minni has made a standard continuation bet of half the pot. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that middle pair is relatively strong in heads-up play, a call is the most appropriate action. Raising would be risky given the straight potential and sets already possible on this texture (like 7-10 or 99/88/66), while folding would be far too tight against a wide button opening range. I will call to see the turn and evaluate how the board develops.

I am currently holding middle pair (eights) with a King kicker on a very coordinated board (9-8-6-4). On the turn, the four of spades is relatively blank, though it does complete some unlikely straights (like 7-5). As the out-of-position player who called a flop bet, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Minni proceeds. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on this texture doesn't accomplish much, as better hands won't fold and I want to keep the pot small with a marginal made hand.

Turn pairs my 4 (I hold 4♦3♠), so I’ve improved to a very weak pair. Flash checked after calling my flop c‑bet; his range includes many better pairs (9x/8x/6x, pocket pairs) plus some floats/overcards. There are no meaningful draws to protect against, and betting has thin value while risking building a pot I’m likely behind in. I’ll control the pot and use position — check and evaluate the river. If Flash fires a small bet on the turn I can consider a call, but I’ll fold to a large shove since my pair is second/weak.

The river 5h completes a potential straight if Minni has a seven (4-5-6-7-8 or 5-6-7-8-9). My pair of eights is a marginal showdown hand. Since the turn went check-check, it's unlikely Minni has a monster, but leading into this board texture is risky as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands. Checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size and try to reach showdown with my middle pair.

I improved to a very weak pair (4s) on the turn and Flash has been passive all streets (called my flop c-bet, then checked turn and river). That line is consistent with either a better made hand (9x/8x/6x or a pocket pair) or a missed float/overcards — neither case produces a reliable thin-value call for me, and a bluff here has poor chances of folding out enough hands to be +EV. The pot is small (1,200) relative to stacks, and betting risks getting raised by hands that already beat me where I must fold. I want to avoid bloating a marginal spot: checking to showdown preserves chips and lets me see what Flash shows. Therefore I will check and take the showdown rather than bet or bluff.

I am on the button with 95 offsuit. This is a relatively weak hand, but in heads-up play, almost any two cards are playable from the small blind for a minimum raise or a call. However, looking at the tournament status, I am down significantly in chips (11,300 vs 28,550) with only 9 hands remaining. Minni has been quite aggressive with pre-flop raises. 95o is near the bottom of my range. While I could call the 50 to see a flop, folding is a safe option to wait for a slightly better hand to try and double up. Given the stacks and the stage of the tournament, I will choose to fold this hand and look for a more advantageous spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button with T8♥ is a playable, positionally strong hand — good postflop playability and backdoor flush/straight potential. I want to seize the initiative, apply pressure to the big blind, and build a pot I can play for value or bluff in position. With deep stacks (28.6k vs 11.2k) and the table's standard open sizing, a 3x BB open to 300 is appropriate: it gives fold equity, avoids cheap multiway flops, and keeps postflop options wide. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold or reassess; if he calls I’ll use position to play for value or apply pressure. Total I’ll put in the pot is 300.

I have 9-8 of diamonds in the big blind, which is a strong suited connector. Minni has raised to 3 bets (300) from the button. Given my stack size of 11,200 compared to the pot and the tournament progress (hand 143/150), calling to see a flop is the most standard play here. Suited connectors play well in heads-up pots, and I have direct position on the flop as the big blind defender. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary given my hand's strength as a post-flop realization hand.

I am first to act on the flop as the Big Blind. The board of 3h-7h-2s is quite low and disjointed. With 9d-8d, I have two overcards and some backdoor possibilities, but no made hand or direct draw. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to evaluate Minni's continuation bet.

Flop 3h-7h-2s with Th8h: I have the nut flush draw (4 hearts) plus two overcards — a very strong semi-bluff heads-up in position. Flash’s wide preflop call means his range contains many missed hands and medium pairs. Betting now both builds the pot when I hit and uses fold equity to win the pot immediately. I choose a ~2/3-pot sizing (400 into 600) — big enough to fold out many single-pair hands and deny free cards, but not so large that I overcommit with a draw. If called I’ll proceed to evaluate the turn; if I face a large check-raise I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and pot odds (with 9 outs to the nut flush (~35% to complete by river) I can call moderate raises but will fold to extreme aggression).

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 8d. After calling a pre-flop raise of 300 from Minni, the flop comes 3h 7h 2s. I check to the aggressor, and Minni bets 400 into a 600 pot. While 9-8 suited is a decent starting hand, this board does not connect with my cards at all, providing no straight or flush draws. I only have two overcards to the board's highest card (7). Minni's bet of 2/3 pot is quite strong, and given my chip deficit and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 143/150), I choose to preserve my 11,000-chip stack rather than floating with 9-high. I will look for a better spot to commit chips in the remaining seven hands.

In heads-up play, Ace-high hands like Ace-Four offsuit are well above the average starting hand strength. As the dealer, I have position for the rest of the hand. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size to put pressure on the big blind and build a pot with a hand that is likely ahead of Minni's random defending range.

Facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200 → pot odds ≈ 33%). I hold 7♦5♠ — a marginal but defensible heads-up hand with decent post‑flop playability (straight/2‑pair potential) and reasonable equity vs a wide BTN open (est. ~40%+ vs a typical heads‑up opening range). 3‑betting here is unattractive: this hand has little fold‑equity or value to inflate a pot OOP, and a small squeeze to 500 won’t meaningfully improve the situation. Folding is too tight given the pot odds and implied odds against a shorter-stacked opponent. I will call (total 300) to see a flop and proceed: continue aggressively on low/connected boards or strong draws, concede to heavy action on dry high‑card boards, and extract value with made hands (two pair+/nut draws).

Board is A♣-6♥-2♦ (rainbow). I hold 7♦5♠ — no pair and only remote backdoor outs. This is a dry, Ace‑high flop where my hand has very little equity and little fold equity if I lead. Flash opened wide from the button, so he has many Ax/overcards and bluffs, but he also c‑bets this texture frequently. I’ll check to control the pot and see whether he blinks. If he fires a meaningful c‑bet or raises I will fold (concede to heavy action); I’d only entertain calling a tiny probe bet. With a big stack advantage there’s no reason to bloat the pot OOP with junk.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a very dry board. Since Minni checked to me, I should bet for value and protection. While my kicker is weak, my hand is likely the best at this stage. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard continuation bet to extract value from smaller pairs or hands that might be drawing thin.

Flop: A♣-6♥-2♦, I hold 7♦5♠ and am out of position. Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 would give me 300/(900+300)=25% pot odds. My hand has essentially no showdown value and only remote runner-runner straight possibilities (equity well under the required ~25%, roughly single-digit %). Versus a button opener’s c-bet range this size, many Ax and strong hands are likely, and I have no realistic fold equity OOP. With negative expected value to chase and plenty of better spots to pick from given my larger stack, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 4♠8♠ — a marginal but playable suited hand that plays very well in position. The pot is tiny relative to our deep stacks, so opening wide to seize initiative is profitable; calling 50 is passive and lets the BB see a cheap flop. I’ll raise to the minimum standard heads-up open (200 total) to steal the blind and play the postflop play in position. This sizing keeps the pot controllable, gets value/calls from worse hands, and lets me c-bet favorable flops or fold to significant 3-bets depending on sizing and reads.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs7s, a decent suited semi-connector in a heads-up situation. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has made a small raise to 2bb (200 total). I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. This hand plays well post-flop and is definitely strong enough to defend against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop.

On a flop of 3s-3h-Qc, I have top pair with a 7 kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, to see if she continues with a continuation bet. Checking allows her to continue bluffing with her air or betting with weaker hands that I currently beat. If she bets, I plan to call or possibly raise depending on the sizing, but for now, checking is the standard and most deceptive play.

I opened preflop and am in position heads-up, so I should c-bet a high frequency on this texture to exploit missed hands. The flop is paired and relatively dry (3♠ 3♥ Q♣). My 8♠4♠ has no pair — only a backdoor spade draw — so I don’t want to build a big pot with marginal equity. Flash checked from the big blind (likely a wide calling range), so a small continuation bet should fold out pure air (A/K, other misses), get value from some worse pairs and Qx, and keep the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet small (~150 into 400, ~35–40% pot). Plan: fold to a large raise, and if called re-evaluate on the turn — continue only with meaningful improvement (a spade or a strong card) or check to control the pot.

On a dry paired board of 3s-3h-Qc, my top pair (Queens with a 7 kicker) is a very strong hand heads-up. Minni's continuation bet of 150 into 400 is small and consistent with her aggressive playstyle. There is little reason to raise here as I want to keep her range wide and allow her to continue bluffing with air or value betting worse hands like middle pairs or weaker Queens. Raising might force her to fold everything except hands that beat me (like AQ or a 3). Calling is the most profitable line to extract value on later streets.

On the turn, the board has paired the 3s and now shows a 9c alongside the Queen. My top pair of Queens with a 7 kicker remains a very strong hand in a heads-up match. Since I was the caller on the flop, leading out here (donk betting) would likely scare off Minni's bluffs and weaker hands. I will check to her once more to maintain a deceptive line, allowing her to continue betting with her air, draws, or middle pairs. This keeps the pot manageable while giving her the opportunity to make mistakes by over-bluffing or value-betting worse hands.

Turn: 3♠ 3♥ Q♣ 9♣. I hold 8♠4♠ and after the 9♣ my tiny backdoor spade possibility is gone — I have no pair and essentially no draw. Flash called my small flop c-bet and now checks the turn, which suggests Qx, a pocket pair, or a float/air-check. Betting the turn would be building the pot with almost zero equity and opens me up to a raise I don’t want to call. With position and a thin hand, pot control is the highest EV line: check back, realize showdown equity, and look to bluff or value-bet only if I improve on the river (or face a very weak/abnormally small bet). Action: check.

On the river, the King is an overcard to my Queen. After Minni checked back the turn, it's possible she has a medium pair, a weak Queen, or was floating the flop with air. By checking the river, I allow her to bluff with missed draws or value bet worse hands, while keeping the pot small in case the King helped her or she has me out-kicked. My hand has significant showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value after the King arrives.

I have 8♠4♠ on a 3♠-3♥-Q♣-9♣-K♦ board. I never improved and my backdoor spade equity is gone. BB called my small flop c-bet, then checked turn and checked river — that line is consistent with Qx, a small/mid pair, a slow-played 33, or a weak float. With no pair, no relevant blocker to K/Q, and limited fold equity vs a caller that already showed willingness to call small bets, a bluff here is low EV and a value bet would mostly get called by hands that beat me. Pot control and preserving stack is the +EV line: check and take showdown. If faced with a river bet I can re-evaluate for a fold or a thin call, but I should not fire a bluff into a caller-prone range.

With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, I am currently trailing Minni's stack significantly. While 3s 2s is suited, it is arguably the weakest starting hand in poker. Given the proximity to the end of the session and my goal to maximize my remaining stack, I will fold this hand to avoid losing chips in a potentially dominated situation, waiting for a better opportunity in the final four hands.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a wide range. T4o is marginal but fine to raise heads-up to seize the initiative rather than limp. The pot is 150 and I only need to put in 200 total to make a standard 3x-style open; that pressures Flash's BB while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: raise to 200 total; fold to a large 3-bet or serious aggression preflop, and if called use position to c-bet favorable flops or check-protect on bad boards.

I am holding pocket Tens (Td Tc), which is an extremely strong starting hand in heads-up play. Minni, on the button, has opened with a minimum raise to 200. Given the strength of my hand and the need to build a pot while I have a significant equity advantage, I will put in a 3-bet. A raise to 700 (3.5x the open) is a standard out-of-position sizing that extracts value from Minni's wide button opening range and allows me to take control of the hand pre-flop.

I opened to 200 on the button to take the initiative, but Flash 3-bet to 700 (an extra 500) which is substantial versus my open. I have T4o offsuit — a weak, often-dominated hand versus a 3-bet range. The pot is 900 and calling 500 more would require ~36% equity to break even (500 / (900+500) ≈ 35.7%), which T4o rarely has against a reasonable 3-bet range. Re-raising to 1200 would bloat the pot and commit a lot of chips with very poor equity. I planned to fold to a large 3-bet preflop, so I preserve my deep stack for better spots and fold here.

I hold Ace-Deuce offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. Any Ace is a strong hand in this context. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds, or see a flop with the lead.

Heads-up, deep stacks, facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button. 4h3d is a weak, but defensible heads-up big blind defender: it's connected (backdoor straight potential) and can realize equity postflop. Calling 200 (making my total commitment 300) gives ~33% break-even equity (200 into a 600 pot after my call) and vs a wide opening range I expect at least that. I won't 3-bet light out of position with this holding — raising would bloat the pot with a hand that plays poorly OOP. Plan: call, see a flop; continue on favorable low/connected boards or when I pair/upside, fold to heavy aggression when unimproved to protect my deep stack.

I flopped bottom pair (3s) with a 4 kicker on 5♠ J♣ 3♥. Versus a wide button open in heads-up this is a hand I want to continue with, but I’m out of position and stacks are deep (effective ~11.5k). Pot control is my priority: checking keeps the pot small, induces spotty c-bets from a wide range, and lets me call reasonable bets to realize equity. Plan: check now, call small-to-medium continuation bets and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to a large, polarizing check-raise that represents J/5/sets or a committed range.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding Ace-high on a relatively dry flop of J-5-3. Since Minni checked to me, a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate to maintain pressure, represent a Jack, and fold out her weaker hands that missed this board.

Heads-up on a 5♠ J♣ 3♥ flop I have bottom pair (3♦4♥) with a weak kicker. Button opened wide preflop and his 300 flop bet (~1/2 pot) represents a mix of Jx/overcards, draws and bluffs. Calling 300 to potentially win ~1,200 after my call gives ~25% break-even equity — vs his wide c-bet range I have sufficient equity to continue, and I want to keep the pot controlled out of position. Raising OOP with this marginal hand would bloat the pot and commit me with a poor kicker, so I’ll call, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy, polarized aggression if unimproved; extract/raise if I improve to trips or a cleaner two-pair).

Check. I currently have a weak pair of threes (3♦4♥) with a very poor kicker on a turned board J♣ J♦ 5♠ 3♥. The turn pairing the jack massively helps the button’s continuing range (many Jx hands now beat me), and I’m out of position against an opponent who opens and barrels fairly wide. With effective stacks (~11.2k) I want to keep the pot controlled — betting OOP would bloat the pot and commit me with a marginal hand. I’ll check to induce bluffs and to call small-to-medium bets to realize equity; I’ll fold to large, polarized aggression and re-evaluate on the river if we see a cheap showdown or a favorable runout.

The turn pairs the Jack on a board of J-5-3-J. I have Ace-high. Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, which indicates she likely has a piece of the board or a draw. By checking the turn, I control the pot size and realize the showdown value of my Ace-high. It also protects me from a check-raise if she actually holds a Jack. If she checks the river, I can decide whether to value bet or check it down depending on the final card.

I have 4♥3♦ on J♣ J♦ 5♠ 3♥ 4♣ — that’s two pair (4s and 3s). Flash opened, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn and the river. That line is consistent with either a weak c‑bet that cooled off or a pot‑control Jx; it’s less consistent with an aggressive trap (quads/full house/sets) which would often extract value. My two‑pair is vulnerable to any Jack (trips/full house) and to rare full houses/quads, so betting risks getting called/raised by hands that already beat me while only folding out worse hands that likely already folded earlier. The pot is small (1,200) relative to effective stacks, so the correct play is to keep the pot controlled and realize equity. I check to see a cheap showdown; if Flash fires a small bet I can call, but I’ll fold to a large, polarized raise.

On the river, the board is 5s-Jc-3h-Jd-4c. I hold Ace-Deuce (Ad 2c), which gives me a straight (Ace-high wheel: A-2-3-4-5). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet and checking the turn. This is a clear value betting situation as my hand is extremely strong. I will bet 800 into the 1,200 pot to get value from her likely middle pairs or any Ace-high hands she might be stubborn with.

I hold 4♥3♦ on Jc Jd 5s 3h 4c — two pair (4s & 3s). Flash opened, c‑bet the flop then checked the turn; that line is consistent with either Jx / thin value or missed c‑bet bluffs. He now bets 800 into a 1,200 pot; calling 800 to win 2,000 requires ~40% equity. My two‑pair is vulnerable to trips/full houses (any J, Jx or J+3/J+4 combos) but crushes his missed draws and many bluffs. Given his wide preflop range and the medium river size, calling to see showdown is the +EV play rather than folding my two pair or bloating the pot with a raise. I will call 800; if he turns this into a large, polarized raise I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold).

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Ad2s — an ace is very strong heads-up and this hand plays well in position. Limping/calling is passive; raising to take the initiative and either pick up the pot or play postflop in position is standard. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call, but I’ll raise to the min-open of 200 (2.0x BB) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable given both stacks (my stack 26,750 vs his 13,100). If he folds I win the blinds; if he calls I can c-bet favourable Ace-high textures or control the pot on wet boards. Versus a large 3-bet I’ll fold A2o unless the sizing or history suggests a light 3-bet, in which case I may call or mix.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 4c. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. While I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (100 to call for a 400 pot), 43 offsuit is one of the weakest possible hands and plays very poorly out of position. Given that we are on Hand 149 of 150, I want to preserve my stack for the final hand rather than defending with a hand that will likely have to check-fold on most flops. Minni has been consistently aggressive, and I'd prefer to see the final hand with my current stack intact rather than losing more chips post-flop.

On the final hand of the tournament, I am holding Jack-Eight offsuit on the button. This is well within the range of hands to open for a raise in heads-up play. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and try to maximize my stack on this last opportunity.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB while I'm in the BB with 6♦5♥. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (300 total committed including my 100 BB). Heads-up this is a defendable, playable hand: 6-5 offsuit has straight/two-pair potential and decent equity versus a wide button opening range. Pot odds: I must call 200 to win ~600 after my call → ~33% immediate pot odds, which is in the ballpark of this hand's equity vs a wide opener. I won't 3-bet here because raising from the BB doesn't fix my postflop positional disadvantage (I'll still be OOP), and a small raise to 500 would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding; a larger 3-bet as a bluff is unnecessary against an opponent who opens frequently and will often call. Plan: call to see a flop, keep the pot controlled, and play postflop—fold to clear, heavy aggression on dry boards; continue (bet/call or raise) on paired boards, strong draws or when I flop equity.

I have 6♦5♥ on a 6♠9♦6♥ flop — that’s trips (three sixes). Against the button’s wide open-raising range this is a very strong hand: only a few holdings (pocket 9s, or the lone remaining 6 with a higher kicker or a 6+9 for an immediate full house) realistically dominate. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep, so my objective is to extract value from worse pairs (9x, smaller pocket pairs), single 6’s with weaker kickers, and other hands that will call, while not giving free cards. Betting first for value and to deny a free turn makes the most sense; a modestly large value bet (~2/3 pot) should keep worse hands in. I choose to bet 400. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting; if he check-raises large I’ll assume a very narrow, strong range and re-evaluate (likely call a reasonable raise and commit more for showdown value, fold only to an extremely polarizing shove that I’m beat by).

On this final hand of the tournament, I raised pre-flop with J8o and was called by Minni. The flop comes 6s-9d-6h, a paired board. Minni has led out for 400 into the 600 pot. This 'donk bet' often signifies either a piece of the board (like a 9 or a 6) or an attempt to take the pot away on a texture that likely missed my opening range. While I have two overcards to the 6 and one to the 9, J-high is extremely weak to continue with against significant aggression on a board where I have very little equity or drawing potential. Since the goal is to maximize my final stack and this is the last hand, making a marginal call or a speculative bluff-raise is unlikely to be profitable. I will fold and preserve my remaining 12,500 chips.
